message.content: acquaint

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

file.content:
# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**docs: cycle protocol updates (0.59.0)**

Three CLAUDE.md cycle protocol refinements iterated during
0.58.0 land in one focused cycle (first squash candidate):
merge as default for multi-commit cycles, close-out captures
follow-up Todos, meta-note that close-out often surfaces
follow-ups.

- 0.59.0-0 Preparation (done)
  - backfill `0.58.0` `Commits:` ref in chores-12
  - bump Cargo.toml to `0.59.0-0`
  - open chores-12 next section (anticipated close-out title)
  - delete stray `close-out.txt` (swept into 0.58.0-0)
  - capture 0.58.0 follow-up Todo (`vc-x1 push` N:1 support)
    in `notes/todo.md > ## Todo` per Option C (retroactive)
  - refresh `## Priorities`:
    - drop satisfied P1 (chores-12 active)
    - promote `vc-x1 push` N:1 to new P1
  - populate `## In Progress` (this block)
- 0.59.0-0.1 capture design notes for `-1` as `###` subsections
  in chores-12 (end-of-day handoff before `/exit`) (done)
- 0.59.0-1 CLAUDE.md updates: merge default + close-out
  follow-up capture + meta-note
- 0.59.0 close-out (first squash candidate)

## Priorities

- P1 is highest priority, same priority are grouped equally
- A line with `...` is a prefix-match against a `## Todo` title
  - resolves locally (in this file); should be unique
- Entries without a prefix-match are free-form
  - chores tasks, ad-hoc reminders.

### P1

- `**vc-x1 push: support...**`

### P2

- `**single-field...**`

message.content:
So 0.59.0 we are going to refine the Cycle Protocol. In particular I think we
should promote the Cycle Protocol into it's own file /notes/cycle-protocol.md
and replace /notes/substep-protocol.md which is aged as noted in CLAUDE.md.

So a Cycle Protocol is:

  ## Preparation
  ## Work-N
  ## Close-out

And I want one a single source of truth to minimize redundancy.

Also, I'd like to add a new todo section, `## Ideas`, where we put
items that come up, but they aren't solid enough to create a todo
or we're in the closing-out or end-of-day phase and this is
the place these last minute-changes go. Items in Ideas should not
languish, but should be properly cataloged on the next preparation
stage.

attachment.snippet:
1	# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
8	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
9	
10	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
11	
12	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
13	
14	- App repo: `.` (project root)
15	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
16	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
17	
18	## Working Directory
19	
20	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
21	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
22	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
23	command failures downstream.
24	
25	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
26	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
27	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
28	clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
29	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
30	tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
31	not a stylistic choice.
32	
33	## File reads — read the slice you need
34	
35	Long notes files are appended to over time. Read only the
36	slice your task needs; grep or read further on demand.
37	
38	- **`notes/todo.md`** (the routine acquaint read) — first
39	  ~60 lines covers intro + `## In Progress` + `## Priorities`.
40	  `Read` with `offset=0, limit=60`. Read further only when
41	  picking up a `## Todo` entry, chasing a `[N]` ref, or
42	  auditing the whole list.
43	- **`notes/todo-backlog.md`** — the long-tail backlog
44	  (non-prioritized `## Todo` entries). Read only when
45	  picking up a backlog item; grep to locate it first.
46	- **`notes/bugs.md`** — the bug list. Small; read whole
47	  when triaging a bug or chasing the `## Bugs` pointer in
48	  todo.md.
49	- **`notes/done.md`** + **`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`** —
50	  historical / append-mostly. Scan headings first
51	  (`grep '^## ' notes/chores/chores-NN.md`), then read only
52	  the section you need.
53	
54	**Why:** before the 0.58.0 split, `notes/todo.md` ran ~370
55	lines and grew every cycle. The split moved the backlog and
56	bugs into sibling files so the routine read stays small; the
57	same "slice you need" rule applies to historical files.
58	
59	## Memory
60	
61	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
62	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
63	CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
64	and it loses on discoverability:
65	
66	- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
67	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
68	  (human or bot).
69	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
70	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
71	
72	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
73	durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
74	
75	## Speculation marker
76	
77	Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
78	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
79	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
80	enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
81	tell the measured from the inferred.
82	
83	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
84	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
85	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
86	in the written record.
87	
88	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
89	marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
90	"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
91	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
92	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
93	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
94	
95	## jj Basics
96	
97	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
98	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
99	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
100	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
101	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
102	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
103	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
104	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
105	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
106	  session because session data updates continuously.
107	- **Post-amend `jj new`** — `jj edit <rev>`,
108	  `jj rebase -r <rev> -d ...`, and `jj squash --into <rev>`
109	  all leave `@` on the rewritten commit rather than on an
110	  empty change above. The most common case is the close-out
111	  push for a [Merge (non-FF)](#pushing) shape (`jj rebase
112	  -r <tip> -d <prev> -d <sub-tip>` leaves `@` on the new
113	  merge). In a colocated jj+git repo, git then detaches
114	  HEAD at the commit's first parent, so `git status`,
115	  gitk, and IDE diff views read the commit's content as
116	  uncommitted relative to HEAD. Follow up with `jj new` to
117	  create an empty `@` above; HEAD shifts to the commit and
118	  the working tree reads clean.
119	
120	## Prose form
121	
122	Long-lived prose on this project follows one basic shape: a short intro
123	that explains the *why* or the high-level *what*, then a `-` bullet
124	list for the details. Wrap lines at ≤72 cols (bullet continuations
125	indent two spaces). Avoid wall-of-prose paragraphs — they hide the
126	structure that bullets make scannable.
127	
128	Surfaces that use this shape:
129	
130	- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files —
131	  see [Doc comments](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method).
132	- Commit message bodies (both app-repo and session-repo). The
133	  ≤50-col title is the commit-specific add-on; see
134	  [Per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
135	- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` — see
136	  [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
137	- Todo and Done entries in `notes/todo.md` when an entry needs more
138	  than one line of detail. Pure one-liners are still fine.
139	
140	Bullet *content* differs by surface:
141	
142	- **Commit bodies** — bullets are file-by-file: one bullet per file
143	  changed, file plus a one-line gist (e.g.
144	  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Source of truth for the
145	  mechanical edit list.
146	- **Chores / todo / done** — bullets are conceptual (design points,
147	  structural notes, the "what landed and why" at a notch above
148	  file-list granularity). Never a copy of the commit's edit list —
149	  see [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
150	- **Doc comments** — bullets are whatever structure fits (fields,
151	  cases, invariants).
152	
153	**Problem + plan shape.** `## In Progress` cycle blocks,
154	chores section intros, and `## Todo` entries use a sharper
155	form of the same shape:
156	
157	- **Problem statement** (the why) — one or two sentences;
158	  don't pad with intent, don't restate the plan.
159	- **Plan bullets** (the what/when) — formality differs by
160	  surface:
161	  - In Progress / chores: numbered ladder (`-N` suffixes,
162	    `(current)` / `(done)` markers) — a committed sequence.
163	  - Todo entries: rough informal bullets, no numbering;
164	    formalized only when the entry is picked up into a
165	    cycle.
166	
167	**Semicolons inside bullets.** A bullet that joins
168	multiple clauses with semicolons (`A; B; C`) is a list
169	hiding inside running prose — break the clauses into
170	sub-bullets so the structure shows. Semicolons in running
171	prose (intro paragraphs, sentence-joins) are fine. Not

lastPrompt: So 0.59.0 we are going to refine the Cycle Protocol. In particular I think we should promote the Cycle Protocol into it's own file /notes/cycle-protocol.md and replace /notes/substep-protocol.md which…

content.text: Let me look at the current state of the Cycle Protocol in CLAUDE.md and the aged substep-protocol.md before discussing.

content.content:
180	Reference *citations* are double-bracketed so the brackets render
181	— `[[N]]`, or `[[2]],[[3]]` for several (comma-separated, not
182	`[2,3]` or `[[2]][[3]]`). The `[N]:` definitions in a file's
183	`# References` section and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)`
184	links stay single-bracketed. See
185	[Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.
186	
187	### Markdown anchor links
188	
189	GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
190	characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
191	**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
192	on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
193	`:`). General markdown reference:
194	[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
195	publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
196	de-facto reference implementation is
197	[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).
198	
199	### Headings and entries that record a commit
200	
201	A `chores-NN.md` `##` section header that records a specific
202	commit, the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry, and any `[N]`
203	reference to that section all use **exactly that commit's
204	title** — `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, the same string the
205	commit gets (see [Per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)).
206	E.g. the chores header `## refactor: port push to Context
207	(0.48.0-6)` and the Done line `- refactor: port push to Context
208	(0.48.0-6) [[3]]`. The `## Done` entry uses the close-out
209	commit's title.
210	
211	This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
212	`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
213	inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
214	Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
215	default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
216	line a record up with its commit.
217	
218	A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
219	progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
220	`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
221	commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
222	for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
223	dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
224	(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).
225	
226	Existing sections and `## Done` entries (most of chores-01..09;
227	the pre-`0.48.1` `## Done` lines) predate this and keep their
228	free-form text; the convention applies going forward — `0.48.2`
229	converts the `0.48.1` section + its Done entry as the worked
230	example.
231	
232	### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record
233	
234	A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
235	header — see below), then [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro +
236	bullets) for what landed and why, and any `###` design
237	subsections. Bullets here are **conceptual** — design points,
238	structural notes — never a per-file edit list. That lives in the
239	commit message body, which is the source of truth for "what
240	changed mechanically" (immutable, `git show`-able, naturally
241	scoped to the commit). The chores section is the source of truth
242	for the design thinking; the two cross-link, neither restates the
243	other.
244	
245	When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
246	hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
247	own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
248	wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
249	change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
250	with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
251	normal ref direction).
252	
253	**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
254	to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
255	drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
256	narrative; `Commits:` links them.
257	
258	### Chores commit references
259	
260	The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
261	line citing the git commit(s) that section records:
262	
263	```
264	## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)
265	
266	Commits: [[3]]
267	
268	<intro paragraph...>
269	```
270	
271	`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
272	[notes/README.md](notes/README.md#reference-numbering)),
273	**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
274	or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
275	literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
276	`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
277	GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
278	puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
279	40-hex SHA** in the title slot:
280	
281	```
282	[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
283	```
284	
285	- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
286	  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
287	  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
288	- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
289	  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
290	  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
291	  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.
292	
293	**Timing.** The commit doesn't exist when its chores section is
294	written, so the `Commits:` line is **backfilled when the next
295	change to that repo is started** — the cycle-start step grabs
296	the just-pushed commit's URL + SHA and fills it in. The single
297	newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
298	commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
299	finds it.
300	
301	## Cycle Protocol
302	
303	Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation → Work-N →
304	Close-out. One protocol; push and squash are actions you take
305	when wanted, not modes chosen up front. The mechanics below
306	run in the order they happen.
307	
308	### Numbering
309	
310	The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
311	separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
312	the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):
313	
314	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
315	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
316	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
317	
318	A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
319	rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
320	into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
321	(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
322	`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
323	`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
324	so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
325	(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
326	adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.
327	
328	Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
329	`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
330	so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.
331	
332	**Ordering caveat.** The one ordering guarantee semver gives
333	is that the bare `X.Y.Z` close-out outranks every `X.Y.Z-…`
334	dev version. Within the ladder it does *not* hold for nested
335	close-outs — `X.Y.Z-3` sorts *before* its children
336	`X.Y.Z-3.0` / `X.Y.Z-3.1`, because a prefix always precedes
337	its extensions. Cosmetic — nothing depends on within-ladder
338	order — but don't claim monotonicity.
339	
340	**Why numeric suffixes** (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:
341	semver pre-release identifiers compare per spec and Cargo
342	accepts them. `-dev` adds no information the git log doesn't
343	carry and doubles typing per commit. The final close-out
344	commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) signals completion rather than amending
345	prior commits — history stays readable, exploratory vs final
346	commits obvious.
347	
348	### Preparation
349	
350	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
351	
352	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
353	  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
354	  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
355	  [Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references).
356	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
357	- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
358	  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
359	  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
360	  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
361	  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
362	  [Prose form](#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape). A cycle
363	  that merges several entangled `## Todo` items moves all
364	  of them. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates current
365	  `## In Progress` work is a process bug.
366	- **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
367	  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
368	  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
369	  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
370	  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
371	  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
372	  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.
373	
374	### Per-commit flow
375	
376	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
377	through:
378	
379	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
380	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
381	   work.
382	2. **Do the work.**
383	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
384	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
385	   the commit captures the completed state.
386	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
387	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
388	   1. `cargo fmt`
389	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
390	   3. `cargo test`
391	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
392	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
393	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
394	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
395	   working-copy changes in their editor. Most change
396	   requests come here — iterate until the work is right.
397	6. **Write the commit description.** [Conventional
398	   Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with a
399	   version suffix:
400	
401	   ```
402	   <type>: <short description> (<version>)
403	   ```
404	
405	   - **Title**: ≤50 chars. Common types: `feat`, `fix`,
406	     `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`. The version
407	     suffix counts toward the 50 — favor terse phrasings.
408	   - **Body**: [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
409	     wrap ≤72. App-repo bullets are **file-by-file** — one
410	     bullet per file changed, file plus a one-line gist.
411	     When a file has several distinct changes, list them
412	     as `-` sub-bullets under the file bullet.
413	
414	     ```
415	     - path/to/file1
416	       - first distinct change
417	       - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
418	          with continuation indented 5
419	     ```
420	
421	     The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
422	     cycle's mechanical change record; chores carries the
423	     narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
424	     "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###`
425	     subsection.
426	   - **`ochid:` trailer** as the last line of the body —
427	     see [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).
428	   - **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: same shape; bullets
429	     describe in-session activity rather than code changes.
430	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
431	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
432	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
433	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
434	   keeps the verb visible):
435	
436	   ```
437	   jj commit -m \
438	   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
439	   -m "<intro paragraph>
440	
441	   - file1: gist
442	   - file2: gist
443	
444	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
445	   -R .
446	   ```
447	
448	**Two overrides apply:**
449	
450	- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
451	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
452	  surface it; don't push through.
453	- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
454	  a review or question forward.
455	
456	### Reviewing changes
457	
458	Work review looks at **uncommitted working-copy changes**
459	in your editor, on the way to commit. Zed shows the diff
460	naturally; for terminal use:
461	
462	```
463	jj diff                          # working-copy diff (uncommitted)
464	jj diff -r @-                    # diff of the previous commit
465	jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>      # any two revisions
466	jj show -r <X>                   # description + diff for one rev
467	```
468	
469	Pipe through `delta` or `diff-so-fancy | less -R` for color
470	and side-by-side. To launch your editor as the diff tool:
471	
472	```
473	# ~/.config/jj/config.toml
474	[ui]
475	diff-editor = ["zed", "--diff", "$left", "$right"]
476	```
477	
478	Then `jj diff --from X --to Y` opens it. Don't `jj edit
479	-r @-` to view a past commit — that marks it mutable and
480	shifts the working-copy pointer; use `jj diff -r @-` or
481	`jj show -r @-`.
482	
483	[`notes/substep-protocol.md`](notes/substep-protocol.md) is
484	a longer-form companion (close-out squash recipe, recovery)
485	that predates this rewrite and still uses the old "sub-step"
486	vocabulary — reconciling it is a follow-up. Revset
487	primitives (`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) are in
488	[`notes/jj-revsets.md`](notes/jj-revsets.md).
489	
490	### Close-out
491	
492	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):
493	
494	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
495	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
496	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
497	  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
498	  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
499	  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
500	  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
501	  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
502	  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
503	  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
504	  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
505	  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
506	  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
507	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
508	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
509	
510	The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
511	in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
512	Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
513	— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
514	keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
515	review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
516	close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
517	squash-language before the decision is made.
518	
519	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
520	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
521	see [Pushing](#pushing).
522	
523	### Pushing
524	
525	**Push is discretionary** — done when you want to back up
526	work or publish interim progress, and **always at
527	close-out** (the cycle's result must be published). Interim
528	pushes are a judgment call; nothing forces push-per-commit.
529	
530	**Post close-out review (close-out push only).** After
531	the close-out commit and before any `jj squash` / `jj
532	rebase` / `jj git push` invocation, stop and ask the user
533	which shape the cycle lands as (squash / merge / keep)
534	and to approve the push. The choice changes the commits
535	on the target and is hard to undo cleanly once pushed;
536	treat it like the Work and Commit Description reviews —
537	surface the options, wait for the user, don't auto-pick.
538	ESC-ESC and the deviation override apply as usual.
539	
540	**Squash, merge, or keep at the close-out push.** Before
541	the publishing push, decide what shape the cycle lands as:
542	
543	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
544	  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
545	  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
546	  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
547	  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its header
548	  becomes the close-out commit title), and every anchor
549	  back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed — real
550	  work, done as part of the squash.
551	- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
552	  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
553	  sub-step commits stay reachable as the merge's second
554	  parent. `git log` shows everything; `git log
555	  --first-parent` shows main as one jump per cycle. Best
556	  when the sub-steps are worth preserving but main should
557	  read linearly. The paired `.claude` commit's body
558	  carries a multi-line ochid listing every app commit in
559	  the push — the multi-ochid case
560	  [`notes/forks-multi-user.md`](notes/forks-multi-user.md)
561	  designs. Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev> -d
562	  <sub-tip>` before invoking `jj git push`.
563	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on main —
564	  when the decomposition is itself informative and you
565	  want `git log` to show every step. Each chores section
566	  keeps its own header / `Commits:` ref; no consolidation
567	  churn.
568	
569	If squashing or merging, do it before invoking `vc-x1
570	push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
571	non-standard shapes).
572	
573	The flow is wrapped by `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`, which runs:
574	
575	1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` / `clippy`
576	   / `test`).
577	2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
578	   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
579	3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
580	   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
581	4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
582	   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
583	   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).
584	
585	`<bookmark>` is the **app-side working branch** (often
586	`main`, sometimes a feature branch). `.claude` always pushes
587	its `main` regardless of the app-side bookmark. See
588	`vc-x1 push --help` for the full flag list, resumability
589	(`--from`, `--restart`, `--status`), and recovery; CLAUDE.md
590	keeps only the rules that govern bot behavior. `vc-x1 sync
591	--check` may also be run manually before editing to surface
592	remote changes early.
593	
594	**`.claude` cadence — once per push.** The `.claude`
595	working-copy node accumulates session data across the
596	cycle's commits and is described + committed when a push
597	happens — one push, one `.claude` commit, paired with every
598	code commit in that push. Its change ID is stable from the
599	first code commit that references it through to that
600	`.claude` commit (working-copy snapshots, `jj describe`, and
601	the finalize `jj commit` all preserve the change ID), so the
602	`ochid:` trailers resolve across the push.
603	
604	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives on
605	`main`. The repo has no need for parallel feature-branch
606	bookmarks to mirror app-side branches; when the app sits on
607	a feature branch, `.claude` still commits to `main`.
608	Cross-references between sides are carried by the `ochid:`
609	trailer + commit timestamps; that is enough. **Do not
610	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror app-side
611	branches** — tried during 0.41.1-6.7, went unused, and
612	risks the bot steering session pushes to the wrong remote
613	ref.
614	
615	**Bot communication at the reviews.** Use plain prose —
616	no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N", etc.):
617	
618	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
619	  "Work complete. Please review."
620	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE` and
621	  `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to commit (or
622	  push). Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
623	  --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"` invocation by
624	  default; it's mechanical and obvious from title/body.
625	  Show it only on explicit request.
626	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
627	  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief trade-off
628	  summary, plus the push target; wait for the user's
629	  choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` / `jj git

file.content:
Reference *citations* are double-bracketed so the brackets render
— `[[N]]`, or `[[2]],[[3]]` for several (comma-separated, not
`[2,3]` or `[[2]][[3]]`). The `[N]:` definitions in a file's
`# References` section and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)`
links stay single-bracketed. See
[Todo format](notes/README.md#todo-format) for details.

### Markdown anchor links

GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
`:`). General markdown reference:
[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
de-facto reference implementation is
[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).

### Headings and entries that record a commit

A `chores-NN.md` `##` section header that records a specific
commit, the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry, and any `[N]`
reference to that section all use **exactly that commit's
title** — `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, the same string the
commit gets (see [Per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)).
E.g. the chores header `## refactor: port push to Context
(0.48.0-6)` and the Done line `- refactor: port push to Context
(0.48.0-6) [[3]]`. The `## Done` entry uses the close-out
commit's title.

This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
line a record up with its commit.

A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).

Existing sections and `## Done` entries (most of chores-01..09;
the pre-`0.48.1` `## Done` lines) predate this and keep their
free-form text; the convention applies going forward — `0.48.2`
converts the `0.48.1` section + its Done entry as the worked
example.

### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record

A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
header — see below), then [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro +
bullets) for what landed and why, and any `###` design
subsections. Bullets here are **conceptual** — design points,
structural notes — never a per-file edit list. That lives in the
commit message body, which is the source of truth for "what
changed mechanically" (immutable, `git show`-able, naturally
scoped to the commit). The chores section is the source of truth
for the design thinking; the two cross-link, neither restates the
other.

When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
normal ref direction).

**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
narrative; `Commits:` links them.

### Chores commit references

The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
line citing the git commit(s) that section records:

```
## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)

Commits: [[3]]

<intro paragraph...>
```

`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
[notes/README.md](notes/README.md#reference-numbering)),
**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
40-hex SHA** in the title slot:

```
[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
```

- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.

**Timing.** The commit doesn't exist when its chores section is
written, so the `Commits:` line is **backfilled when the next
change to that repo is started** — the cycle-start step grabs
the just-pushed commit's URL + SHA and fills it in. The single
newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
finds it.

## Cycle Protocol

Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation → Work-N →
Close-out. One protocol; push and squash are actions you take
when wanted, not modes chosen up front. The mechanics below
run in the order they happen.

### Numbering

The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.

Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.

**Ordering caveat.** The one ordering guarantee semver gives
is that the bare `X.Y.Z` close-out outranks every `X.Y.Z-…`
dev version. Within the ladder it does *not* hold for nested
close-outs — `X.Y.Z-3` sorts *before* its children
`X.Y.Z-3.0` / `X.Y.Z-3.1`, because a prefix always precedes
its extensions. Cosmetic — nothing depends on within-ladder
order — but don't claim monotonicity.

**Why numeric suffixes** (`-0`, `-1`, …) rather than `-devN`:
semver pre-release identifiers compare per spec and Cargo
accepts them. `-dev` adds no information the git log doesn't
carry and doubles typing per commit. The final close-out
commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) signals completion rather than amending
prior commits — history stays readable, exploratory vs final
commits obvious.

### Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
  [Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references).
- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
  [Prose form](#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape). A cycle
  that merges several entangled `## Todo` items moves all
  of them. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates current
  `## In Progress` work is a process bug.
- **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.

### Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
   work.
2. **Do the work.**
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
   the commit captures the completed state.
4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
   cycle mandatory at close-out):
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   working-copy changes in their editor. Most change
   requests come here — iterate until the work is right.
6. **Write the commit description.** [Conventional
   Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with a
   version suffix:

   ```
   <type>: <short description> (<version>)
   ```

   - **Title**: ≤50 chars. Common types: `feat`, `fix`,
     `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`. The version
     suffix counts toward the 50 — favor terse phrasings.
   - **Body**: [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
     wrap ≤72. App-repo bullets are **file-by-file** — one
     bullet per file changed, file plus a one-line gist.
     When a file has several distinct changes, list them
     as `-` sub-bullets under the file bullet.

     ```
     - path/to/file1
       - first distinct change
       - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
          with continuation indented 5
     ```

     The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
     cycle's mechanical change record; chores carries the
     narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
     "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###`
     subsection.
   - **`ochid:` trailer** as the last line of the body —
     see [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).
   - **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: same shape; bullets
     describe in-session activity rather than code changes.
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

### Reviewing changes

Work review looks at **uncommitted working-copy changes**
in your editor, on the way to commit. Zed shows the diff
naturally; for terminal use:

```
jj diff                          # working-copy diff (uncommitted)
jj diff -r @-                    # diff of the previous commit
jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>      # any two revisions
jj show -r <X>                   # description + diff for one rev
```

Pipe through `delta` or `diff-so-fancy | less -R` for color
and side-by-side. To launch your editor as the diff tool:

```
# ~/.config/jj/config.toml
[ui]
diff-editor = ["zed", "--diff", "$left", "$right"]
```

Then `jj diff --from X --to Y` opens it. Don't `jj edit
-r @-` to view a past commit — that marks it mutable and
shifts the working-copy pointer; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

[`notes/substep-protocol.md`](notes/substep-protocol.md) is
a longer-form companion (close-out squash recipe, recovery)
that predates this rewrite and still uses the old "sub-step"
vocabulary — reconciling it is a follow-up. Revset
primitives (`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) are in
[`notes/jj-revsets.md`](notes/jj-revsets.md).

### Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
squash-language before the decision is made.

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
see [Pushing](#pushing).

### Pushing

**Push is discretionary** — done when you want to back up
work or publish interim progress, and **always at
close-out** (the cycle's result must be published). Interim
pushes are a judgment call; nothing forces push-per-commit.

**Post close-out review (close-out push only).** After
the close-out commit and before any `jj squash` / `jj
rebase` / `jj git push` invocation, stop and ask the user
which shape the cycle lands as (squash / merge / keep)
and to approve the push. The choice changes the commits
on the target and is hard to undo cleanly once pushed;
treat it like the Work and Commit Description reviews —
surface the options, wait for the user, don't auto-pick.
ESC-ESC and the deviation override apply as usual.

**Squash, merge, or keep at the close-out push.** Before
the publishing push, decide what shape the cycle lands as:

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its header
  becomes the close-out commit title), and every anchor
  back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed — real
  work, done as part of the squash.
- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
  sub-step commits stay reachable as the merge's second
  parent. `git log` shows everything; `git log
  --first-parent` shows main as one jump per cycle. Best
  when the sub-steps are worth preserving but main should
  read linearly. The paired `.claude` commit's body
  carries a multi-line ochid listing every app commit in
  the push — the multi-ochid case
  [`notes/forks-multi-user.md`](notes/forks-multi-user.md)
  designs. Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev> -d
  <sub-tip>` before invoking `jj git push`.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on main —
  when the decomposition is itself informative and you
  want `git log` to show every step. Each chores section
  keeps its own header / `Commits:` ref; no consolidation
  churn.

If squashing or merging, do it before invoking `vc-x1
push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
non-standard shapes).

The flow is wrapped by `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`, which runs:

1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` / `clippy`
   / `test`).
2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).

`<bookmark>` is the **app-side working branch** (often
`main`, sometimes a feature branch). `.claude` always pushes
its `main` regardless of the app-side bookmark. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for the full flag list, resumability
(`--from`, `--restart`, `--status`), and recovery; CLAUDE.md
keeps only the rules that govern bot behavior. `vc-x1 sync
--check` may also be run manually before editing to surface
remote changes early.

**`.claude` cadence — once per push.** The `.claude`
working-copy node accumulates session data across the
cycle's commits and is described + committed when a push
happens — one push, one `.claude` commit, paired with every
code commit in that push. Its change ID is stable from the
first code commit that references it through to that
`.claude` commit (working-copy snapshots, `jj describe`, and
the finalize `jj commit` all preserve the change ID), so the
`ochid:` trailers resolve across the push.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives on
`main`. The repo has no need for parallel feature-branch
bookmarks to mirror app-side branches; when the app sits on
a feature branch, `.claude` still commits to `main`.
Cross-references between sides are carried by the `ochid:`
trailer + commit timestamps; that is enough. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror app-side
branches** — tried during 0.41.1-6.7, went unused, and
risks the bot steering session pushes to the wrong remote
ref.

**Bot communication at the reviews.** Use plain prose —
no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE` and
  `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to commit (or
  push). Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
  --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"` invocation by
  default; it's mechanical and obvious from title/body.
  Show it only on explicit request.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief trade-off
  summary, plus the push target; wait for the user's
  choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` / `jj git

1	# Substep protocol
2	
3	When a single multi-step `X.Y.Z-N` cycle covers several
4	separable concerns and the per-step diff would be too large
5	to review in one pass, work each concern as its own substep
6	on its own jj `@`. The ladder collapses into a single
7	commit at close-out and ships through the normal
8	`vc-x1 push` flow.
9	
10	This file is the protocol of record. CLAUDE.md points here.
11	
12	## When to use
13	
14	Use substeps when an `X.Y.Z-N` step splits naturally into
15	two or more units of work that:
16	
17	- can be reviewed independently,
18	- want bisectable history during the cycle (so a regression
19	  surfaced by tests can be pinned to one substep),
20	- benefit from intermediate-test gates rather than one
21	  end-of-step gate.
22	
23	Don't use substeps for trivial or single-concern steps. The
24	per-substep `cargo test --bins` cost is real; pay it only
25	when the review and bisection wins are real.
26	
27	## Per-substep contract
28	
29	For each substep:
30	
31	1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
32	2. Do the substep's work on this `@`.
33	3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable.** Build and
34	   clippy alone are not enough — past experience
35	   (0.41.1-6.5) had a regression introduced in an early
36	   substep that didn't surface until a later substep ran
37	   the full suite, by which point bisection cost was
38	   higher than it needed to be.
39	4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — give the substep
40	   a working title and body. The title does **not** need a
41	   version suffix during the ladder; close-out collects
42	   every substep into one final commit which carries the
43	   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker. Substep titles are scratch — they
44	   never reach the remote.
45	
46	The ladder is local-only. Substep commits exist for review
47	and bisection during the cycle and disappear with the
48	close-out squash.
49	
50	## Navigating the ladder
51	
52	For revset basics (chid, cid, `@`, `@-`, `@+`, `..`, `::`,
53	prefix matching) see [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).
54	
55	Common moves while a ladder is active:
56	
57	- `jj log -r 'all()' -R .` — see the whole ladder at a
58	  glance
59	- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any commit in
60	  the ladder by chid prefix. Useful for bisection: re-run
61	  the failing test at each candidate without manual
62	  checkout juggling.
63	- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two substeps.
64	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one substep's diff in
65	  isolation.
66	
67	### Watch the set of substep commits
68	
69	Below is an example of using a revset using the `::` operator
70	and chosing last commit pushed to the remote, lulqxovr. So
71	by using log and `lu::` we can immediately see all of the commits
72	associated with the substep:
73	```
74	wink@3900x 26-05-01T23:32:51.723Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1 (@xqpnxtyu)
75	$ jj log -r lu::
76	○  pynknoqu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 33612c81
77	│  docs: CLAUDE.md substep-protocol pointer
78	@  xqpnxtyu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 f40c49a7
79	│  docs: substep-protocol.md formal protocol
80	○  lzqmlpvy wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 8c722f8c
81	│  docs: jj-revsets.md review fixes
82	○  wmmxkuoz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 11:28:46 substep-protocol-x1 65a6705f
83	│  chore: scratch — jj-revsets learning + substep test scaffolding
84	◆  lulqxovr wink@saville.com 2026-04-27 22:27:23 main 55eadc8e
85	│  docs: capture 0.41.1 init+clone redesign plan (0.42.0-4)
86	~
87	```
88	
89	Substep work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected.
90	Use `jj edit -r <chid>` to switch to whichever substep you
91	want to work on — for example, `jj edit -r x` lands on the
92	substep whose chid starts with `x`, where it can be examined,
93	tested, or modified. Modifications to a selected substep
94	rewrite that commit in place, and descendants auto-rebase.
95	
96	## Close-out/squash the substeps into a Step
97	
98	When all substeps are done and `cargo test --bins` is green
99	on the latest:
100	
101	```
102	jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
103	```
104	
105	Where `<base>` is the parent of the **first** substep —
106	typically the bookmark or commit the ladder branched from.
107	The `-u` flag (use destination message) tells jj to keep
108	`@`'s description and discard the source descriptions; the
109	intermediate titles served their purpose, and `vc-x1 push`
110	will overwrite `@`'s description anyway.
111	
112	After squash:
113	
114	- All substep diffs are folded into `@`.
115	- The intermediate substep commits become empty and are
116	  abandoned automatically.
117	- History is linear: `<base> → @`.
118	
119	Then run `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` exactly as for any other
120	step. Push's `commit-app` stage finalizes `@` with the
121	close-out title/body and the proper `(X.Y.Z-N)` version
122	suffix.
123	
124	### Validation status
125	
126	The recipe was validated 2026-05-01 against scratch ladders
127	built by [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh):
128	
129	- 3-substep ladder (`base..@-` = {s1, s2}, `@ = s3`):
130	  squash produces `base → @` linear; intermediate commits
131	  abandoned; `@` retains its description.
132	- 5-substep ladder (`base..@-` = {s1..s4}, `@ = s5`): same
133	  outcome at scale.
134	
135	The recipe is N-agnostic for N ≥ 2 substeps. For N = 1 the
136	close-out squash is a no-op (`base..@-` is empty when `@-`
137	is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.
138	
139	## Recovery
140	
141	If a substep goes wrong and you want to back out without
142	losing prior substeps:
143	
144	- **Discard the current substep's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
145	  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
146	  parent. Earlier substeps are untouched.
147	- **Edit an earlier substep.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
148	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-substep-chid>`
149	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
150	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
151	  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
152	  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
153	  substep work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
154	  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
155	  above is.
156	
157	## Worked example
158	
159	A ladder of 3 substeps starting from bookmark `main` at
160	commit `abc1234`:
161	
162	```
163	jj new -R .                                # substep 1
164	... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
165	jj describe -m "substep 1: ..." -R .
166	
167	jj new -R .                                # substep 2
168	... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
169	jj describe -m "substep 2: ..." -R .
170	
171	jj new -R .                                # substep 3
172	... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
173	jj describe -m "substep 3: ..." -R .
174	```
175	
176	`jj log -r 'all()'` now shows:
177	
178	```
179	@  yyy ... substep 3: ...
180	○  xxx ... substep 2: ...
181	○  www ... substep 1: ...
182	○  abc1234 main | <previous step's commit>
183	```
184	
185	Close out:
186	
187	```
188	jj squash --from "main..@-" --into @ -u -R .
189	```
190	
191	Result:
192	
193	```
194	@  yyy ... substep 3: ...   (now contains all substep diffs)
195	○  abc1234 main | <previous step's commit>
196	```
197	
198	Then `vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."` finalizes
199	the cycle.
200	
201	**Note:** Above you see `-R .` being used to explicitly select
202	the current repo. For the bot the explicitness is desirable;
203	for others it's optional.
204	
205	# References
206	
207	- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
208	  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
209	- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
210	  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
211	  for squash-recipe experiments.
212	- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-substep usage. The
213	  per-substep `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
214	  after a regression introduced in an early substep wasn't
215	  caught until a later substep ran the full suite.

# Substep protocol

When a single multi-step `X.Y.Z-N` cycle covers several
separable concerns and the per-step diff would be too large
to review in one pass, work each concern as its own substep
on its own jj `@`. The ladder collapses into a single
commit at close-out and ships through the normal
`vc-x1 push` flow.

This file is the protocol of record. CLAUDE.md points here.

## When to use

Use substeps when an `X.Y.Z-N` step splits naturally into
two or more units of work that:

- can be reviewed independently,
- want bisectable history during the cycle (so a regression
  surfaced by tests can be pinned to one substep),
- benefit from intermediate-test gates rather than one
  end-of-step gate.

Don't use substeps for trivial or single-concern steps. The
per-substep `cargo test --bins` cost is real; pay it only
when the review and bisection wins are real.

## Per-substep contract

For each substep:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the substep's work on this `@`.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable.** Build and
   clippy alone are not enough — past experience
   (0.41.1-6.5) had a regression introduced in an early
   substep that didn't surface until a later substep ran
   the full suite, by which point bisection cost was
   higher than it needed to be.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — give the substep
   a working title and body. The title does **not** need a
   version suffix during the ladder; close-out collects
   every substep into one final commit which carries the
   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker. Substep titles are scratch — they
   never reach the remote.

The ladder is local-only. Substep commits exist for review
and bisection during the cycle and disappear with the
close-out squash.

## Navigating the ladder

For revset basics (chid, cid, `@`, `@-`, `@+`, `..`, `::`,
prefix matching) see [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).

Common moves while a ladder is active:

- `jj log -r 'all()' -R .` — see the whole ladder at a
  glance
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any commit in
  the ladder by chid prefix. Useful for bisection: re-run
  the failing test at each candidate without manual
  checkout juggling.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two substeps.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one substep's diff in
  isolation.

### Watch the set of substep commits

Below is an example of using a revset using the `::` operator
and chosing last commit pushed to the remote, lulqxovr. So
by using log and `lu::` we can immediately see all of the commits
associated with the substep:
```
wink@3900x 26-05-01T23:32:51.723Z:~/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1 (@xqpnxtyu)
$ jj log -r lu::
○  pynknoqu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 33612c81
│  docs: CLAUDE.md substep-protocol pointer
@  xqpnxtyu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 f40c49a7
│  docs: substep-protocol.md formal protocol
○  lzqmlpvy wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 8c722f8c
│  docs: jj-revsets.md review fixes
○  wmmxkuoz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 11:28:46 substep-protocol-x1 65a6705f
│  chore: scratch — jj-revsets learning + substep test scaffolding
◆  lulqxovr wink@saville.com 2026-04-27 22:27:23 main 55eadc8e
│  docs: capture 0.41.1 init+clone redesign plan (0.42.0-4)
~
```

Substep work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected.
Use `jj edit -r <chid>` to switch to whichever substep you
want to work on — for example, `jj edit -r x` lands on the
substep whose chid starts with `x`, where it can be examined,
tested, or modified. Modifications to a selected substep
rewrite that commit in place, and descendants auto-rebase.

## Close-out/squash the substeps into a Step

When all substeps are done and `cargo test --bins` is green
on the latest:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Where `<base>` is the parent of the **first** substep —
typically the bookmark or commit the ladder branched from.
The `-u` flag (use destination message) tells jj to keep
`@`'s description and discard the source descriptions; the
intermediate titles served their purpose, and `vc-x1 push`
will overwrite `@`'s description anyway.

After squash:

- All substep diffs are folded into `@`.
- The intermediate substep commits become empty and are
  abandoned automatically.
- History is linear: `<base> → @`.

Then run `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` exactly as for any other
step. Push's `commit-app` stage finalizes `@` with the
close-out title/body and the proper `(X.Y.Z-N)` version
suffix.

### Validation status

The recipe was validated 2026-05-01 against scratch ladders
built by [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh):

- 3-substep ladder (`base..@-` = {s1, s2}, `@ = s3`):
  squash produces `base → @` linear; intermediate commits
  abandoned; `@` retains its description.
- 5-substep ladder (`base..@-` = {s1..s4}, `@ = s5`): same
  outcome at scale.

The recipe is N-agnostic for N ≥ 2 substeps. For N = 1 the
close-out squash is a no-op (`base..@-` is empty when `@-`
is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

## Recovery

If a substep goes wrong and you want to back out without
losing prior substeps:

- **Discard the current substep's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent. Earlier substeps are untouched.
- **Edit an earlier substep.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-substep-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
  substep work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
  above is.

## Worked example

A ladder of 3 substeps starting from bookmark `main` at
commit `abc1234`:

```
jj new -R .                                # substep 1
... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
jj describe -m "substep 1: ..." -R .

jj new -R .                                # substep 2
... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
jj describe -m "substep 2: ..." -R .

jj new -R .                                # substep 3
... edit files, cargo test --bins ...
jj describe -m "substep 3: ..." -R .
```

`jj log -r 'all()'` now shows:

```
@  yyy ... substep 3: ...
○  xxx ... substep 2: ...
○  www ... substep 1: ...
○  abc1234 main | <previous step's commit>
```

Close out:

```
jj squash --from "main..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Result:

```
@  yyy ... substep 3: ...   (now contains all substep diffs)
○  abc1234 main | <previous step's commit>
```

Then `vc-x1 push main --title "..." --body "..."` finalizes
the cycle.

**Note:** Above you see `-R .` being used to explicitly select
the current repo. For the bot the explicitness is desirable;
for others it's optional.

# References

- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.
- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-substep usage. The
  per-substep `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
  after a regression introduced in an early substep wasn't
  caught until a later substep ran the full suite.

content.text:
Understood. Three significant moves: (1) extract `## Cycle Protocol` from CLAUDE.md into a new `notes/cycle-protocol.md` (single source of truth, CLAUDE.md becomes mostly a pointer), (2) retire `notes/substep-protocol.md` (the nested `X.Y.Z-N.M` mechanism in the current protocol already supersedes substeps), and (3) add `## Ideas` to `notes/todo.md`. This broadens 0.59.0's scope well past the originally planned `-1`, so the cycle's ladder needs re-planning.

Before drafting the new ladder, three design choices to settle:

questions.question: What stays in CLAUDE.md once the Cycle Protocol moves out?

options.label: Pointer + tiny essentials (Recommended)
options.description: One paragraph naming the phases (Preparation / Work-N / Close-out), the numbering rule one-liner (X.Y.Z-0 / -1 / bare), and a prominent link to notes/cycle-protocol.md. Bot has just enough to recognize the protocol at session start without reading the full file.

options.label: Pointer only
options.description: A single section that says 'the cycle protocol lives in notes/cycle-protocol.md — read it before any commit work.' Maximally non-redundant. Cost: the bot has to read the protocol file every session to know phase names.

options.label: Keep numbering + push rules in CLAUDE.md
options.description: Move only the long descriptive prose (Preparation/Per-commit/Close-out/Reviewing/Pushing) to the new file; keep Numbering and Pushing in CLAUDE.md since those are dense rules the bot consults often. More residue, less re-reading.

questions.question: Where does the new `## Ideas` section sit in `notes/todo.md`?

options.label: Between `## In Progress` and `## Priorities` (Recommended)
options.description: Top of file after In Progress — visible during the routine 60-line acquaint read, so end-of-day captures and stale items both stay in view. Forces the next Preparation to look at Ideas before declaring the cycle plan.

options.label: Between `## Priorities` and `## Todo`
options.description: After Priorities, before Todo. Keeps `## In Progress` and `## Priorities` adjacent (current shape preserved). Cost: Ideas may fall outside the 60-line read; languishing becomes easier.

options.label: Bottom of file (after `## Todo`)
options.description: Last section in todo.md. Cleanest separation; Ideas is explicitly low-priority. Cost: invisible in the routine read; relies on Preparation discipline to sweep them.

options.label: Delete; fold recovery/squash recipes into cycle-protocol.md (Recommended)
options.description: The nested `X.Y.Z-N.M` mechanism in the current protocol already covers what substeps did. Still-useful pieces (recovery recipes, the close-out squash recipe, the substep-test.sh reference) move into the new cycle-protocol.md as a `## Sub-cycle mechanics` or `## Recovery` section. The .md file itself goes away.

options.label: Delete; drop the recipes too
options.description: The recipes haven't been used much since the nested-cycle mechanism landed. Delete the file outright; if a recipe is needed later it can be rediscovered from jj docs. Smallest new file.

options.label: Leave in place as deprecated
options.description: Add a deprecation banner pointing at cycle-protocol.md; keep the content reachable for history. Cost: continued redundancy, exactly what 0.59.0 is trying to remove.

content.text:
Proposed 0.59.0 ladder (broadened — supersedes the original `-1`):

- **`0.59.0-1` Relocate.** Create `notes/cycle-protocol.md` by extracting `## Cycle Protocol` from CLAUDE.md (verbatim, no content changes). Fold substep-protocol.md's still-useful recipes (squash close-out, recovery, sub-cycle navigation) into the new file; delete substep-protocol.md. CLAUDE.md replaces `## Cycle Protocol` with a pointer + tiny essentials (phases, numbering one-liner, link).
- **`0.59.0-2` Refine.** Add `## Ideas` to notes/todo.md between In Progress and Priorities. Update cycle-protocol.md with the three 0.58.0 follow-ups, now Ideas-aware:
  - Preparation triages `## Ideas` (promote to Todo / fold into the picked cycle / drop) before declaring the plan.
  - Close-out captures unprocessed follow-ups into `## Ideas` (was originally "Todos"; Ideas is the better-fit bucket since close-out items are usually unvetted).
  - Merge becomes the default shape for multi-commit cycles.
  - Meta-note that close-out often surfaces follow-ups.
- **`0.59.0` Close-out.** Standard bookkeeping.

**One open question I have a leaning on:** `## Pre-commit Requirements` in CLAUDE.md (chores commit refs, commit-recording headings, markdown anchors) is *consulted by* the Cycle Protocol but is framed as a general writing convention. My take: leave it in CLAUDE.md and link from cycle-protocol.md — moving it widens 0.59.0's blast radius and those rules apply outside the cycle too. Easy to redirect if you'd rather it all live in the new file.

Want me to proceed with this ladder, or adjust?
