content.content:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation → Work-N →
4	Close-out. One protocol; push and squash are actions you take
5	when wanted, not modes chosen up front. The mechanics below
6	run in the order they happen.
7	
8	## Numbering
9	
10	The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
11	separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
12	the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):
13	
14	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
15	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
16	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
17	
18	A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
19	rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
20	into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
21	(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
22	`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
23	`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
24	so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
25	(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
26	adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.
27	
28	Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
29	`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
30	so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.
31	
32	## Preparation
33	
34	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
35	
36	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
37	  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
38	  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
39	  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
40	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
41	- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
42	  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
43	  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
44	  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
45	  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
46	  [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).
47	  A cycle that merges several entangled `## Todo` items moves
48	  all of them. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates current
49	  `## In Progress` work is a process bug.
50	- **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
51	  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
52	  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
53	  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
54	  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
55	  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
56	  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.
57	
58	## Per-commit flow
59	
60	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
61	through:
62	
63	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
64	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
65	   work.
66	2. **Do the work.**
67	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
68	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
69	   the commit captures the completed state.
70	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
71	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
72	   1. `cargo fmt`
73	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
74	   3. `cargo test`
75	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
76	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
77	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
78	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
79	   working-copy changes in their editor. Most change
80	   requests come here — iterate until the work is right.
81	6. **Write the commit description.** [Conventional
82	   Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with a
83	   version suffix:
84	
85	   ```
86	   <type>: <short description> (<version>)
87	   ```
88	
89	   - **Title**: ≤50 chars. Common types: `feat`, `fix`,
90	     `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`. The version
91	     suffix counts toward the 50 — favor terse phrasings.
92	   - **Body**: [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro
93	     + bullets), wrap ≤72. App-repo bullets are
94	     **file-by-file** — one bullet per file changed, file
95	     plus a one-line gist. When a file has several distinct
96	     changes, list them as `-` sub-bullets under the file
97	     bullet.
98	
99	     ```
100	     - path/to/file1
101	       - first distinct change
102	       - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
103	          with continuation indented 5
104	     ```
105	
106	     The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
107	     cycle's mechanical change record; chores carries the
108	     narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
109	     "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###`
110	     subsection.
111	   - **`ochid:` trailer** as the last line of the body —
112	     see [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).
113	   - **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: same shape; bullets
114	     describe in-session activity rather than code changes.
115	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
116	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
117	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
118	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
119	   keeps the verb visible):
120	
121	   ```
122	   jj commit -m \
123	   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
124	   -m "<intro paragraph>
125	
126	   - file1: gist
127	   - file2: gist
128	
129	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
130	   -R .
131	   ```
132	
133	**Two overrides apply:**
134	
135	- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
136	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
137	  surface it; don't push through.
138	- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
139	  a review or question forward.
140	
141	## Reviewing changes
142	
143	Work review looks at **uncommitted working-copy changes**
144	in your editor, on the way to commit. Zed shows the diff
145	naturally; for terminal use:
146	
147	```
148	jj diff                          # working-copy diff (uncommitted)
149	jj diff -r @-                    # diff of the previous commit
150	jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>      # any two revisions
151	jj show -r <X>                   # description + diff for one rev
152	```
153	
154	Pipe through `delta` or `diff-so-fancy | less -R` for color
155	and side-by-side. To launch your editor as the diff tool:
156	
157	```
158	# ~/.config/jj/config.toml
159	[ui]
160	diff-editor = ["zed", "--diff", "$left", "$right"]
161	```
162	
163	Then `jj diff --from X --to Y` opens it. Don't `jj edit
164	-r @-` to view a past commit — that marks it mutable and
165	shifts the working-copy pointer; use `jj diff -r @-` or
166	`jj show -r @-`.
167	
168	[Sub-cycles](#sub-cycles) below covers the close-out
169	squash recipe and recovery for ladders. Revset primitives
170	(`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) are in
171	[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).
172	
173	## Close-out
174	
175	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):
176	
177	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
178	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
179	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
180	  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
181	  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
182	  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
183	  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
184	  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
185	  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
186	  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
187	  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
188	  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
189	  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
190	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
191	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
192	
193	The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
194	in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
195	Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
196	— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
197	keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
198	review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
199	close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
200	squash-language before the decision is made.
201	
202	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
203	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
204	see [Pushing](#pushing).
205	
206	## Pushing
207	
208	**Push is discretionary** — done when you want to back up
209	work or publish interim progress, and **always at
210	close-out** (the cycle's result must be published). Interim
211	pushes are a judgment call; nothing forces push-per-commit.
212	
213	**Post close-out review (close-out push only).** After
214	the close-out commit and before any `jj squash` / `jj
215	rebase` / `jj git push` invocation, stop and ask the user
216	which shape the cycle lands as (squash / merge / keep)
217	and to approve the push. The choice changes the commits
218	on the target and is hard to undo cleanly once pushed;
219	treat it like the Work and Commit Description reviews —
220	surface the options, wait for the user, don't auto-pick.
221	ESC-ESC and the deviation override apply as usual.
222	
223	**Squash, merge, or keep at the close-out push.** Before
224	the publishing push, decide what shape the cycle lands as:
225	
226	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
227	  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
228	  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
229	  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
230	  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its header
231	  becomes the close-out commit title), and every anchor
232	  back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed — real
233	  work, done as part of the squash.
234	- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
235	  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
236	  sub-step commits stay reachable as the merge's second
237	  parent. `git log` shows everything; `git log
238	  --first-parent` shows main as one jump per cycle. Best
239	  when the sub-steps are worth preserving but main should
240	  read linearly. The paired `.claude` commit's body
241	  carries a multi-line ochid listing every app commit in
242	  the push — the multi-ochid case
243	  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) designs.
244	  Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev> -d <sub-tip>`
245	  before invoking `jj git push`.
246	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on main —
247	  when the decomposition is itself informative and you
248	  want `git log` to show every step. Each chores section
249	  keeps its own header / `Commits:` ref; no consolidation
250	  churn.
251	
252	If squashing or merging, do it before invoking `vc-x1
253	push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
254	non-standard shapes).
255	
256	The flow is wrapped by `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`, which runs:
257	
258	1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` / `clippy`
259	   / `test`).
260	2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
261	   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
262	3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
263	   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
264	4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
265	   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
266	   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).
267	
268	`<bookmark>` is the **app-side working branch** (often
269	`main`, sometimes a feature branch). `.claude` always pushes
270	its `main` regardless of the app-side bookmark. See
271	`vc-x1 push --help` for the full flag list, resumability
272	(`--from`, `--restart`, `--status`), and recovery; CLAUDE.md
273	keeps only the rules that govern bot behavior. `vc-x1 sync
274	--check` may also be run manually before editing to surface
275	remote changes early.
276	
277	**`.claude` cadence — once per push.** The `.claude`
278	working-copy node accumulates session data across the
279	cycle's commits and is described + committed when a push
280	happens — one push, one `.claude` commit, paired with every
281	code commit in that push. Its change ID is stable from the
282	first code commit that references it through to that
283	`.claude` commit (working-copy snapshots, `jj describe`, and
284	the finalize `jj commit` all preserve the change ID), so the
285	`ochid:` trailers resolve across the push.
286	
287	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives on
288	`main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
289	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror app-side
290	branches** — risks the bot steering session pushes to the
291	wrong remote ref.
292	
293	**Bot communication at the reviews.** Use plain prose —
294	no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N", etc.):
295	
296	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
297	  "Work complete. Please review."
298	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE` and
299	  `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to commit (or
300	  push). Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
301	  --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"` invocation by
302	  default; it's mechanical and obvious from title/body.
303	  Show it only on explicit request.
304	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
305	  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief trade-off
306	  summary, plus the push target; wait for the user's
307	  choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` / `jj git
308	  push` invocation.
309	
310	**After finalize: stop and wait.** After `vc-x1 finalize`
311	launches — whether mid-session per-push or at session-end —
312	you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a next step, edit files, run
313	tools, or emit any text until the user explicitly directs
314	you to continue. Treat finalize as a hard stop for the
315	whole turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
316	*before* executing finalize. The user controls cadence —
317	auto-proceeding bypasses the push+finalize checkpoint.
318	
319	If push exits before `finalize-claude` (e.g. failure between
320	`push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run finalize by hand:
321	
322	```
323	vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
324	```
325	
326	Clear push's saved state after any out-of-band recovery —
327	`rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
328	--restart` — otherwise push resumes from a stale stage.
329	
330	A late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
331	updating CLAUDE.md or memory) requires `jj squash
332	--ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a remote rewrite
333	and needs explicit approval like any push.
334	
335	## ochid trailers
336	
337	Every commit body carries `ochid:` trailer(s) linking it to
338	its counterpart(s) in the other repo. The path is
339	workspace-root-relative — `/.claude/<chid>` from the app
340	side, `/<chid>` from the `.claude` side.
341	
342	Because `.claude` commits once per push:
343	
344	- **Code-side commits** each carry one
345	  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
346	  working-copy change ID, stable until `.claude` is
347	  committed at push time.
348	- **The `.claude` commit** carries one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
349	  per code commit in that push: a single trailer when one
350	  code commit is pushed (the squashed case), a list when
351	  several are. The multi-line `ochid:` list and its
352	  fork / multi-user generalization are designed in
353	  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md).
354	
355	Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs
356	(first line app, second `.claude`).
357	
358	## Sub-cycles
359	
360	A Work commit `X.Y.Z-N` may need subdividing into its own
361	sub-cycle — `X.Y.Z-N.0` Preparation, `X.Y.Z-N.1` /
362	`X.Y.Z-N.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-N` Close-out. See
363	[Numbering](#numbering) for the suffix rules.
364	
365	The local ladder of sub-cycle Work commits is typically
366	kept on its own jj `@` chain and **collapsed into the
367	sub-cycle's Close-out** before the parent cycle continues.
368	Sub-cycle ladder commits are scratch — they exist for
369	review and bisection during the sub-cycle and disappear
370	into one commit at sub-cycle close-out.
371	
372	### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder
373	
374	For each Work commit in the ladder:
375	
376	1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
377	2. Do the commit's work on this `@`.
378	3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable.** Build and
379	   clippy alone are not enough — past experience
380	   (0.41.1-6.5) had a regression introduced in an early
381	   ladder commit that didn't surface until a later one ran
382	   the full suite, by which point bisection cost was higher
383	   than it needed to be.
384	4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — give it a working
385	   title and body. The title does **not** need a version
386	   suffix during the ladder; the sub-cycle Close-out collects
387	   every commit into one final commit carrying the
388	   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker. Ladder titles are scratch — they
389	   never reach the remote.
390	
391	### Navigating the ladder
392	
393	Common moves while a ladder is active:
394	
395	- `jj log -r 'all()' -R .` — see the whole ladder at a
396	  glance.
397	- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any commit in
398	  the ladder by chid prefix. Useful for bisection: re-run
399	  the failing test at each candidate without manual
400	  checkout juggling.
401	- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
402	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit's diff in
403	  isolation.
404	
405	Example using a revset with the `::` operator from the last
406	commit pushed to the remote:
407	
408	```
409	$ jj log -r lu::
410	○  pynknoqu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 33612c81
411	│  docs: cycle protocol pointer
412	@  xqpnxtyu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 f40c49a7
413	│  docs: cycle protocol formal protocol
414	○  lzqmlpvy wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 8c722f8c
415	│  docs: jj-revsets.md review fixes
416	○  wmmxkuoz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 11:28:46 65a6705f
417	│  chore: scratch — jj-revsets learning + scaffolding
418	◆  lulqxovr wink@saville.com 2026-04-27 22:27:23 main 55eadc8e
419	│  docs: capture 0.41.1 init+clone redesign plan (0.42.0-4)
420	~
421	```
422	
423	Ladder work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected.
424	Use `jj edit -r <chid>` to switch to whichever ladder
425	commit you want to work on. Modifications to a selected
426	commit rewrite that commit in place; descendants auto-rebase.
427	
428	### Close-out: squash the ladder
429	
430	When all ladder Work commits are done and `cargo test --bins`
431	is green on the latest:
432	
433	```
434	jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
435	```
436	
437	Where `<base>` is the parent of the **first** ladder
438	commit — typically the bookmark or commit the ladder
439	branched from. The `-u` flag (use destination message)
440	tells jj to keep `@`'s description and discard the source
441	descriptions; the intermediate titles served their purpose,
442	and `vc-x1 push` will overwrite `@`'s description anyway.
443	
444	After squash:
445	
446	- All ladder diffs are folded into `@`.
447	- The intermediate commits become empty and are
448	  abandoned automatically.
449	- History is linear: `<base> → @`.
450	
451	Then run `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` exactly as for any other
452	commit. Push's `commit-app` stage finalizes `@` with the
453	sub-cycle Close-out title/body and the proper `(X.Y.Z-N)`
454	version suffix.
455	
456	For N = 1 the close-out squash is a no-op (`base..@-` is
457	empty when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit
458	directly.
459	
460	### Recovery
461	
462	If a ladder commit goes wrong and you want to back out
463	without losing prior commits:
464	
465	- **Discard the current commit's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
466	  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
467	  parent. Earlier commits are untouched.
468	- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
469	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
470	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
471	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
472	  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
473	  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
474	  ladder work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
475	  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
476	  above is.
477	
478	# References
479	
480	- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
481	  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
482	- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) — multi-ochid
483	  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
484	- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
485	  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
486	  for squash-recipe experiments.
487	- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-commit ladder usage. The
488	  per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
489	  after a regression introduced in an early commit wasn't
490	  caught until a later one ran the full suite.
491

file.content:
# Cycle protocol

Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation → Work-N →
Close-out. One protocol; push and squash are actions you take
when wanted, not modes chosen up front. The mechanics below
run in the order they happen.

## Numbering

The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.

Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
  [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).
  A cycle that merges several entangled `## Todo` items moves
  all of them. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates current
  `## In Progress` work is a process bug.
- **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.

## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
   work.
2. **Do the work.**
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
   the commit captures the completed state.
4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
   cycle mandatory at close-out):
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   working-copy changes in their editor. Most change
   requests come here — iterate until the work is right.
6. **Write the commit description.** [Conventional
   Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with a
   version suffix:

   ```
   <type>: <short description> (<version>)
   ```

   - **Title**: ≤50 chars. Common types: `feat`, `fix`,
     `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`. The version
     suffix counts toward the 50 — favor terse phrasings.
   - **Body**: [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro
     + bullets), wrap ≤72. App-repo bullets are
     **file-by-file** — one bullet per file changed, file
     plus a one-line gist. When a file has several distinct
     changes, list them as `-` sub-bullets under the file
     bullet.

     ```
     - path/to/file1
       - first distinct change
       - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
          with continuation indented 5
     ```

     The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
     cycle's mechanical change record; chores carries the
     narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
     "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###`
     subsection.
   - **`ochid:` trailer** as the last line of the body —
     see [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).
   - **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: same shape; bullets
     describe in-session activity rather than code changes.
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at **uncommitted working-copy changes**
in your editor, on the way to commit. Zed shows the diff
naturally; for terminal use:

```
jj diff                          # working-copy diff (uncommitted)
jj diff -r @-                    # diff of the previous commit
jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>      # any two revisions
jj show -r <X>                   # description + diff for one rev
```

Pipe through `delta` or `diff-so-fancy | less -R` for color
and side-by-side. To launch your editor as the diff tool:

```
# ~/.config/jj/config.toml
[ui]
diff-editor = ["zed", "--diff", "$left", "$right"]
```

Then `jj diff --from X --to Y` opens it. Don't `jj edit
-r @-` to view a past commit — that marks it mutable and
shifts the working-copy pointer; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

[Sub-cycles](#sub-cycles) below covers the close-out
squash recipe and recovery for ladders. Revset primitives
(`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) are in
[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
squash-language before the decision is made.

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
see [Pushing](#pushing).

## Pushing

**Push is discretionary** — done when you want to back up
work or publish interim progress, and **always at
close-out** (the cycle's result must be published). Interim
pushes are a judgment call; nothing forces push-per-commit.

**Post close-out review (close-out push only).** After
the close-out commit and before any `jj squash` / `jj
rebase` / `jj git push` invocation, stop and ask the user
which shape the cycle lands as (squash / merge / keep)
and to approve the push. The choice changes the commits
on the target and is hard to undo cleanly once pushed;
treat it like the Work and Commit Description reviews —
surface the options, wait for the user, don't auto-pick.
ESC-ESC and the deviation override apply as usual.

**Squash, merge, or keep at the close-out push.** Before
the publishing push, decide what shape the cycle lands as:

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its header
  becomes the close-out commit title), and every anchor
  back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed — real
  work, done as part of the squash.
- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
  sub-step commits stay reachable as the merge's second
  parent. `git log` shows everything; `git log
  --first-parent` shows main as one jump per cycle. Best
  when the sub-steps are worth preserving but main should
  read linearly. The paired `.claude` commit's body
  carries a multi-line ochid listing every app commit in
  the push — the multi-ochid case
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) designs.
  Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev> -d <sub-tip>`
  before invoking `jj git push`.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on main —
  when the decomposition is itself informative and you
  want `git log` to show every step. Each chores section
  keeps its own header / `Commits:` ref; no consolidation
  churn.

If squashing or merging, do it before invoking `vc-x1
push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
non-standard shapes).

The flow is wrapped by `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`, which runs:

1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` / `clippy`
   / `test`).
2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).

`<bookmark>` is the **app-side working branch** (often
`main`, sometimes a feature branch). `.claude` always pushes
its `main` regardless of the app-side bookmark. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for the full flag list, resumability
(`--from`, `--restart`, `--status`), and recovery; CLAUDE.md
keeps only the rules that govern bot behavior. `vc-x1 sync
--check` may also be run manually before editing to surface
remote changes early.

**`.claude` cadence — once per push.** The `.claude`
working-copy node accumulates session data across the
cycle's commits and is described + committed when a push
happens — one push, one `.claude` commit, paired with every
code commit in that push. Its change ID is stable from the
first code commit that references it through to that
`.claude` commit (working-copy snapshots, `jj describe`, and
the finalize `jj commit` all preserve the change ID), so the
`ochid:` trailers resolve across the push.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives on
`main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror app-side
branches** — risks the bot steering session pushes to the
wrong remote ref.

**Bot communication at the reviews.** Use plain prose —
no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE` and
  `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to commit (or
  push). Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
  --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"` invocation by
  default; it's mechanical and obvious from title/body.
  Show it only on explicit request.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief trade-off
  summary, plus the push target; wait for the user's
  choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` / `jj git
  push` invocation.

**After finalize: stop and wait.** After `vc-x1 finalize`
launches — whether mid-session per-push or at session-end —
you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a next step, edit files, run
tools, or emit any text until the user explicitly directs
you to continue. Treat finalize as a hard stop for the
whole turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
*before* executing finalize. The user controls cadence —
auto-proceeding bypasses the push+finalize checkpoint.

If push exits before `finalize-claude` (e.g. failure between
`push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run finalize by hand:

```
vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
```

Clear push's saved state after any out-of-band recovery —
`rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
--restart` — otherwise push resumes from a stale stage.

A late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
updating CLAUDE.md or memory) requires `jj squash
--ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a remote rewrite
and needs explicit approval like any push.

## ochid trailers

Every commit body carries `ochid:` trailer(s) linking it to
its counterpart(s) in the other repo. The path is
workspace-root-relative — `/.claude/<chid>` from the app
side, `/<chid>` from the `.claude` side.

Because `.claude` commits once per push:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
  working-copy change ID, stable until `.claude` is
  committed at push time.
- **The `.claude` commit** carries one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
  per code commit in that push: a single trailer when one
  code commit is pushed (the squashed case), a list when
  several are. The multi-line `ochid:` list and its
  fork / multi-user generalization are designed in
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs
(first line app, second `.claude`).

## Sub-cycles

A Work commit `X.Y.Z-N` may need subdividing into its own
sub-cycle — `X.Y.Z-N.0` Preparation, `X.Y.Z-N.1` /
`X.Y.Z-N.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-N` Close-out. See
[Numbering](#numbering) for the suffix rules.

The local ladder of sub-cycle Work commits is typically
kept on its own jj `@` chain and **collapsed into the
sub-cycle's Close-out** before the parent cycle continues.
Sub-cycle ladder commits are scratch — they exist for
review and bisection during the sub-cycle and disappear
into one commit at sub-cycle close-out.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work on this `@`.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable.** Build and
   clippy alone are not enough — past experience
   (0.41.1-6.5) had a regression introduced in an early
   ladder commit that didn't surface until a later one ran
   the full suite, by which point bisection cost was higher
   than it needed to be.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — give it a working
   title and body. The title does **not** need a version
   suffix during the ladder; the sub-cycle Close-out collects
   every commit into one final commit carrying the
   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker. Ladder titles are scratch — they
   never reach the remote.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves while a ladder is active:

- `jj log -r 'all()' -R .` — see the whole ladder at a
  glance.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any commit in
  the ladder by chid prefix. Useful for bisection: re-run
  the failing test at each candidate without manual
  checkout juggling.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit's diff in
  isolation.

Example using a revset with the `::` operator from the last
commit pushed to the remote:

```
$ jj log -r lu::
○  pynknoqu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 33612c81
│  docs: cycle protocol pointer
@  xqpnxtyu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 f40c49a7
│  docs: cycle protocol formal protocol
○  lzqmlpvy wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 8c722f8c
│  docs: jj-revsets.md review fixes
○  wmmxkuoz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 11:28:46 65a6705f
│  chore: scratch — jj-revsets learning + scaffolding
◆  lulqxovr wink@saville.com 2026-04-27 22:27:23 main 55eadc8e
│  docs: capture 0.41.1 init+clone redesign plan (0.42.0-4)
~
```

Ladder work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected.
Use `jj edit -r <chid>` to switch to whichever ladder
commit you want to work on. Modifications to a selected
commit rewrite that commit in place; descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and `cargo test --bins`
is green on the latest:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Where `<base>` is the parent of the **first** ladder
commit — typically the bookmark or commit the ladder
branched from. The `-u` flag (use destination message)
tells jj to keep `@`'s description and discard the source
descriptions; the intermediate titles served their purpose,
and `vc-x1 push` will overwrite `@`'s description anyway.

After squash:

- All ladder diffs are folded into `@`.
- The intermediate commits become empty and are
  abandoned automatically.
- History is linear: `<base> → @`.

Then run `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` exactly as for any other
commit. Push's `commit-app` stage finalizes `@` with the
sub-cycle Close-out title/body and the proper `(X.Y.Z-N)`
version suffix.

For N = 1 the close-out squash is a no-op (`base..@-` is
empty when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit
directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong and you want to back out
without losing prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent. Earlier commits are untouched.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
  ladder work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
  above is.

# References

- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) — multi-ochid
  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.
- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-commit ladder usage. The
  per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
  after a regression introduced in an early commit wasn't
  caught until a later one ran the full suite.

input.content:
# Cycle protocol

## Cycles

A cycle has three phases:

- **Preparation** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's first commit.
  Sets up the cycle by bumping the version, picking up a
  `## Todo` item into `## In Progress`, and opening the
  chores section header. Operational steps:
  [Preparation](#preparation).
- **Work-N** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the work commits
  that implement the change. As many as the change needs;
  each runs through the [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
  Operational notes: [Work-N](#work-n).
- **Close-out** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's last commit.
  Bookkeeping only: moves `## In Progress` into the chores
  section, adds a `## Done` entry, optionally updates
  `notes/README.md`. Operational steps: [Close-out](#close-out).

A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
result must always be published at close-out. See
[Pushing](#pushing).

**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
for the local-ladder mechanics.

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
  [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).
  A cycle that merges several entangled `## Todo` items moves
  all of them. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates current
  `## In Progress` work is a process bug.
- **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits implement the change. Number as
many `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … as the change needs; each
runs through the [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
Interim pushes are optional (backup, progress visibility);
close-out is the only mandatory push — see
[Pushing](#pushing). For sub-cycles see
[Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
squash-language before the decision is made.

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
see [Pushing](#pushing).

## Numbering

The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.

Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.

## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
   work.
2. **Do the work.**
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
   the commit captures the completed state.
4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
   cycle mandatory at close-out):
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   working-copy changes in their editor. Most change
   requests come here — iterate until the work is right.
6. **Write the commit description.** [Conventional
   Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with a
   version suffix:

   ```
   <type>: <short description> (<version>)
   ```

   - **Title**: ≤50 chars. Common types: `feat`, `fix`,
     `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`. The version
     suffix counts toward the 50 — favor terse phrasings.
   - **Body**: [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro
     + bullets), wrap ≤72. App-repo bullets are
     **file-by-file** — one bullet per file changed, file
     plus a one-line gist. When a file has several distinct
     changes, list them as `-` sub-bullets under the file
     bullet.

     ```
     - path/to/file1
       - first distinct change
       - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
          with continuation indented 5
     ```

     The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
     cycle's mechanical change record; chores carries the
     narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
     "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###`
     subsection.
   - **`ochid:` trailer** as the last line of the body —
     see [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).
   - **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: same shape; bullets
     describe in-session activity rather than code changes.
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at **uncommitted working-copy changes**
in your editor, on the way to commit. Zed shows the diff
naturally; for terminal use:

```
jj diff                          # working-copy diff (uncommitted)
jj diff -r @-                    # diff of the previous commit
jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>      # any two revisions
jj show -r <X>                   # description + diff for one rev
```

Pipe through `delta` or `diff-so-fancy | less -R` for color
and side-by-side. To launch your editor as the diff tool:

```
# ~/.config/jj/config.toml
[ui]
diff-editor = ["zed", "--diff", "$left", "$right"]
```

Then `jj diff --from X --to Y` opens it. Don't `jj edit
-r @-` to view a past commit — that marks it mutable and
shifts the working-copy pointer; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

[Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) below covers the
close-out squash recipe and recovery for ladders. Revset
primitives (`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) are in
[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).

## Pushing

**Push is discretionary** — done when you want to back up
work or publish interim progress, and **always at
close-out** (the cycle's result must be published). Interim
pushes are a judgment call; nothing forces push-per-commit.

**Post close-out review (close-out push only).** After
the close-out commit and before any `jj squash` / `jj
rebase` / `jj git push` invocation, stop and ask the user
which shape the cycle lands as (squash / merge / keep)
and to approve the push. The choice changes the commits
on the target and is hard to undo cleanly once pushed;
treat it like the Work and Commit Description reviews —
surface the options, wait for the user, don't auto-pick.
ESC-ESC and the deviation override apply as usual.

**Squash, merge, or keep at the close-out push.** Before
the publishing push, decide what shape the cycle lands as:

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its header
  becomes the close-out commit title), and every anchor
  back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed — real
  work, done as part of the squash.
- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
  sub-step commits stay reachable as the merge's second
  parent. `git log` shows everything; `git log
  --first-parent` shows main as one jump per cycle. Best
  when the sub-steps are worth preserving but main should
  read linearly. The paired `.claude` commit's body
  carries a multi-line ochid listing every app commit in
  the push — the multi-ochid case
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) designs.
  Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev> -d <sub-tip>`
  before invoking `jj git push`.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on main —
  when the decomposition is itself informative and you
  want `git log` to show every step. Each chores section
  keeps its own header / `Commits:` ref; no consolidation
  churn.

If squashing or merging, do it before invoking `vc-x1
push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
non-standard shapes).

The flow is wrapped by `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`, which runs:

1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` / `clippy`
   / `test`).
2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).

`<bookmark>` is the **app-side working branch** (often
`main`, sometimes a feature branch). `.claude` always pushes
its `main` regardless of the app-side bookmark. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for the full flag list, resumability
(`--from`, `--restart`, `--status`), and recovery; CLAUDE.md
keeps only the rules that govern bot behavior. `vc-x1 sync
--check` may also be run manually before editing to surface
remote changes early.

**`.claude` cadence — once per push.** The `.claude`
working-copy node accumulates session data across the
cycle's commits and is described + committed when a push
happens — one push, one `.claude` commit, paired with every
code commit in that push. Its change ID is stable from the
first code commit that references it through to that
`.claude` commit (working-copy snapshots, `jj describe`, and
the finalize `jj commit` all preserve the change ID), so the
`ochid:` trailers resolve across the push.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives on
`main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror app-side
branches** — risks the bot steering session pushes to the
wrong remote ref.

**Bot communication at the reviews.** Use plain prose —
no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE` and
  `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to commit (or
  push). Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
  --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"` invocation by
  default; it's mechanical and obvious from title/body.
  Show it only on explicit request.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief trade-off
  summary, plus the push target; wait for the user's
  choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` / `jj git
  push` invocation.

**After finalize: stop and wait.** After `vc-x1 finalize`
launches — whether mid-session per-push or at session-end —
you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a next step, edit files, run
tools, or emit any text until the user explicitly directs
you to continue. Treat finalize as a hard stop for the
whole turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
*before* executing finalize. The user controls cadence —
auto-proceeding bypasses the push+finalize checkpoint.

If push exits before `finalize-claude` (e.g. failure between
`push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run finalize by hand:

```
vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
```

Clear push's saved state after any out-of-band recovery —
`rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
--restart` — otherwise push resumes from a stale stage.

A late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
updating CLAUDE.md or memory) requires `jj squash
--ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a remote rewrite
and needs explicit approval like any push.

## ochid trailers

Every commit body carries `ochid:` trailer(s) linking it to
its counterpart(s) in the other repo. The path is
workspace-root-relative — `/.claude/<chid>` from the app
side, `/<chid>` from the `.claude` side.

Because `.claude` commits once per push:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
  working-copy change ID, stable until `.claude` is
  committed at push time.
- **The `.claude` commit** carries one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
  per code commit in that push: a single trailer when one
  code commit is pushed (the squashed case), a list when
  several are. The multi-line `ochid:` list and its
  fork / multi-user generalization are designed in
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs
(first line app, second `.claude`).

## Sub-cycle ladders

The local ladder of sub-cycle Work commits is typically
kept on its own jj `@` chain and **collapsed into the
sub-cycle's Close-out** before the parent cycle continues.
Ladder commits are scratch — they exist for review and
bisection during the sub-cycle and disappear into one
commit at sub-cycle close-out.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work on this `@`.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable.** Build and
   clippy alone are not enough — past experience
   (0.41.1-6.5) had a regression introduced in an early
   ladder commit that didn't surface until a later one ran
   the full suite, by which point bisection cost was higher
   than it needed to be.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — give it a working
   title and body. The title does **not** need a version
   suffix during the ladder; the sub-cycle Close-out collects
   every commit into one final commit carrying the
   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker. Ladder titles are scratch — they
   never reach the remote.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves while a ladder is active:

- `jj log -r 'all()' -R .` — see the whole ladder at a
  glance.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any commit in
  the ladder by chid prefix. Useful for bisection: re-run
  the failing test at each candidate without manual
  checkout juggling.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit's diff in
  isolation.

Example using a revset with the `::` operator from the last
commit pushed to the remote:

```
$ jj log -r lu::
○  pynknoqu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 33612c81
│  docs: cycle protocol pointer
@  xqpnxtyu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 f40c49a7
│  docs: cycle protocol formal protocol
○  lzqmlpvy wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 8c722f8c
│  docs: jj-revsets.md review fixes
○  wmmxkuoz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 11:28:46 65a6705f
│  chore: scratch — jj-revsets learning + scaffolding
◆  lulqxovr wink@saville.com 2026-04-27 22:27:23 main 55eadc8e
│  docs: capture 0.41.1 init+clone redesign plan (0.42.0-4)
~
```

Ladder work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected.
Use `jj edit -r <chid>` to switch to whichever ladder
commit you want to work on. Modifications to a selected
commit rewrite that commit in place; descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and `cargo test --bins`
is green on the latest:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Where `<base>` is the parent of the **first** ladder
commit — typically the bookmark or commit the ladder
branched from. The `-u` flag (use destination message)
tells jj to keep `@`'s description and discard the source
descriptions; the intermediate titles served their purpose,
and `vc-x1 push` will overwrite `@`'s description anyway.

After squash:

- All ladder diffs are folded into `@`.
- The intermediate commits become empty and are
  abandoned automatically.
- History is linear: `<base> → @`.

Then run `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` exactly as for any other
commit. Push's `commit-app` stage finalizes `@` with the
sub-cycle Close-out title/body and the proper `(X.Y.Z-N)`
version suffix.

For N = 1 the close-out squash is a no-op (`base..@-` is
empty when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit
directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong and you want to back out
without losing prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent. Earlier commits are untouched.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
  ladder work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
  above is.

# References

- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) — multi-ochid
  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.
- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-commit ladder usage. The
  per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
  after a regression introduced in an early commit wasn't
  caught until a later one ran the full suite.
