toolUseResult.content:
# Cycle protocol

## Cycles

A cycle has three phases:

- **Preparation** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's first commit.
  Sets up the cycle by bumping the version, picking up a
  `## Todo` item into `## In Progress`, and opening the
  chores section header. Operational steps:
  [Preparation](#preparation).
- **Work-N** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the work commits
  that implement the change. As many as the change needs;
  each runs through the [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
  Operational notes: [Work-N](#work-n).
- **Close-out** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's last commit.
  Bookkeeping only: moves `## In Progress` into the chores
  section, adds a `## Done` entry, optionally updates
  `notes/README.md`. Operational steps: [Close-out](#close-out).

A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
result must always be published at close-out. See
[Pushing](#pushing).

**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
for the local-ladder mechanics.

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
  [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).
  A cycle that merges several entangled `## Todo` items moves
  all of them. A `## Todo` entry that duplicates current
  `## In Progress` work is a process bug.
- **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits implement the change. Number as
many `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … as the change needs; each
runs through the [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
Interim pushes are optional (backup, progress visibility);
close-out is the only mandatory push — see
[Pushing](#pushing). For sub-cycles see
[Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
squash-language before the decision is made.

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
see [Pushing](#pushing).

## Numbering

The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.

Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.

## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
   work.
2. **Do the work.**
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
   the commit captures the completed state.
4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
   cycle mandatory at close-out):
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   working-copy changes in their editor. Most change
   requests come here — iterate until the work is right.
6. **Write the commit description.** [Conventional
   Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with a
   version suffix:

   ```
   <type>: <short description> (<version>)
   ```

   - **Title**: ≤50 chars. Common types: `feat`, `fix`,
     `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`. The version
     suffix counts toward the 50 — favor terse phrasings.
   - **Body**: [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro
     + bullets), wrap ≤72. App-repo bullets are
     **file-by-file** — one bullet per file changed, file
     plus a one-line gist. When a file has several distinct
     changes, list them as `-` sub-bullets under the file
     bullet.

     ```
     - path/to/file1
       - first distinct change
       - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
          with continuation indented 5
     ```

     The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
     cycle's mechanical change record; chores carries the
     narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
     "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###`
     subsection.
   - **`ochid:` trailer** as the last line of the body —
     see [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).
   - **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: same shape; bullets
     describe in-session activity rather than code changes.
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

**Two overrides apply:**

- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
  surface it; don't push through.
- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
  a review or question forward.

## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at **uncommitted working-copy changes**
in your editor, on the way to commit. Zed shows the diff
naturally; for terminal use:

```
jj diff                          # working-copy diff (uncommitted)
jj diff -r @-                    # diff of the previous commit
jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>      # any two revisions
jj show -r <X>                   # description + diff for one rev
```

Pipe through `delta` or `diff-so-fancy | less -R` for color
and side-by-side. To launch your editor as the diff tool:

```
# ~/.config/jj/config.toml
[ui]
diff-editor = ["zed", "--diff", "$left", "$right"]
```

Then `jj diff --from X --to Y` opens it. Don't `jj edit
-r @-` to view a past commit — that marks it mutable and
shifts the working-copy pointer; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

[Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) below covers the
close-out squash recipe and recovery for ladders. Revset
primitives (`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) are in
[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).

## Pushing

**Push is discretionary** — done when you want to back up
work or publish interim progress, and **always at
close-out** (the cycle's result must be published). Interim
pushes are a judgment call; nothing forces push-per-commit.

**Post close-out review (close-out push only).** After
the close-out commit and before any `jj squash` / `jj
rebase` / `jj git push` invocation, stop and ask the user
which shape the cycle lands as (squash / merge / keep)
and to approve the push. The choice changes the commits
on the target and is hard to undo cleanly once pushed;
treat it like the Work and Commit Description reviews —
surface the options, wait for the user, don't auto-pick.
ESC-ESC and the deviation override apply as usual.

**Squash, merge, or keep at the close-out push.** Before
the publishing push, decide what shape the cycle lands as:

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its header
  becomes the close-out commit title), and every anchor
  back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed — real
  work, done as part of the squash.
- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
  sub-step commits stay reachable as the merge's second
  parent. `git log` shows everything; `git log
  --first-parent` shows main as one jump per cycle. Best
  when the sub-steps are worth preserving but main should
  read linearly. The paired `.claude` commit's body
  carries a multi-line ochid listing every app commit in
  the push — the multi-ochid case
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) designs.
  Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev> -d <sub-tip>`
  before invoking `jj git push`.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on main —
  when the decomposition is itself informative and you
  want `git log` to show every step. Each chores section
  keeps its own header / `Commits:` ref; no consolidation
  churn.

If squashing or merging, do it before invoking `vc-x1
push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
non-standard shapes).

The flow is wrapped by `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`, which runs:

1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` / `clippy`
   / `test`).
2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).

`<bookmark>` is the **app-side working branch** (often
`main`, sometimes a feature branch). `.claude` always pushes
its `main` regardless of the app-side bookmark. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for the full flag list, resumability
(`--from`, `--restart`, `--status`), and recovery; CLAUDE.md
keeps only the rules that govern bot behavior. `vc-x1 sync
--check` may also be run manually before editing to surface
remote changes early.

**`.claude` cadence — once per push.** The `.claude`
working-copy node accumulates session data across the
cycle's commits and is described + committed when a push
happens — one push, one `.claude` commit, paired with every
code commit in that push. Its change ID is stable from the
first code commit that references it through to that
`.claude` commit (working-copy snapshots, `jj describe`, and
the finalize `jj commit` all preserve the change ID), so the
`ochid:` trailers resolve across the push.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives on
`main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror app-side
branches** — risks the bot steering session pushes to the
wrong remote ref.

**Bot communication at the reviews.** Use plain prose —
no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE` and
  `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to commit (or
  push). Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
  --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"` invocation by
  default; it's mechanical and obvious from title/body.
  Show it only on explicit request.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief trade-off
  summary, plus the push target; wait for the user's
  choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` / `jj git
  push` invocation.

**After finalize: stop and wait.** After `vc-x1 finalize`
launches — whether mid-session per-push or at session-end —
you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a next step, edit files, run
tools, or emit any text until the user explicitly directs
you to continue. Treat finalize as a hard stop for the
whole turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
*before* executing finalize. The user controls cadence —
auto-proceeding bypasses the push+finalize checkpoint.

If push exits before `finalize-claude` (e.g. failure between
`push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run finalize by hand:

```
vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
```

Clear push's saved state after any out-of-band recovery —
`rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
--restart` — otherwise push resumes from a stale stage.

A late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
updating CLAUDE.md or memory) requires `jj squash
--ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a remote rewrite
and needs explicit approval like any push.

## ochid trailers

Every commit body carries `ochid:` trailer(s) linking it to
its counterpart(s) in the other repo. The path is
workspace-root-relative — `/.claude/<chid>` from the app
side, `/<chid>` from the `.claude` side.

Because `.claude` commits once per push:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
  working-copy change ID, stable until `.claude` is
  committed at push time.
- **The `.claude` commit** carries one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
  per code commit in that push: a single trailer when one
  code commit is pushed (the squashed case), a list when
  several are. The multi-line `ochid:` list and its
  fork / multi-user generalization are designed in
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs
(first line app, second `.claude`).

## Sub-cycle ladders

The local ladder of sub-cycle Work commits is typically
kept on its own jj `@` chain and **collapsed into the
sub-cycle's Close-out** before the parent cycle continues.
Ladder commits are scratch — they exist for review and
bisection during the sub-cycle and disappear into one
commit at sub-cycle close-out.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work on this `@`.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable.** Build and
   clippy alone are not enough — past experience
   (0.41.1-6.5) had a regression introduced in an early
   ladder commit that didn't surface until a later one ran
   the full suite, by which point bisection cost was higher
   than it needed to be.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — give it a working
   title and body. The title does **not** need a version
   suffix during the ladder; the sub-cycle Close-out collects
   every commit into one final commit carrying the
   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker. Ladder titles are scratch — they
   never reach the remote.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves while a ladder is active:

- `jj log -r 'all()' -R .` — see the whole ladder at a
  glance.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any commit in
  the ladder by chid prefix. Useful for bisection: re-run
  the failing test at each candidate without manual
  checkout juggling.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit's diff in
  isolation.

Example using a revset with the `::` operator from the last
commit pushed to the remote:

```
$ jj log -r lu::
○  pynknoqu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 33612c81
│  docs: cycle protocol pointer
@  xqpnxtyu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 f40c49a7
│  docs: cycle protocol formal protocol
○  lzqmlpvy wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 8c722f8c
│  docs: jj-revsets.md review fixes
○  wmmxkuoz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 11:28:46 65a6705f
│  chore: scratch — jj-revsets learning + scaffolding
◆  lulqxovr wink@saville.com 2026-04-27 22:27:23 main 55eadc8e
│  docs: capture 0.41.1 init+clone redesign plan (0.42.0-4)
~
```

Ladder work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected.
Use `jj edit -r <chid>` to switch to whichever ladder
commit you want to work on. Modifications to a selected
commit rewrite that commit in place; descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and `cargo test --bins`
is green on the latest:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Where `<base>` is the parent of the **first** ladder
commit — typically the bookmark or commit the ladder
branched from. The `-u` flag (use destination message)
tells jj to keep `@`'s description and discard the source
descriptions; the intermediate titles served their purpose,
and `vc-x1 push` will overwrite `@`'s description anyway.

After squash:

- All ladder diffs are folded into `@`.
- The intermediate commits become empty and are
  abandoned automatically.
- History is linear: `<base> → @`.

Then run `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` exactly as for any other
commit. Push's `commit-app` stage finalizes `@` with the
sub-cycle Close-out title/body and the proper `(X.Y.Z-N)`
version suffix.

For N = 1 the close-out squash is a no-op (`base..@-` is
empty when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit
directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong and you want to back out
without losing prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent. Earlier commits are untouched.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
  ladder work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
  above is.

# References

- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) — multi-ochid
  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.
- 0.41.1-6.5 cycle — first multi-commit ladder usage. The
  per-commit `cargo test --bins` gate originated there
  after a regression introduced in an early commit wasn't
  caught until a later one ran the full suite.

message.content: In preperation you it says "opening the chores section" in both ## Cycles and ## Preparation, clarify for me what is meant by "open" and what "chores section" are we referring to?

attachment.snippet:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	## Cycles
4	
5	A cycle has three phases:
6	
7	- **Preparation** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's first commit.
8	  Sets up the cycle by bumping the version, picking up a
9	  `## Todo` item into `## In Progress`, and opening the
10	  chores section header. Operational steps:
11	  [Preparation](#preparation).
12	- **Work-N** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the work commits
13	  that implement the change. As many as the change needs;
14	  each runs through the [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
15	  Operational notes: [Work-N](#work-n).
16	- **Close-out** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's last commit.
17	  Bookkeeping only: moves `## In Progress` into the chores
18	  section, adds a `## Done` entry, optionally updates
19	  `notes/README.md`. Operational steps: [Close-out](#close-out).
20	
21	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
22	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
23	result must always be published at close-out. See
24	[Pushing](#pushing).
25	
26	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
27	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
28	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
29	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
30	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
31	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
32	for the local-ladder mechanics.
33	
34	## Preparation
35	
36	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
37	
38	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
39	  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
40	  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
41	  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
42	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
43	- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
44	  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
45	  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
46	  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
47	  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
48	  [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).
49	- **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
50	  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
51	  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
52	  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
53	  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
54	  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
55	  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.
56	
57	## Work-N
58	
59	The cycle's work commits implement the change. Number as
60	many `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … as the change needs; each
61	runs through the [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
62	Interim pushes are optional (backup, progress visibility);
63	close-out is the only mandatory push — see
64	[Pushing](#pushing). For sub-cycles see
65	[Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders).
66	
67	## Close-out
68	
69	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):
70	
71	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
72	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
73	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
74	  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
75	  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
76	  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
77	  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
78	  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
79	  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
80	  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
81	  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
82	  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
83	  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
84	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
85	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
86	
87	The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
88	in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
89	Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
90	— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
91	keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
92	review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
93	close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
94	squash-language before the decision is made.
95	
96	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
97	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
98	see [Pushing](#pushing).
99	
100	## Numbering
101	
102	The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
103	separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
104	the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):
105	
106	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
107	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
108	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
109	
110	A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
111	rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
112	into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
113	(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
114	`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
115	`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
116	so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
117	(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
118	adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.
119	
120	Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
121	`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
122	so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.
123	
124	## Per-commit flow
125	
126	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
127	through:
128	
129	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
130	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
131	   work.
132	2. **Do the work.**
133	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
134	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
135	   the commit captures the completed state.
136	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
137	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
138	   1. `cargo fmt`
139	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
140	   3. `cargo test`
141	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
142	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
143	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
144	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
145	   working-copy changes in their editor. Most change
146	   requests come here — iterate until the work is right.
147	6. **Write the commit description.** [Conventional
148	   Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with a
149	   version suffix:
150	
151	   ```
152	   <type>: <short description> (<version>)
153	   ```
154	
155	   - **Title**: ≤50 chars. Common types: `feat`, `fix`,
156	     `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`. The version
157	     suffix counts toward the 50 — favor terse phrasings.
158	   - **Body**: [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro
159	     + bullets), wrap ≤72. App-repo bullets are
160	     **file-by-file** — one bullet per file changed, file
161	     plus a one-line gist. When a file has several distinct
162	     changes, list them as `-` sub-bullets under the file
163	     bullet.
164	
165	     ```
166	     - path/to/file1
167	       - first distinct change
168	       - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
169	          with continuation indented 5
170	     ```
171	
172	     The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
173	     cycle's mechanical change record; chores carries the
174	     narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
175	     "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###`
176	     subsection.
177	   - **`ochid:` trailer** as the last line of the body —
178	     see [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).

content.text:
**"the chores section"** = the `##` section in the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` file that records this cycle. Each cycle gets exactly one (or rarely several — 0.55.0 had four) such section in that file. For 0.59.0, it's `## docs: cycle protocol updates (0.59.0)` at line 51 of `notes/chores/chores-12.md`.

**"open"** = write the `##` header line for this cycle into that file, with the anticipated close-out title. The section starts with just the header (and the `Commits:` line that gets backfilled in the *next* cycle's Preparation). Body content gets filled in at close-out — that's when the `## In Progress` problem statement + plan ladder move into the section per the "title only holds position" rule.

So the literal Preparation action is: append a new `## <type>: <desc> (X.Y.Z)` line to the current chores file. The section is "opened" because it now exists, but holds no narrative yet.

**Why this isn't fully spelled out in cycle-protocol.md**: the chores-file convention itself (what `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` is, the `Commits:` line, the no-edit-list rule, the commit-recording header rule) lives in CLAUDE.md's `## Pre-commit Requirements` — which we deliberately left in CLAUDE.md as a general writing convention rather than moving into the cycle protocol. So the protocol *uses* "chores section" as a term that the chores convention defines elsewhere.

If you want the protocol doc to be more self-contained on this, options:

- **Tightest**: change the `## Preparation` bullet to start with "**Open the chores section** (`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`)." Two more words, gives the reader the file path so they can find an example.
- **More**: add a cross-link to CLAUDE.md's chores convention so anyone unfamiliar can read the definition: `(see [Chores section content](../CLAUDE.md#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record))`. Spends a line.
- **Most**: relocate the chores convention into cycle-protocol.md too. Broader scope, more redundancy work in `-2` and elsewhere — probably not worth it now.

My lean: the "tightest" option — just add `(notes/chores/chores-NN.md)` to the bullet so the reader has a concrete handle. Want me to apply that?

message.content:
I think that phrase needs to be defined here, ATM this file is really referenced only by CLAUDE.md
but I'm pretty sure it is fundamental and will also be referred to by README.md. So phrases,
such as "opening the chores section", need to be defined locally or well understood.

content.text:
Agree. Here's what I'd add as a new `## Chores sections` immediately after `## Cycles` (before `## Preparation`), tight enough to be a quick definition:

```
## Chores sections

A **chores section** is a `##` section in
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle. The
header is the cycle's commit title (e.g. `## refactor:
foo bar (X.Y.Z)`); the body — prose intro + bullets +
optional `###` design subsections — fills in at close-out
from the `## In Progress` block. The first line under
the header is a `Commits:` line citing the recorded
commit(s) by file-local `[N]` ref, backfilled at the
*next* cycle's Preparation since the commit doesn't
exist yet when the section is written.

**"Open" the chores section** (Preparation) = append the
`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
with the anticipated close-out title. The body is empty;
content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).

Fuller chores conventions — content rules ("no edit
list; git is the record"), header sync to the final
commit title, design subsection pattern, `Commits:`
formatting — live in CLAUDE.md
[Pre-commit Requirements](../CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-requirements).
```

Then the `## Preparation` bullet stays the same since "open the chores section" is now defined just above.

That's a focused fix. But it raises a related question worth flagging: the chores convention currently lives in CLAUDE.md `## Pre-commit Requirements` (specifically `### Chores section content`, `### Chores commit references`, `### Headings and entries that record a commit`). Those three subsections are about cycle bookkeeping, not general writing — they're really cycle-protocol material. If you want cycle-protocol.md to be fully self-contained for README.md to reference, those probably want to move here too.

Two paths:

- **Now (Option A — tight)**: just add the `## Chores sections` definition above; cross-link to CLAUDE.md for the fuller rules. Cheap, addresses the local-definition need, leaves the split.
- **Now or later (Option B — fuller)**: also relocate the three chores-related subsections from CLAUDE.md `## Pre-commit Requirements` into cycle-protocol.md. CLAUDE.md keeps only general writing conventions (`### Notes references`, `### Markdown anchor links`). cycle-protocol.md becomes the single source for both cycle workflow AND chores convention.

My lean: Option A now (as part of `-1`'s relocate scope, since we're already there), Option B later — capture as a `## Ideas` or `## Todo` entry once Ideas exists in `-2`. Keeps `-1` from sprawling further while putting the eventual move on the radar.

Apply Option A?

message.content: A

content.text: Applying Option A — adding the `## Chores sections` definition between `## Cycles` and `## Preparation`.

message.content: Review my changes to the cycles three phases.

attachment.snippet:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	## Cycles
4	
5	A cycle has three phases:
6	
7	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's first commit.
8	  Sets up the cycle by:
9	  - bumping the version
10	  - Adding a item to `## In Progress` typically from `## Priorities
11	    list of `## Todo`.
12	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the commits
13	  that implement the change. As many as the change needs, see
14	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
15	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's last commit.
16	  Bookkeeping only: moves `## In Progress` into a [chores
17	  section)[#chores-sections], adds a `## Done` entry and optionally
18	  updates `notes/README.md`.
19	
20	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
21	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
22	result must always be published at close-out. See
23	[Pushing](#pushing).
24	
25	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
26	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
27	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
28	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
29	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
30	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
31	for the local-ladder mechanics.
32	
33	## Chores sections
34	
35	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
36	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle. The
37	header is the cycle's commit title (e.g. `## refactor:
38	foo bar (X.Y.Z)`); the body — prose intro + bullets +
39	optional `###` design subsections — fills in at close-out
40	from the `## In Progress` block. The first line under
41	the header is a `Commits:` line citing the recorded
42	commit(s) by file-local `[N]` ref, backfilled at the
43	*next* cycle's Preparation since the commit doesn't
44	exist yet when the section is written.
45	
46	**"Open" the chores section** (Preparation) = append the
47	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
48	with the anticipated close-out title. The body is empty;
49	content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).
50	
51	Fuller chores conventions — content rules ("no edit
52	list; git is the record"), header sync to the final
53	commit title, design subsection pattern, `Commits:`
54	formatting — live in CLAUDE.md
55	[Pre-commit Requirements](../CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-requirements).
56	
57	## Preparation
58	
59	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
60	
61	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
62	  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
63	  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
64	  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
65	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
66	- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
67	  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
68	  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
69	  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
70	  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
71	  [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).
72	- **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
73	  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
74	  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
75	  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
76	  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
77	  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
78	  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.
79	
80	## Work-N
81	
82	The cycle's work commits implement the change. Number as
83	many `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … as the change needs; each
84	runs through the [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
85	Interim pushes are optional (backup, progress visibility);
86	close-out is the only mandatory push — see
87	[Pushing](#pushing). For sub-cycles see
88	[Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders).
89	
90	## Close-out
91	
92	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):
93	
94	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
95	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
96	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
97	  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
98	  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
99	  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
100	  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
101	  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
102	  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
103	  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
104	  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
105	  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
106	  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
107	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
108	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
109	
110	The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
111	in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
112	Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
113	— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
114	keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
115	review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
116	close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
117	squash-language before the decision is made.
118	
119	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
120	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
121	see [Pushing](#pushing).
122	
123	## Numbering
124	
125	The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
126	separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
127	the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):
128	
129	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
130	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
131	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
132	
133	A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
134	rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
135	into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
136	(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
137	`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
138	`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
139	so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
140	(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
141	adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.
142	
143	Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
144	`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
145	so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.
146	
147	## Per-commit flow
148	
149	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
150	through:
151	
152	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
153	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
154	   work.
155	2. **Do the work.**
156	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
157	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
158	   the commit captures the completed state.
159	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
160	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
161	   1. `cargo fmt`
162	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
163	   3. `cargo test`
164	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
165	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
166	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
167	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
168	   working-copy changes in their editor. Most change
169	   requests come here — iterate until the work is right.
170	6. **Write the commit description.** [Conventional
171	   Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with a
172	   version suffix:
173	
174	   ```
175	   <type>: <short description> (<version>)
176	   ```
177	
178	   - **Title**: ≤50 chars. Common types: `feat`, `fix`,
179	     `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`. The version
180	     suffix counts toward the 50 — favor terse phrasings.
181	   - **Body**: [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro
182	     + bullets), wrap ≤72. App-repo bullets are
183	     **file-by-file** — one bullet per file changed, file

file.content:
# Cycle protocol

## Cycles

A cycle has three phases:

- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's first commit.
  Sets up the cycle by:
  - Backfill the previuos cycle's chores.
  - Bump the version.
  - Adding an item to `## In Progress` typically from `## Priorities
    list of `## Todo`.
  - Succinct probem statement.
  - Open the chores section.
- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the commits
  that implement the change. As many as the change needs, see
  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's last commit.
  - Move the current items title into `## Done`.
  - Move `In Progress` into a [chores section)[#chores-sections].
  - Optionally updates `notes/README.md`.

A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
either incrementally or as one batch at close-out
  - The result must always be published at close-out. See
[Pushing](#pushing).

**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
for the local-ladder mechanics.

## Chores sections

A **chores section** is a `##` section in
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle. The
header is the cycle's commit title (e.g. `## refactor:
foo bar (X.Y.Z)`); the body — prose intro + bullets +
optional `###` design subsections — fills in at close-out
from the `## In Progress` block. The first line under
the header is a `Commits:` line citing the recorded
commit(s) by file-local `[N]` ref, backfilled at the
*next* cycle's Preparation since the commit doesn't
exist yet when the section is written.

**"Open" the chores section** (Preparation) = append the
`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
with the anticipated close-out title. The body is empty;
content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).

Fuller chores conventions — content rules ("no edit
list; git is the record"), header sync to the final
commit title, design subsection pattern, `Commits:`
formatting — live in CLAUDE.md
[Pre-commit Requirements](../CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-requirements).

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
  [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).
- **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits implement the change. Number as
many `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … as the change needs; each
runs through the [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
Interim pushes are optional (backup, progress visibility);
close-out is the only mandatory push — see
[Pushing](#pushing). For sub-cycles see
[Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of

message.content: Review my changes to the three phases in ## Cycles, I've tried to match the information in corresponding sub-sections.

content.text:
Reviewed. Most of the substance is right — there are some concrete defects and one design-level question.

**Concrete defects to fix:**

1. Line 9: typo `previuos` → `previous`.
2. Line 11-12: `## Priorities` missing closing backtick; the line reads `'## Priorities\n    list of '## Todo'` with broken backticks — likely meant `from `## Priorities` (references into `## Todo`)`.
3. Line 13: typo `probem` → `problem`.
4. Line 20: broken markdown link `[chores section)[#chores-sections]` — uses `)` and `[` instead of `]` and `(`. Should be `[chores section](#chores-sections)`.
5. Line 19: `Move the current items title` — possessive `item's`, and "current item" is vague; the `## Close-out` subsection calls it "the picked-up item." Either name match.
6. Line 20: ``Move `In Progress` into a [chores section]`` — missing `##` prefix and code formatting (`` `## In Progress` ``). Also "a chores section" implies any; it's *the* one opened during Preparation.
7. Lines 24-26: indentation glitch — the "The result must always be published at close-out" line is a 2-space-indented `-` bullet hanging off a non-list paragraph. Either inline it (`incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the result must always be published at close-out`) or restructure as a real list.

**Dropped info — intentional or oversight?**

- Preparation bullet sub-list mentions `## In Progress` and "succinct problem statement" but drops the **plan ladder** (the third element of the In Progress entry per the subsection). If aiming for overview parity, that's missing.
- Close-out's `notes/README.md` bullet drops the `if functionality changed (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior)` qualifier — without it, "Optionally update `notes/README.md`" reads as if you might update it for any reason.

**Design-level observation:**

The detail level is now uneven across the three phases:

- **Preparation**: 5 sub-bullets (close to subsection parity)
- **Work-N**: 0 sub-bullets (one inline line)
- **Close-out**: 3 sub-bullets (close to subsection parity)

Two consistent shapes are reasonable:

- **(i) Terse overview** — single-line description per phase (like current Work-N); subsection holds operational detail. Tighter; subsection is the only place rules are stated; no drift risk.
- **(ii) Mini-summary** — sub-bullets recap subsection actions for at-a-glance scan (current Preparation/Close-out shape). More scannable; duplicates subsection content — needs both kept in sync when rules change.

You've picked (ii) for Preparation/Close-out and (i) for Work-N. Either shape works, but probably want to commit to one for all three. (i) is the cheaper option long-term; (ii) is better for a reader who wants to skim without scrolling.

Want me to apply the defect fixes, or do you want to make them yourself? On the design question — pick (i) or (ii) or leave mixed?

lastPrompt: Review my changes to the three phases in ## Cycles, I've tried to match the information in corresponding sub-sections.

message.content: go with (i), apply the fixes

Done. The three phase bullets now follow the terse-overview shape (i) consistently — each is one prose-form bullet per phase, no sub-bullets, defects fixed:

- **Preparation**: typos (previous/problem), backtick balance on `## Priorities`, and the missing `(typically via `## Priorities`)` framing for how Todos surface.
- **Work-N**: unchanged; already terse.
- **Close-out**: broken link `[chores section)[#chores-sections]` → `[chores section](#chores-sections)`; "current items" → "picked-up item" (matches the subsection); `In Progress` → `## In Progress`; "Optionally updates" → "optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality changed" (kept the conditional).
- **Publishing paragraph**: stray indented `-` bullet inlined into prose (semicolon-joined with the preceding clause).

File is 544 lines. Ready for further iteration or for the Work review prompt when you want to lock in `-1`.

- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the

  last commit. Bookkeeping only: move the picked-up item to
  `## Done`, move the `## In Progress` block into the
  [chores section](#chores-sections), and optionally update
  `notes/README.md` if functionality changed.

either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the

- **Preparation**: 4 sub-bullets (Backfill / Bump / Pick up / Open).
- **Work-N**: one-line inline — kept terse since it's genuinely one action ("each runs through per-commit flow").

Work-N is the asymmetric one. If you'd prefer it also sub-bulleted for visual symmetry, the natural three sub-bullets would be: per-commit flow / interim-push policy / sub-cycle pointer. Let me know.

input.old_string:
A **chores section** is a `##` section in
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle. The
header is the cycle's commit title (e.g. `## refactor:
foo bar (X.Y.Z)`); the body — prose intro + bullets +
optional `###` design subsections — fills in at close-out
from the `## In Progress` block. The first line under
the header is a `Commits:` line citing the recorded
commit(s) by file-local `[N]` ref, backfilled at the
*next* cycle's Preparation since the commit doesn't
exist yet when the section is written.

**"Open" the chores section** (Preparation) = append the
`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
with the anticipated close-out title. The body is empty;
content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).

attachment.snippet:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	## Cycles
4	
5	A cycle has three phases:
6	
7	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
8	  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
9	  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
10	  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
11	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically via `## Priorities`)
12	    into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
13	    statement + plan ladder).
14	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
15	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
16	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
17	  needs; each runs through the
18	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
19	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
20	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
21	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
22	    `## Done`.
23	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
24	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
25	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
26	    changed.
27	
28	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
29	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
30	result must always be published at close-out. See
31	[Pushing](#pushing).
32	
33	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
34	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
35	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
36	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
37	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
38	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
39	for the local-ladder mechanics.
40	
41	## Chores sections
42	
43	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
44	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle.
45	
46	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
47	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
48	with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
49	`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
50	content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).
51	
52	Fuller chores conventions live in CLAUDE.md
53	[Pre-commit Requirements](../CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-requirements).
54	
55	delete: Fuller chores conventions — content rules ("no edit
56	list; git is the record"), header sync to the final
57	commit title, design subsection pattern, `Commits:`
58	formatting — live in CLAUDE.md
59	[Pre-commit Requirements](../CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-requirements).
60	
61	## Preparation
62	
63	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
64	
65	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
66	  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
67	  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
68	  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
69	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
70	- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
71	  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
72	  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
73	  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
74	  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
75	  [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).
76	- **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
77	  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
78	  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
79	  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
80	  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
81	  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
82	  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.
83	
84	## Work-N
85	
86	The cycle's work commits implement the change. Number as
87	many `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … as the change needs; each
88	runs through the [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
89	Interim pushes are optional (backup, progress visibility);
90	close-out is the only mandatory push — see
91	[Pushing](#pushing). For sub-cycles see
92	[Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders).
93	
94	## Close-out
95	
96	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):
97	
98	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
99	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
100	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
101	  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
102	  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
103	  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
104	  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
105	  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
106	  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
107	  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
108	  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
109	  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
110	  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
111	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
112	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
113	
114	The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
115	in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
116	Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
117	— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
118	keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
119	review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
120	close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
121	squash-language before the decision is made.
122	
123	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
124	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
125	see [Pushing](#pushing).
126	
127	## Numbering
128	
129	The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
130	separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
131	the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):
132	
133	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
134	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
135	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
136	
137	A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
138	rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
139	into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
140	(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
141	`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
142	`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
143	so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
144	(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
145	adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.
146	
147	Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
148	`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
149	so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.
150	
151	## Per-commit flow
152	
153	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
154	through:
155	
156	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
157	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
158	   work.
159	2. **Do the work.**
160	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
161	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
162	   the commit captures the completed state.
163	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
164	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
165	   1. `cargo fmt`
166	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
167	   3. `cargo test`
168	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
169	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
170	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
171	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
172	   working-copy changes in their editor. Most change
173	   requests come here — iterate until the work is right.
174	6. **Write the commit description.** [Conventional
175	   Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with a
176	   version suffix:
177	
178	   ```
179	   <type>: <short description> (<version>)
180	   ```
181	
182	   - **Title**: ≤50 chars. Common types: `feat`, `fix`,
183	     `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`. The version
184	     suffix counts toward the 50 — favor terse phrasings.
185	   - **Body**: [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro
186	     + bullets), wrap ≤72. App-repo bullets are
187	     **file-by-file** — one bullet per file changed, file

35	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
36	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
37	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
38	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
39	for the local-ladder mechanics.
40	
41	## Chores sections
42	
43	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
44	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle.
45	
46	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
47	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
48	with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
49	`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
50	content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).
51	
52	Fuller chores conventions live in CLAUDE.md
53	(content rules, header sync, design subsection pattern,
54	commits formatting)
55	[Pre-commit Requirements](../CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-requirements).
56	
57	## Preparation
58	
59	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
60	
61	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores**, see
62	  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
63	
64	delete: - **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
65	  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
66	  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
67	  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
68	
69	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
70	
71	- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one).
72	  - Move that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
73	    Progress`
74	  - The **bold title line** is an exact match of the
75	    chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix)
76	  - **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
77	    [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).
78	
79	delete: - **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
80	  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
81	  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
82	  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
83	  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
84	  [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).
85	
86	- **Open the chores section.**
87	  - Header is the cycle's **anticipated close-out title**
88	    (e.g. `## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`).
89	  - No body — the cycle's live narrative lives in `## In Progress`
90	    and moves into the section at close-out
91	  - One chores section per cycle is the default
92	
93	delete: - **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
94	  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
95	  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
96	  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
97	  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
98	  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
99	  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.
100	
101	## Work-N
102	
103	The cycle's work commits implement the change. Number as
104	many `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … as the change needs; each
105	runs through the [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
106	Interim pushes are optional (backup, progress visibility);
107	close-out is the only mandatory push — see
108	[Pushing](#pushing). For sub-cycles see
109	[Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders).
110	
111	## Close-out
112	
113	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):
114	
115	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
116	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
117	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
118	  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
119	  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
120	  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
121	  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
122	  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
123	  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
124	  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
125	  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
126	  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
127	  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
128	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
129	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
130	
131	The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
132	in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
133	Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
134	— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
135	keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
136	review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
137	close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
138	squash-language before the decision is made.
139	
140	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
141	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
142	see [Pushing](#pushing).
143	
144	## Numbering
145	
146	The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
147	separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
148	the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):
149	
150	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
151	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
152	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
153	
154	A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
155	rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
156	into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
157	(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
158	`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
159	`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
160	so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
161	(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
162	adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.
163	
164	Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
165	`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
166	so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.
167	
168	## Per-commit flow
169	
170	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
171	through:
172	
173	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
174	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
175	   work.
176	2. **Do the work.**
177	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
178	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
179	   the commit captures the completed state.
180	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
181	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
182	   1. `cargo fmt`
183	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
184	   3. `cargo test`
185	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
186	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
187	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
188	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the

content.text:
Reviewed `## Preparation` changes. The new versions are tighter, but a few defects + design observations:

**Defects in the new versions:**

1. **Pick up Todo bullet** (lines 71-77):
   - Line 73: missing period after `Progress`.
   - Line 75: stray closing `)` with no opening paren — `"minus the `## ` prefix)"`.
   - Line 76 starts mid-sentence with `**succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**.` — reads as a fragment. Probably wants the three In Progress elements as three parallel sub-bullets:
     ```
     - **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one).
       Move into `## In Progress` as:
       - A **bold title line** — exact match of the chores
         section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
       - A **succinct problem statement**.
       - A **plan ladder**.

       See [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form).
     ```

2. **Open chores section bullet** (lines 86-91):
   - Missing periods after `close-out` (line 90) and `default` (line 91).

3. **Backfill bullet** (line 61): "previous cycle's chores" — drops "section" or "`Commits:` ref"; reads slightly vague. Suggest `"previous cycle's chores section `Commits:` ref"` for term consistency with `## Chores sections`.

**Design observation worth flagging:**

The new **Open the chores section** bullet (lines 86-91) restates content that's already in `## Chores sections` (header is the anticipated close-out title, body empty, etc.). Now that we have a dedicated definition section, the Preparation bullet could collapse to a pointer:

```
- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** — append a
  `##` header with the cycle's anticipated close-out title.
```

That's one line. The rule details (no body, content arrives at close-out, one per cycle default) live in `## Chores sections` and don't get re-stated here. Saves duplication.

**Also (carry-over from `## Chores sections`):** the parenthetical you added is misplaced — currently reads `live in CLAUDE.md (content rules, ...) [Pre-commit Requirements]`, which puts the parenthetical between the noun and the link awkwardly. Cleaner:

```
Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live
in CLAUDE.md
[Pre-commit Requirements](../CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-requirements).
```

Also `Commits:` should keep its backticks (it's the literal field name).

**Apply the deletes + my suggested fixes** (period fixes, stray `)`, flatter Pick-up sub-bullets, one-line Open pointer, chores parenthetical reorder)? Or just do the deletes and leave the new versions as-is for you to fix?

message.content: apply the deletes + your suggested fixes

file.content:
## Chores sections

A **chores section** is a `##` section in
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle.

The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).

Fuller chores conventions live in CLAUDE.md
(content rules, header sync, design subsection pattern,
commits formatting)
[Pre-commit Requirements](../CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-requirements).

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores**, see
  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).

delete: - **Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.**
  Grab the just-pushed commit's URL + full SHA, fill in the
  newest chores section that was left `Commits:`-less. See
  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).

- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.

- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one).
  - Move that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
    Progress`
  - The **bold title line** is an exact match of the
    chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix)
  - **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
    [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).

delete: - **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one). Move
  that entry's text out of `## Todo` and into `## In
  Progress` — a **bold title line** (exact match for the
  chores section header, minus the `## ` prefix) +
  **succinct problem statement** + **plan ladder**. See
  [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (Problem + plan shape).

- **Open the chores section.**
  - Header is the cycle's **anticipated close-out title**
    (e.g. `## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`).
  - No body — the cycle's live narrative lives in `## In Progress`
    and moves into the section at close-out
  - One chores section per cycle is the default

delete: - **Open the chores section.** Header = the cycle's
  **anticipated close-out title** (e.g. `## refactor: foo
  bar (X.Y.Z)`). No body — the cycle's live narrative
  lives in `## In Progress` and moves into the section at
  close-out per the "title only holds position" rule. One
  chores section per cycle is the default; per-commit
  sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice.

## Work-N

The cycle's work commits implement the change. Number as
many `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … as the change needs; each
runs through the [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
Interim pushes are optional (backup, progress visibility);
close-out is the only mandatory push — see
[Pushing](#pushing). For sub-cycles see
[Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders).

## Close-out

The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):

- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).

The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
squash-language before the decision is made.

Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
see [Pushing](#pushing).

## Numbering

content.content:
538 notes/cycle-protocol.md
---
## Chores sections

A **chores section** is a `##` section in
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle.

The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).

Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live
in CLAUDE.md
[Pre-commit Requirements](../CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-requirements).

## Preparation

The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):

- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
  `Commits:` ref** — see
  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one).
  Move into `## In Progress` as:
  - A **bold title line** — exact match of the chores
    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
  - A **succinct problem statement**.
  - A **plan ladder**.

  See [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form).
- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
  close-out title.

content.text:
Done. Work-N now matches the bulleted shape of Preparation and Close-out:

- Short intro line ending in `:`
- 3 bullets covering per-commit flow, interim push policy, sub-cycle subdivision

Bold emphasis is mixed-style on purpose: bullet 1 emphasizes the link target (per-commit flow is the key mechanism), bullet 2 leads with the topic (interim pushes — it's a policy not an action), bullet 3 leads with the action (subdivide). The Preparation/Close-out bullets do the same — `**Backfill ...**` and `**Bump ...**` are actions, while `**Open the [chores section]**` puts emphasis on the topic. So Work-N's mixing fits the pattern in this file.

File is 542 lines. Ready for the next iteration.
