attachment.snippet:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	## Cycles
4	
5	A cycle has three phases:
6	
7	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
8	  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
9	  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
10	  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
11	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically via `## Priorities`)
12	    into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
13	    statement + plan ladder).
14	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
15	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
16	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
17	  needs; each runs through the
18	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
19	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
20	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
21	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
22	    `## Done`.
23	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
24	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
25	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
26	    changed.
27	
28	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
29	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
30	result must always be published at close-out. See
31	[Pushing](#pushing).
32	
33	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
34	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
35	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
36	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
37	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
38	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
39	for the local-ladder mechanics.
40	
41	## Chores sections
42	
43	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
44	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle.
45	
46	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
47	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
48	with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
49	`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
50	content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).
51	
52	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
53	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live
54	in CLAUDE.md
55	[Pre-commit Requirements](../CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-requirements).
56	
57	## Preparation
58	
59	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
60	
61	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
62	  `Commits:` ref** — see
63	  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
64	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
65	- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one).
66	  Move into `## In Progress` as:
67	  - A **bold title line** — exact match of the chores
68	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
69	  - A **succinct problem statement**.
70	  - A **plan ladder**.
71	
72	  See [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form).
73	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
74	  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
75	  close-out title.
76	
77	## Work-N
78	
79	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
80	implement the change. As many as needed:
81	
82	- Each commit runs through the
83	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
84	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
85	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
86	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
87	  visibility)
88	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
89	  scope grows enough (see
90	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
91	
92	## Close-out
93	
94	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`):
95	
96	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
97	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
98	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the chores
99	  section.** Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
100	  `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the chores
101	  `##` header carries the same string) and paste them
102	  under the bare chores `##` header (the title-only
103	  placeholder from Preparation). Sync the chores header to
104	  the **final** commit title if the cycle's scope shifted,
105	  and update every markdown anchor back-reference to it.
106	  Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
107	  commits; `### Outcome` notes if useful. Replace
108	  `## In Progress` with `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
109	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
110	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
111	
112	The close-out commit's body should describe **only what's
113	in that commit** (the bookkeeping: clearing `## In
114	Progress`, the `## Done` entry, chores section finalize)
115	— not what would happen post-squash. Squash / merge /
116	keep is a separate push-time decision (Post close-out
117	review, see [Pushing](#pushing) below); keeping the
118	close-out body shape-neutral avoids locking in
119	squash-language before the decision is made.
120	
121	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
122	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
123	see [Pushing](#pushing).
124	
125	## Numbering
126	
127	The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
128	separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
129	the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):
130	
131	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
132	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
133	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
134	
135	A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
136	rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
137	into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
138	(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
139	`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
140	`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
141	so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
142	(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
143	adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.
144	
145	Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
146	`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
147	so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.
148	
149	## Per-commit flow
150	
151	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
152	through:
153	
154	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
155	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
156	   work.
157	2. **Do the work.**
158	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
159	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
160	   the commit captures the completed state.
161	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
162	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
163	   1. `cargo fmt`
164	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
165	   3. `cargo test`
166	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
167	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
168	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
169	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
170	   working-copy changes in their editor. Most change
171	   requests come here — iterate until the work is right.
172	6. **Write the commit description.** [Conventional
173	   Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with a
174	   version suffix:
175	
176	   ```
177	   <type>: <short description> (<version>)
178	   ```
179	
180	   - **Title**: ≤50 chars. Common types: `feat`, `fix`,
181	     `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`. The version
182	     suffix counts toward the 50 — favor terse phrasings.
183	   - **Body**: [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro
184	     + bullets), wrap ≤72. App-repo bullets are
185	     **file-by-file** — one bullet per file changed, file
186	     plus a one-line gist. When a file has several distinct
187	     changes, list them as `-` sub-bullets under the file
188	     bullet.
189	
190	     ```
191	     - path/to/file1
192	       - first distinct change
193	       - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
194	          with continuation indented 5
195	     ```
196	
197	     The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
198	     cycle's mechanical change record; chores carries the
199	     narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any

content.text:
Reviewed. Two small notes:

1. **Defect**: missing period at end of `(backup, progress visibility)` — line 87 ends with `)` instead of `).`
2. **Order observation**: the push-related bullets are now `close-out mandatory` → `interim optional`. Slightly more natural narrative order is `interim optional` → `close-out mandatory` (general permissive policy, then the exception). Not wrong as-is — just a small flow consideration.

Apply the period fix? Want the reorder too, or leave as-is?

message.content:
Nice, really readable!
Onto ## Numbering

content.text:
Here's what I'd do for `## Numbering`:

**Changes:**

1. Drop the semver parenthetical ("semver mandates `-` once at the start..." — rationale, not rule; the examples make the format unambiguous).
2. Promote "**trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**" into the intro line — it's a universal rule that applies at every nesting depth, not just within Nesting.
3. Restructure the dense Nesting paragraph into a parallel bullet list matching the basic-suffix bullets — shows the symmetry and is scannable.
4. Keep the "no Preparation" carve-out as a tight one-line paragraph after the Nesting bullets.
5. Tighten the Cargo.toml paragraph (drop the "Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**" preamble — the bullets above already establish that titles carry the suffix).

**Proposed result:**

```
## Numbering

The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase —
a **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**:

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:

- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle

Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.

Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
reports the active phase at build time.
```

That's 19 lines (current is 22). Apply, or want changes?

input.old_string:
## Numbering

The version suffix on each commit carries its phase, with one
separator — `.` — at every depth (semver mandates `-` once at
the start of the prerelease, then `.` for each level after):

- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)

A **trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**, and the
rule nests to any depth: when a Work commit needs subdividing
into its own sub-cycle, append another level — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
(Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle), `X.Y.Z-3.1` /
`X.Y.Z-3.2` (its Work), `X.Y.Z-3` (its Close-out),
`X.Y.Z-3.1.0` (Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle), and
so on. A sub-cycle needing no Preparation just omits the `.0`
(`-3.1`, `-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later
adds `-3.0` with no renumbering of siblings.

Each commit carries its phase version in its title **and**
`Cargo.toml` — bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase,
so `vc-x1 -V` shows the active phase at build time.

message.content: Let me ask you, are we losing information or making things somehow ambigious with this effort? This is primarilly for you, but making it readable for me is something I'd like.

content.text:
Honest assessment — this is genuinely useful to step back on.

**Yes, we're losing some information.** Most of it is rationale/history (Why numeric suffixes, Ordering caveat, 0.41.1-6.7 anchors, validation dates, worked examples). That's the cheap stuff to lose — it doesn't change what the rules are, only why they exist. I can rederive the rationale if needed; the rules themselves stayed.

**But a few losses do hit operational substance** that I'd want back:

1. **"Pick up a `## Todo` item that duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process bug"** — this was a defensive check that catches a real failure mode (picking up a Todo I'm already working on). Cheap one-liner; I'd restore it.
2. **"per-commit sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate choice"** — explicit permission for the per-commit chores carve-out. Without it, I'd default to "one per cycle is mandatory" instead of "default with documented alternative." Cheap to restore — one line in `## Chores sections`.
3. **`## Pushing > After finalize` second paragraph** — the "even when the next step seems obvious. Wait." emphasis was load-bearing *specifically* because of the temptation to auto-proceed. Consolidating to one paragraph weakened the emphasis on a rule I have to follow.
4. **`## Sub-cycle ladders > Worked example`** — a judgment call. Removed 40 lines; cost is that the first time I'm asked to drive a sub-cycle ladder, I have rules but no concrete walkthrough. A 5-10 line mini-example showing `jj new` × N → `jj squash --from "<base>..@-"` → push would preserve most of the value at 1/4 the cost.

**Ambiguity introduced:**

- "**trailing `0` identifier marks a Preparation**" — easy to misread as "ends in 0." Should be "the final identifier is `0`" or similar to disambiguate `-10` (Work) vs `-1.0` (Preparation). Worth a one-word fix.
- "Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0`" — but *when* does a sub-cycle not need Preparation? Not stated. A reader has to infer from context.
- `## Cycles` overview duplicates content with the dedicated sections. Real drift risk over time: when rules change in `## Preparation`, the overview sub-bullets need parallel updates or they go stale.

**Readability for you:** clear net win. Bullets scan faster than walls of prose; the file is now skimmable.

**Operational fitness for me:** net win, with the 3-4 restorations above. Without them, mostly net win but with a few specific failure modes more likely (Todo duplication, per-commit chores edge case, after-finalize auto-proceed temptation).

**Recommendation:** I'd restore items 1-3 (low-cost, real value) and write a tight worked example for item 4 (40 lines → 8 lines). Plus the "final identifier" disambiguation. Probably 15-20 lines of net additions to a now-538-line file. Want me to draft those restorations for review?

content.text:
Drafts for review — each labeled with location:

**1. Pick up Todo bullet** (`## Preparation`, append to the "See Prose form" continuation line):

> See [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form). A Todo that
> duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process
> bug.

**2. Chores sections** (`## Chores sections`, append to first paragraph):

> A **chores section** is a `##` section in
> `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle. One
> section per cycle is the default; per-commit sections
> (like 0.55.0's four) are a deliberate alternative.

**3. After finalize** (`## Pushing > **After finalize: stop and wait.**`, insert one bolded sentence after the "MUST NEVER ... continue" clause):

> ... until the user explicitly directs you to continue.
> **This holds even when the next step seems obvious —
> wait.** Treat finalize as a hard stop for the whole
> turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
> *before* executing finalize. ...

**4. Worked example** (`## Sub-cycle ladders`, new subsection after `### Recovery`):

```
### Worked example

A 3-commit ladder from `main`, then close-out squash:

```
jj new -R .                   # repeat per commit:
... edit files; cargo test --bins ...
jj describe -m "..." -R .

jj squash --from "main..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Result: linear `main → @` with all ladder diffs folded
into `@`; intermediate commits abandoned. Then
`vc-x1 push main …` per normal.
```

**5. Numbering disambiguation** (`## Numbering`, intro line):

> The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase —
> the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:

Change: `trailing `0` identifier` → `final identifier `0``. Now `-10` (Work #10) and `-1.0` (Preparation of `-1` sub-cycle) are unambiguous.

---

Total addition: ~14 lines net (1 + 1 + 1 + 11 for the example + 0-word fix in Numbering). Apply all five?

message.content: any issues with creating a new node then squashing after I review?

content.text:
No issues — that's exactly the sub-cycle ladder pattern we just documented. Both commits will be undescribed (the current `@` is your `-1` working copy, the new `@` will be the restoration draft), and `jj squash` defaults to squashing `@` into `@-`, which is what you want.

Three small notes:

1. **Diff review**: after I make the restoration edits on the new `@`, you can review just those changes with `jj diff` (working-copy diff vs. `@-`) — clean isolation from the other `-1` edits.
2. **No description churn**: neither commit has a description set, so squash won't ask which to keep. Result: `@-` accumulates the restoration diffs into the broader `-1` working copy; new empty `@` lands on top.
3. **Rollback if needed**: if you reject the restorations, `jj abandon @` drops the draft cleanly; the original `-1` working copy is untouched.

Want me to run `jj new -R .`, apply all five restorations there, and report back so you can `jj diff` them?

508	### Recovery
509	
510	If a ladder commit goes wrong and you want to back out
511	without losing prior commits:
512	
513	- **Discard the current commit's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
514	  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
515	  parent. Earlier commits are untouched.
516	- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
517	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
518	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
519	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
520	  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
521	  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
522	  ladder work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
523	  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
524	  above is.
525	
526	# References
527	
528	- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
529	  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
530	- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) — multi-ochid
531	  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
532	- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
533	  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
534	  for squash-recipe experiments.

file.content:
empty when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit
directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong and you want to back out
without losing prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent. Earlier commits are untouched.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
  ladder work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
  above is.

# References

- [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md) — revset primitives
  (chid/cid, `@`/`@-`/`@+`, `..`/`::` ranges, prefix matching).
- [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) — multi-ochid
  trailer design (fork / multi-user generalization).
- [`substep-test.sh`](substep-test.sh) — script that
  scaffolds a 4-revision ladder under `/tmp/substep-test`
  for squash-recipe experiments.

- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo

input.new_string:
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
  ladder work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
  above is.

### Worked example

A 3-commit ladder from `main`, then close-out squash:

```
jj new -R .                   # repeat per commit:
... edit files; cargo test --bins ...
jj describe -m "..." -R .

jj squash --from "main..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Result: linear `main → @` with all ladder diffs folded
into `@`; intermediate commits abandoned. Then
`vc-x1 push main …` per normal.

# References

content.text:
`## Per-commit flow` is the cycle's most-referenced section, and step 6 is a self-contained format spec buried inside a numbered list. My instinct is to **extract** rather than just tighten in place.

**Proposal: extract step 6 into a new `## Commit description` section** placed immediately after `## Per-commit flow`. Step 6 becomes:

```
6. **Write the commit description** — see
   [Commit description](#commit-description).
```

The new section absorbs the format spec, restructured with `###` subheadings (matching the `## Sub-cycle ladders` precedent):

```
## Commit description

[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)
with a version suffix:

```
<type>: <short description> (<version>)
```

### Title

- ≤50 chars total (version suffix counts).
- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
  `docs`, `chore`.
- Favor terse phrasings.

### Body

[Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:

- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
  files with multiple distinct changes:

  ```
  - path/to/file1
    - first distinct change
    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
       with continuation indented 5
  ```

  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###`
  subsection.

- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
  in-session activity rather than code changes.

### Trailer

`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
[ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).
```

**Other tightening in `## Per-commit flow`:**

- Step 8's shell example (the `jj commit -m ... -R .` template) stays — it's a useful "how to invoke" reference. But could move to `## Commit description` as the assembled example if you prefer the per-commit-flow numbered list to be purely declarative.

**Tradeoff:**

- Per-commit-flow becomes a clean 8-step process, uniformly scannable.
- `## Commit description` is independently navigable (README.md or chores can link to it).
- Net file size ~unchanged (step 6 shrinks from ~25 lines to 2; new section is ~30 lines).
- Cost: one more section heading; reader following per-commit-flow has to follow a link to see the format.

Want extraction (with or without moving step 8's shell example)? Or prefer in-place tightening of step 6?

Same `jj new -R .` → edit → review → squash flow if you want it on a separate node.

file.content:
`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.

Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
reports the active phase at build time.

## Per-commit flow

Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
through:

1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`, before any code/doc
   work.
2. **Do the work.**
3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit, so
   the commit captures the completed state.
4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
   cycle mandatory at close-out):
   1. `cargo fmt`
   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
   3. `cargo test`
   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
   working-copy changes in their editor. Most change
   requests come here — iterate until the work is right.
6. **Write the commit description.** [Conventional
   Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) with a
   version suffix:

   ```
   <type>: <short description> (<version>)
   ```

   - **Title**: ≤50 chars. Common types: `feat`, `fix`,
     `refactor`, `test`, `docs`, `chore`. The version
     suffix counts toward the 50 — favor terse phrasings.
   - **Body**: [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro
     + bullets), wrap ≤72. App-repo bullets are
     **file-by-file** — one bullet per file changed, file
     plus a one-line gist. When a file has several distinct
     changes, list them as `-` sub-bullets under the file
     bullet.

     ```
     - path/to/file1
       - first distinct change
       - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
          with continuation indented 5
     ```

     The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
     cycle's mechanical change record; chores carries the
     narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
     "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###`
     subsection.
   - **`ochid:` trailer** as the last line of the body —
     see [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).
   - **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: same shape; bullets
     describe in-session activity rather than code changes.
7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
   keeps the verb visible):

   ```
   jj commit -m \
   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
   -m "<intro paragraph>

   - file1: gist
   - file2: gist

   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
   -R .
   ```

## Commit description

[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)
with a version suffix:

```
<type>: <short description> (<version>)
```

### Title

- ≤50 chars total (version suffix counts).
- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
  `docs`, `chore`.
- Favor terse phrasings.

### Body

[Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),
wrap ≤72. Bullet content differs per repo:

- **App-repo body**: file-by-file. One bullet per file
  changed (file plus a one-line gist). Sub-bullets for
  files with multiple distinct changes:

  ```
  - path/to/file1
    - first distinct change
    - second distinct change, wrapping to next line
       with continuation indented 5
  ```

  The file-by-file list is the source of truth for the
  cycle's mechanical change record. Chores carries the
  narrative + design, not a copy of it. Promote any
  "why" beyond one sentence to a chores `###` subsection.

- **Session-repo (`.claude`) body**: bullets describe
  in-session activity rather than code changes.

### Trailer

`ochid:` as the last line of the body — see
[ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers).

file.content:
## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at **uncommitted working-copy changes**
in your editor, on the way to commit. Zed shows the diff
naturally; for terminal use:

```
jj diff                          # working-copy diff (uncommitted)
jj diff -r @-                    # diff of the previous commit
jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>      # any two revisions
jj show -r <X>                   # description + diff for one rev
```

Pipe through `delta` or `diff-so-fancy | less -R` for color
and side-by-side. To launch your editor as the diff tool:

```
# ~/.config/jj/config.toml
[ui]
diff-editor = ["zed", "--diff", "$left", "$right"]
```

Then `jj diff --from X --to Y` opens it. Don't `jj edit
-r @-` to view a past commit — that marks it mutable and
shifts the working-copy pointer; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

[Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) below covers the
close-out squash recipe and recovery for ladders. Revset
primitives (`@`, `@-`, `..`, `::`, prefix matching) are in
[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).

## Pushing

**Push is discretionary** — done when you want to back up

content.text:
Looking at `## Reviewing changes` (~26 lines). It's a mix of workflow note + jj reference + Zed-specific editor config.

**Proposed tightening:**

- **Drop the Zed editor config block** (~5 lines: the `~/.config/jj/config.toml` `diff-editor` setup). That's one-time user setup, not cycle protocol. Belongs in a developer-setup doc if anywhere.
- **Drop the "Pipe through `delta` or `diff-so-fancy`" tip** — tooling preference, not protocol.
- **Drop "Zed shows the diff naturally; for terminal use:"** — Zed-specific framing.
- **Convert the jj commands from a code block to bullets** — matches the file's prevailing format; each command + description on its own line.
- **Keep the `jj edit -r @-` warning** — real footgun, operationally important.
- **Keep the cross-refs paragraph** at the end.

**Proposed result:**

```
## Reviewing changes

Work review looks at the **uncommitted working-copy diff**,
on the way to commit. Common commands:

- `jj diff` — working-copy diff (uncommitted)
- `jj diff -r @-` — diff of the previous commit
- `jj diff --from <X> --to <Y>` — any two revisions
- `jj show -r <X>` — description + diff for one rev

Don't `jj edit -r @-` to view a past commit — that marks
it mutable and shifts `@`; use `jj diff -r @-` or
`jj show -r @-`.

See [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders) for the
close-out squash recipe and recovery; revset primitives
are in [`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md).
```

That's ~15 lines (was ~26).

**Tradeoff to flag:** dropping the Zed/delta tips means a new contributor setting up jj won't find that guidance here. My take: that belongs in a setup doc (or jj's own docs), not in the cycle protocol — protocol describes what to do, not how to make your tools pretty.

Apply on a new node? Or do you want to keep more (e.g., the `delta` tip)?

268	## Pushing
269	
270	**Push is discretionary** — done when you want to back up
271	work or publish interim progress, and **always at
272	close-out** (the cycle's result must be published). Interim
273	pushes are a judgment call; nothing forces push-per-commit.
274	
275	**Post close-out review (close-out push only).** After
276	the close-out commit and before any `jj squash` / `jj
277	rebase` / `jj git push` invocation, stop and ask the user
278	which shape the cycle lands as (squash / merge / keep)
279	and to approve the push. The choice changes the commits
280	on the target and is hard to undo cleanly once pushed;
281	treat it like the Work and Commit Description reviews —
282	surface the options, wait for the user, don't auto-pick.
283	ESC-ESC and the deviation override apply as usual.
284	
285	**Squash, merge, or keep at the close-out push.** Before
286	the publishing push, decide what shape the cycle lands as:
287	
288	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
289	  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
290	  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
291	  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
292	  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its header
293	  becomes the close-out commit title), and every anchor
294	  back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed — real
295	  work, done as part of the squash.
296	- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
297	  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
298	  sub-step commits stay reachable as the merge's second
299	  parent. `git log` shows everything; `git log
300	  --first-parent` shows main as one jump per cycle. Best
301	  when the sub-steps are worth preserving but main should
302	  read linearly. The paired `.claude` commit's body
303	  carries a multi-line ochid listing every app commit in
304	  the push — the multi-ochid case
305	  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) designs.
306	  Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev> -d <sub-tip>`
307	  before invoking `jj git push`.
308	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on main —
309	  when the decomposition is itself informative and you
310	  want `git log` to show every step. Each chores section
311	  keeps its own header / `Commits:` ref; no consolidation
312	  churn.
313	
314	If squashing or merging, do it before invoking `vc-x1
315	push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
316	non-standard shapes).
317	
318	The flow is wrapped by `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`, which runs:
319	
320	1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` / `clippy`
321	   / `test`).
322	2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
323	   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
324	3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
325	   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
326	4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
327	   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
328	   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).
329	
330	`<bookmark>` is the **app-side working branch** (often
331	`main`, sometimes a feature branch). `.claude` always pushes
332	its `main` regardless of the app-side bookmark. See
333	`vc-x1 push --help` for the full flag list, resumability
334	(`--from`, `--restart`, `--status`), and recovery; CLAUDE.md
335	keeps only the rules that govern bot behavior. `vc-x1 sync
336	--check` may also be run manually before editing to surface
337	remote changes early.
338	
339	**`.claude` cadence — once per push.** The `.claude`
340	working-copy node accumulates session data across the
341	cycle's commits and is described + committed when a push
342	happens — one push, one `.claude` commit, paired with every
343	code commit in that push. Its change ID is stable from the
344	first code commit that references it through to that
345	`.claude` commit (working-copy snapshots, `jj describe`, and
346	the finalize `jj commit` all preserve the change ID), so the
347	`ochid:` trailers resolve across the push.
348	
349	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives on
350	`main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
351	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror app-side
352	branches** — risks the bot steering session pushes to the
353	wrong remote ref.
354	
355	**Bot communication at the reviews.** Use plain prose —
356	no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N", etc.):
357	
358	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
359	  "Work complete. Please review."
360	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE` and
361	  `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to commit (or
362	  push). Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
363	  --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"` invocation by
364	  default; it's mechanical and obvious from title/body.
365	  Show it only on explicit request.
366	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
367	  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief trade-off
368	  summary, plus the push target; wait for the user's
369	  choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` / `jj git
370	  push` invocation.
371	
372	**After finalize: stop and wait.** After `vc-x1 finalize`
373	launches — whether mid-session per-push or at session-end —
374	you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a next step, edit files, run
375	tools, or emit any text until the user explicitly directs
376	you to continue. **This holds even when the next step
377	seems obvious — wait.** Treat finalize as a hard stop for
378	the whole turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
379	*before* executing finalize. The user controls cadence —
380	auto-proceeding bypasses the push+finalize checkpoint.
381	
382	If push exits before `finalize-claude` (e.g. failure between
383	`push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run finalize by hand:
384	
385	```
386	vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
387	```
388	
389	Clear push's saved state after any out-of-band recovery —
390	`rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
391	--restart` — otherwise push resumes from a stale stage.
392	
393	A late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
394	updating CLAUDE.md or memory) requires `jj squash
395	--ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a remote rewrite
396	and needs explicit approval like any push.
397

## Pushing

**Push is discretionary** — done when you want to back up
work or publish interim progress, and **always at
close-out** (the cycle's result must be published). Interim
pushes are a judgment call; nothing forces push-per-commit.

**Post close-out review (close-out push only).** After
the close-out commit and before any `jj squash` / `jj
rebase` / `jj git push` invocation, stop and ask the user
which shape the cycle lands as (squash / merge / keep)
and to approve the push. The choice changes the commits
on the target and is hard to undo cleanly once pushed;
treat it like the Work and Commit Description reviews —
surface the options, wait for the user, don't auto-pick.
ESC-ESC and the deviation override apply as usual.

**Squash, merge, or keep at the close-out push.** Before
the publishing push, decide what shape the cycle lands as:

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its header
  becomes the close-out commit title), and every anchor
  back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed — real
  work, done as part of the squash.
- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
  sub-step commits stay reachable as the merge's second
  parent. `git log` shows everything; `git log
  --first-parent` shows main as one jump per cycle. Best
  when the sub-steps are worth preserving but main should
  read linearly. The paired `.claude` commit's body
  carries a multi-line ochid listing every app commit in
  the push — the multi-ochid case
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) designs.
  Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev> -d <sub-tip>`
  before invoking `jj git push`.
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on main —
  when the decomposition is itself informative and you
  want `git log` to show every step. Each chores section
  keeps its own header / `Commits:` ref; no consolidation
  churn.

If squashing or merging, do it before invoking `vc-x1
push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
non-standard shapes).

The flow is wrapped by `vc-x1 push <bookmark>`, which runs:

1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` / `clippy`
   / `test`).
2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).

`<bookmark>` is the **app-side working branch** (often
`main`, sometimes a feature branch). `.claude` always pushes
its `main` regardless of the app-side bookmark. See
`vc-x1 push --help` for the full flag list, resumability
(`--from`, `--restart`, `--status`), and recovery; CLAUDE.md
keeps only the rules that govern bot behavior. `vc-x1 sync
--check` may also be run manually before editing to surface
remote changes early.

**`.claude` cadence — once per push.** The `.claude`
working-copy node accumulates session data across the
cycle's commits and is described + committed when a push
happens — one push, one `.claude` commit, paired with every
code commit in that push. Its change ID is stable from the
first code commit that references it through to that
`.claude` commit (working-copy snapshots, `jj describe`, and
the finalize `jj commit` all preserve the change ID), so the
`ochid:` trailers resolve across the push.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives on
`main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror app-side
branches** — risks the bot steering session pushes to the
wrong remote ref.

**Bot communication at the reviews.** Use plain prose —
no insider jargon ("Gate N signal", "Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE` and
  `$BODY` explicitly, then ask permission to commit (or
  push). Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
  --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"` invocation by
  default; it's mechanical and obvious from title/body.
  Show it only on explicit request.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief trade-off
  summary, plus the push target; wait for the user's
  choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` / `jj git
  push` invocation.

**After finalize: stop and wait.** After `vc-x1 finalize`
launches — whether mid-session per-push or at session-end —
you **MUST NEVER** proceed to a next step, edit files, run
tools, or emit any text until the user explicitly directs
you to continue. **This holds even when the next step
seems obvious — wait.** Treat finalize as a hard stop for
the whole turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
*before* executing finalize. The user controls cadence —
auto-proceeding bypasses the push+finalize checkpoint.

If push exits before `finalize-claude` (e.g. failure between
`push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run finalize by hand:

```
vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
```

Clear push's saved state after any out-of-band recovery —
`rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
--restart` — otherwise push resumes from a stale stage.

A late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded (e.g.
updating CLAUDE.md or memory) requires `jj squash
--ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a remote rewrite
and needs explicit approval like any push.

## ochid trailers

content.text:
`## Pushing` is the file's biggest section (~130 lines) and a mix of three things: protocol rules, `vc-x1 push` tool reference, and recovery recipes. Mostly wall-of-prose with bold leads acting as informal subsection headers.

**Main proposal: subdivide with `###` headings + tighten prose.**

Subsections:

- `### Policy` — discretionary during cycle, mandatory at close-out (3 lines).
- `### Shape at close-out push` — the squash / merge / keep decision + post-close-out review + the "if squashing/merging, do it before `vc-x1 push`" note (~25 lines, cleaned up).
- `### vc-x1 push wrapper` — the 4-step internals + `<bookmark>` semantics + pointer to `--help` (~12 lines).
- `### .claude cadence` — once-per-push + linear-journal + bookmark admonition (~12 lines).
- `### Bot communication at the reviews` — the three review-style notes (~12 lines).
- `### After finalize: stop and wait` — the hard-stop rule (~8 lines).
- `### Recovery` — finalize-by-hand recipe + clear saved state + late-tweak rule (~12 lines).

**Tightening within each:**

- The "Post close-out review" bold block folds into `### Shape at close-out push` as an opening paragraph (it's about how to handle the shape decision, so it belongs there).
- The Merge bullet has some verbose prose ("Best when... read linearly. The paired `.claude` commit's body carries a multi-line ochid...") — the multi-ochid pointer to `forks-multi-user.md` is useful but the rest can compress.
- The Cargo.toml + `vc-x1 push --help` references stay; jj/CLAUDE history references trim.

Expected end size: ~110 lines (current 130). Subdivision is the bigger win — the section becomes navigable instead of one long scroll.

**Alternative I considered and rejected for this pass:** extract the `vc-x1 push` tool details (4-step internals, recovery recipes) into a separate `## vc-x1 push` section or a sibling `notes/vc-x1-push.md`. That's ~30 lines of tool-reference content. Reason against: the tool implements the protocol, and co-location makes both more readable. If you'd like the split anyway, it's a clean follow-up.

Apply the subdivision + tightening on the current `@` (`kmqrmtmr` is already empty post-squash, ready to take the edits)?

## Pushing

### Policy

Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
the cycle's result must be published.

### Shape at close-out push

Before the publishing push, decide the shape and get
user approval. The choice changes commits on the target
and is hard to undo; treat like Work / Commit
Description reviews — surface the options, wait for the
user, don't auto-pick.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its
  header becomes the close-out commit title); every
  anchor back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed.
- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit

  cycle commits stay reachable as the merge's second
  parent. `git log --first-parent` shows main as one jump
  per cycle. Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev>
  -d <sub-tip>` before pushing. The paired `.claude`
  commit carries a multi-line ochid (see
  [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers)).
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

If squashing or merging, do it before invoking
`vc-x1 push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
non-standard shapes).

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps:

1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` /
   `clippy` / `test`).
2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for

3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`

`<bookmark>` is the app-side working branch (often
`main`, sometimes a feature branch); `.claude` always

for the full flag list, resumability (`--from`,
`--restart`, `--status`), and recovery. Run `vc-x1 sync
--check` manually before editing to surface remote
changes early.

### .claude cadence

The `.claude` working-copy node accumulates session data
across the cycle's commits and is described + committed
when a push happens — **one push, one `.claude` commit**,
paired with every code commit in that push. Its change
ID is stable from the first code commit that references
it through to that `.claude` commit (working-copy

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit (or

- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief
  trade-off summary plus the push target; wait for the
  user's choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` /
  `jj git push` invocation.

### After finalize: stop and wait

After `vc-x1 finalize` launches — whether mid-session
per-push or at session-end — you **MUST NEVER** proceed
to a next step, edit files, run tools, or emit any text
until the user explicitly directs you to continue.
**This holds even when the next step seems obvious —
wait.** Treat finalize as a hard stop for the whole
turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
*before* executing finalize. The user controls cadence —
auto-proceeding bypasses the push+finalize checkpoint.

### Recovery

- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
  finalize by hand:

  ```
  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
  ```

- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
  stale stage.
- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
  (e.g. updating CLAUDE.md or memory) requires `jj
  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
  push.

## Pushing

### Policy

Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
the cycle's result must be published.

### Shape at close-out push

Before the publishing push, decide the shape and get
user approval. The choice changes commits on the target
and is hard to undo; treat like Work / Commit
Description reviews — surface the options, wait for the
user, don't auto-pick.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its
  header becomes the close-out commit title); every
  anchor back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed.
- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
  cycle commits stay reachable as the merge's second
  parent. `git log --first-parent` shows main as one jump
  per cycle. Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev>
  -d <sub-tip>` before pushing. The paired `.claude`
  commit carries a multi-line ochid (see
  [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers)).
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

If squashing or merging, do it before invoking
`vc-x1 push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
non-standard shapes).

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps:

1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` /
   `clippy` / `test`).
2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).

`<bookmark>` is the app-side working branch (often
`main`, sometimes a feature branch); `.claude` always
pushes its `main` regardless. See `vc-x1 push --help`
for the full flag list, resumability (`--from`,
`--restart`, `--status`), and recovery. Run `vc-x1 sync
--check` manually before editing to surface remote
changes early.

### .claude cadence

The `.claude` working-copy node accumulates session data
across the cycle's commits and is described + committed
when a push happens — **one push, one `.claude` commit**,
paired with every code commit in that push. Its change
ID is stable from the first code commit that references
it through to that `.claude` commit (working-copy
snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize `jj commit`
all preserve it), so the `ochid:` trailers resolve
across the push.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
pushes to the wrong remote ref.

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit (or
  push). Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
  --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"` invocation by
  default; it's mechanical and obvious from title/body.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief
  trade-off summary plus the push target; wait for the
  user's choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` /
  `jj git push` invocation.

### After finalize: stop and wait

After `vc-x1 finalize` launches — whether mid-session
per-push or at session-end — you **MUST NEVER** proceed
to a next step, edit files, run tools, or emit any text
until the user explicitly directs you to continue.
**This holds even when the next step seems obvious —
wait.** Treat finalize as a hard stop for the whole
turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
*before* executing finalize. The user controls cadence —
auto-proceeding bypasses the push+finalize checkpoint.

- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.

  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a

content.text:
Restructured. Same line count (+96/-96) but `## Pushing` is now seven `###` subsections instead of one wall of prose:

- `### Policy` (3 lines)
- `### Shape at close-out push` (~30 lines — squash/merge/keep bullets + pre-push setup)
- `### vc-x1 push wrapper` (~20 lines)
- `### .claude cadence` (~16 lines)
- `### Bot communication at the reviews` (~16 lines)
- `### After finalize: stop and wait` (~10 lines)
- `### Recovery` (~18 lines)

Notable in-place tightenings beyond the subdivision:

- "Post close-out review" bold block folded into `### Shape at close-out push` as the opening paragraph (it was always about that decision).
- Merge bullet's redundant "best when... read linearly" + multi-line ochid description compressed to a cross-link at `## ochid trailers`.
- Dropped "ESC-ESC and the deviation override apply as usual" (already in `## Per-commit flow`'s footer).
- Cleaned up the wrapper paragraph's CLAUDE.md self-reference.

`jj diff -R .` to review; `jj squash -R .` when ready.

message.content: LG squash, but one Q this incremental change technique, loop(change + squash), for a major refactor is working well. Thoughts on where we should capture it, maybe "## Per-commit flow" as a sub-range in "2. **Do the work.**" or have "**Do the work.**" reference a "## Do the work" section?

content.text:
Good question — yes, this loop technique deserves a documented home. My lean is **a new tight `## Iterative work` section**, placed right before `## Sub-cycle ladders` (so the two `jj @`-manipulation topics group together), with a tiny cross-link from step 2 of `## Per-commit flow` so it's discoverable from the flow.

**Why a separate section rather than inline in step 2:**

- Step 2 currently reads as one line ("Do the work."). Inlining the loop instructions would make step 2 disproportionately long for what's conceptually one step of the flow.
- The technique is reusable — chores-section design iteration, doc rewrites, code refactors — not specific to a phase. A standalone section is more discoverable for any of those.
- Sub-cycle ladders uses the same jj primitives (`jj new` + `jj squash`) but for a different scope (multi-commit). Cross-linking the two makes the relationship explicit.

**Why not fold into `## Sub-cycle ladders` as a `### Single-commit iteration` subsection:**

- Conceptually distinct scope: sub-cycles have their own `X.Y.Z-N.M` numbering; in-commit iteration doesn't change the version suffix. Mixing them in one section risks confusing the two.
- Easier to find as `## Iterative work` than nested under Sub-cycle ladders.

**Proposed content (~13 lines):**

```
## Iterative work

When work for a single commit benefits from incremental
review, loop:

1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target
   commit.
2. Make the next round of changes.
3. Review with `jj diff -R .` (just this round).
4. `jj squash -R .` to fold into the target; jj
   auto-creates a new empty `@` for the next round.

Same jj mechanics as a
[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
single-commit scope — the cycle's version suffix
doesn't change.
```

And the step 2 tweak:

```
2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
   for the loop-and-squash technique).
```

**Plus this cycle should squash the current `## Pushing` restructure in first** — it's still on the working node. Then a fresh node for the `## Iterative work` addition.

Approve the design + placement? If yes, I'll squash Pushing, then jj new for Iterative work.

message.content: LG, but I use zed and/or vscode (vscode has a better markdown renderer, handles references well) and not jj, but that info is good just pointing it out and we might mention it.

content.text: Good catch — your editor-based review flow is the reality; the `jj diff` commands are bot/terminal fallback. I'll mention it in `## Reviewing changes` and frame the new `## Iterative work` step 3 to defer there. Squashing Pushing first, then making the additions on a new node.

When work for a single commit benefits from incremental
review, loop:

2. Make the next round of changes.

4. `jj squash -R .` to fold into the target; jj
   auto-creates a new empty `@` for the next round.

single-commit scope — the cycle's version suffix
doesn't change.

file.content:
## ochid trailers

Every commit body carries `ochid:` trailer(s) linking it to
its counterpart(s) in the other repo. The path is
workspace-root-relative — `/.claude/<chid>` from the app
side, `/<chid>` from the `.claude` side.

Because `.claude` commits once per push:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
  working-copy change ID, stable until `.claude` is
  committed at push time.
- **The `.claude` commit** carries one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
  per code commit in that push: a single trailer when one
  code commit is pushed (the squashed case), a list when
  several are. The multi-line `ochid:` list and its
  fork / multi-user generalization are designed in
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs
(first line app, second `.claude`).

## Iterative work

lastPrompt: onto ## ochid trailers

content.text:
`## ochid trailers` is already pretty tight (22 lines) — the wins here are modest. Two small things I'd touch:

1. **Merge the path definition into the intro line** — currently two sentences; can be one.
2. **Reframe "Because `.claude` commits once per push:" → "Asymmetric because `.claude` commits once per push:"** — makes the *purpose* of the lead-in (explaining why the two bullets are different shapes) explicit.
3. **Tighten the `.claude` bullet's trailing multi-ochid sentence** — same content, fewer words.

**Proposed result (~16 lines, was 22):**

```
## ochid trailers

Each commit body carries `ochid:` trailer(s) —
workspace-root-relative paths linking it to its
counterpart(s) in the other repo. Asymmetric because
`.claude` commits once per push:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
  working-copy change ID, stable until `.claude` is
  committed at push time.
- **The `.claude` commit** carries one
  `ochid: /<code-chid>` per code commit in that push: a
  single trailer for one code commit (the squashed
  case), or a multi-line list for several (see
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md)).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change
IDs (first line app, second `.claude`).
```

**Note**: this section doesn't yet codify the **ochid invariant** ("every public ochid resolves in the public graph") or the bot-side rules (never squash, descriptions editable) — those are scoped to `-2` per the current plan. So this pass keeps `## ochid trailers` describing only the existing trailer mechanics; the invariant rules land in `-2` along with the cross-repo / God-repo design.

content.content:
270	## Pushing
271	
272	### Policy
273	
274	Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
275	progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
276	the cycle's result must be published.
277	
278	### Shape at close-out push
279	
280	Before the publishing push, decide the shape and get
281	user approval. The choice changes commits on the target
282	and is hard to undo; treat like Work / Commit
283	Description reviews — surface the options, wait for the
284	user, don't auto-pick.
285	
286	- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
287	  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
288	  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
289	  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
290	  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its
291	  header becomes the close-out commit title); every
292	  anchor back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed.
293	- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
294	  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
295	  cycle commits stay reachable as the merge's second
296	  parent. `git log --first-parent` shows main as one jump
297	  per cycle. Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev>
298	  -d <sub-tip>` before pushing. The paired `.claude`
299	  commit carries a multi-line ochid (see
300	  [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers)).
301	- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
302	  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
303	  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
304	  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.
305	
306	If squashing or merging, do it before invoking
307	`vc-x1 push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
308	non-standard shapes).
309	
310	### vc-x1 push wrapper
311	
312	`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps:
313	
314	1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` /
315	   `clippy` / `test`).
316	2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
317	   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
318	3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
319	   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
320	4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
321	   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
322	   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).
323	
324	`<bookmark>` is the app-side working branch (often
325	`main`, sometimes a feature branch); `.claude` always
326	pushes its `main` regardless. See `vc-x1 push --help`
327	for the full flag list, resumability (`--from`,
328	`--restart`, `--status`), and recovery. Run `vc-x1 sync
329	--check` manually before editing to surface remote
330	changes early.
331	
332	### .claude cadence
333	
334	The `.claude` working-copy node accumulates session data
335	across the cycle's commits and is described + committed
336	when a push happens — **one push, one `.claude` commit**,
337	paired with every code commit in that push. Its change
338	ID is stable from the first code commit that references
339	it through to that `.claude` commit (working-copy
340	snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize `jj commit`
341	all preserve it), so the `ochid:` trailers resolve
342	across the push.
343	
344	`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
345	on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
346	create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
347	app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
348	pushes to the wrong remote ref.
349	
350	### Bot communication at the reviews
351	
352	Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
353	"Checkpoint N", etc.):
354	
355	- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
356	  "Work complete. Please review."
357	- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
358	  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit (or
359	  push). Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
360	  --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"` invocation by
361	  default; it's mechanical and obvious from title/body.
362	- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
363	  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief
364	  trade-off summary plus the push target; wait for the
365	  user's choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` /
366	  `jj git push` invocation.
367	
368	### After finalize: stop and wait
369	
370	After `vc-x1 finalize` launches — whether mid-session
371	per-push or at session-end — you **MUST NEVER** proceed
372	to a next step, edit files, run tools, or emit any text
373	until the user explicitly directs you to continue.
374	**This holds even when the next step seems obvious —
375	wait.** Treat finalize as a hard stop for the whole
376	turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
377	*before* executing finalize. The user controls cadence —
378	auto-proceeding bypasses the push+finalize checkpoint.
379	
380	### Recovery
381	
382	- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
383	  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
384	  finalize by hand:
385	
386	  ```
387	  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
388	  ```
389	
390	- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
391	  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
392	  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
393	  stale stage.
394	- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
395	  (e.g. updating CLAUDE.md or memory) requires `jj
396	  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
397	  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
398	  push.
399

file.content:
## Pushing

### Policy

Push is **discretionary** during the cycle (backup,
progress visibility) and **mandatory at close-out** —
the cycle's result must be published.

### Shape at close-out push

Before the publishing push, decide the shape and get
user approval. The choice changes commits on the target
and is hard to undo; treat like Work / Commit
Description reviews — surface the options, wait for the
user, don't auto-pick.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its
  header becomes the close-out commit title); every
  anchor back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed.
- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
  cycle commits stay reachable as the merge's second
  parent. `git log --first-parent` shows main as one jump
  per cycle. Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev>
  -d <sub-tip>` before pushing. The paired `.claude`
  commit carries a multi-line ochid (see
  [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers)).
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

If squashing or merging, do it before invoking
`vc-x1 push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
non-standard shapes).

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps:

1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` /
   `clippy` / `test`).
2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).

`<bookmark>` is the app-side working branch (often
`main`, sometimes a feature branch); `.claude` always
pushes its `main` regardless. See `vc-x1 push --help`
for the full flag list, resumability (`--from`,
`--restart`, `--status`), and recovery. Run `vc-x1 sync
--check` manually before editing to surface remote
changes early.

### .claude cadence

The `.claude` working-copy node accumulates session data
across the cycle's commits and is described + committed
when a push happens — **one push, one `.claude` commit**,
paired with every code commit in that push. Its change
ID is stable from the first code commit that references
it through to that `.claude` commit (working-copy
snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize `jj commit`
all preserve it), so the `ochid:` trailers resolve
across the push.

`.claude` is a linear journal — all session work lives
on `main`, regardless of the app-side bookmark. **Do not
create or maintain `.claude` bookmarks that mirror
app-side branches** — risks the bot steering session
pushes to the wrong remote ref.

### Bot communication at the reviews

Use plain prose — no insider jargon ("Gate N signal",
"Checkpoint N", etc.):

- **At Work review** — summarize what changed and stop.
  "Work complete. Please review."
- **At Commit Description review** — present `$TITLE`
  and `$BODY` explicitly; ask permission to commit (or
  push). Don't spell out the full `vc-x1 push <bookmark>
  --yes --title "$TITLE" --body "$BODY"` invocation by
  default; it's mechanical and obvious from title/body.
- **At Post close-out review** — surface the shape
  options (squash / merge / keep) with a brief
  trade-off summary plus the push target; wait for the
  user's choice before any `jj squash` / `jj rebase` /
  `jj git push` invocation.

### After finalize: stop and wait

After `vc-x1 finalize` launches — whether mid-session
per-push or at session-end — you **MUST NEVER** proceed
to a next step, edit files, run tools, or emit any text
until the user explicitly directs you to continue.
**This holds even when the next step seems obvious —
wait.** Treat finalize as a hard stop for the whole
turn; any final words go in the approval prompt
*before* executing finalize. The user controls cadence —
auto-proceeding bypasses the push+finalize checkpoint.

### Recovery

- **If push exits before `finalize-claude`** (e.g.
  failure between `push-app` and `finalize-claude`), run
  finalize by hand:

  ```
  vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push <bookmark> --delay 10 --detach --log /tmp/vc-x1-finalize.log
  ```

- **Clear push's saved state** after any out-of-band
  recovery — `rm .vc-x1/push-state.toml` or `vc-x1 push
  <bookmark> --restart` — otherwise push resumes from a
  stale stage.
- **Late code-repo tweak after `push-app` succeeded**
  (e.g. updating CLAUDE.md or memory) requires `jj
  squash --ignore-immutable` and a re-push; that is a
  remote rewrite and needs explicit approval like any
  push.

content.text:
Yes, agreed. Where I see prose bloat in `## Pushing`:

1. **`### Shape at close-out push` intro paragraph** (5 lines → 3): "Decide the shape and get user approval before pushing — the choice is hard to undo. Surface the options, wait for the user." Drops the "treat like Work / Commit Description reviews" cross-reference (the rule is just "surface and wait" — same as any review).

2. **Squash bullet** (7 lines → 5): "single entry on the target" already implies per-commit granularity loss; "every" / "into one" implied. Drop the redundancies.

3. **Merge bullet** (8 lines → 6): "with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip" is implied by "merge commit"; "as the merge's second parent" → "via the second parent"; minor squeezes.

4. **Trailing "If squashing or merging" paragraph** (3 lines → 2): reframe as "Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use `jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes."

5. **`### .claude cadence` first paragraph** (9 lines → 4-5): the cadence rule and the chid-stability mechanism are entangled. Split into a one-line rule + a short mechanism sentence:

   > **Cadence**: one push = one `.claude` commit, paired with every code commit in that push.
   >
   > The `.claude` working copy accumulates session data across the cycle; its change ID stays stable across snapshots, `jj describe`, and the finalize commit, so code-side `ochid:` trailers resolve.

6. **`### Bot communication > Commit Description review` bullet** (5 lines → 3-4): drop "it's mechanical and obvious from title/body" (redundant); abbreviate the example invocation.

7. **`### Bot communication > Post close-out review` bullet** (5 lines → 4): drop "with a brief trade-off summary plus the push target" (the bot can decide format).

8. **`### After finalize`** (10 lines → 7): tighten parenthetical "whether mid-session per-push or at session-end" → "(mid-session per-push or at session-end)"; consolidate the closing "controls cadence — auto-proceeding bypasses" into one sentence.

Net expected: ~130 lines → ~110 lines. About 20 lines of pure prose compression.

Apply on a new node? Or want to narrow the scope first (e.g., just the heavy hitters — Shape bullets + .claude cadence + After finalize)?

input.old_string:
### Shape at close-out push

Before the publishing push, decide the shape and get
user approval. The choice changes commits on the target
and is hard to undo; treat like Work / Commit
Description reviews — surface the options, wait for the
user, don't auto-pick.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target;
  per-commit granularity preserved only in the commit
  body's edit list. Right when the work is one logical
  change with intermediate validation points. Cost: the
  per-commit chores sections collapse into one (its
  header becomes the close-out commit title); every
  anchor back-ref to a now-gone section gets re-pointed.
- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains one merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`) with parents `<prev>` and the cycle tip; the
  cycle commits stay reachable as the merge's second
  parent. `git log --first-parent` shows main as one jump
  per cycle. Set up with `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev>
  -d <sub-tip>` before pushing. The paired `.claude`
  commit carries a multi-line ochid (see
  [ochid trailers](#ochid-trailers)).
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

If squashing or merging, do it before invoking
`vc-x1 push` (or push manually with `jj git push` for
non-standard shapes).

Decide the shape and get user approval before pushing —
the choice is hard to undo once on the target. Surface
the options, wait for the user.

- **Squash to one commit** — single entry on the target.
  Right for one logical change with intermediate
  validation points. Cost: per-commit chores sections
  collapse (header → close-out title); anchor back-refs
  to gone sections get re-pointed.
- **Merge (non-FF)** — `main` gains a merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via the second
  parent. `git log --first-parent` shows main as one
  jump per cycle. Set up: `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev>
  -d <sub-tip>` before pushing. Paired `.claude` commit
  carries a multi-line [ochid trailer](#ochid-trailers).
- **Keep separate** — one commit per cycle entry on
  `main`. Use when the decomposition itself is
  informative. Each chores section keeps its own header /
  `Commits:` ref; no consolidation churn.

Set up squash/merge before invoking `vc-x1 push`; use
`jj git push` directly for non-standard shapes.

### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps:

1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` /
   `clippy` / `test`).
2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.

content.text:
Read it. Three things to surface:

1. **Defect**: "straight forward" → "straightforward" (one word).

2. **The "when to use" criterion shifted** — and the shift gives different answers:
   - My version: "Right for one logical change with intermediate validation points."
   - Yours: "Right for straightforward changes where the Work-N is focused on one or two files."

   These aren't the same lens. Yours uses *size* (file count) as the proxy; mine used *conceptual unity* (one logical change). For this cycle (0.59.0), my criterion says "squash candidate" (the original plan called it that) since it's one logical change — even though it touches 6-7 files. Your criterion says "not a squash candidate" since it's multi-file.

   Is the criterion change intentional? If the file-count proxy is what you actually want as the decision rule, that's a real protocol change — worth noting that the original "first squash candidate" framing for this very cycle would no longer apply under the new criterion.

3. **The "Cost:" sentence dropped** — the squash bullet no longer says what the reader is agreeing to (chores sections collapse; anchor back-refs to gone sections get re-pointed). That was load-bearing info: it's the cleanup work that "decide squash" commits the user to. Without it, the reader sees only "single entry on the target" + when-to-use, but nothing about consequences.

   Is the cost info meant to live elsewhere now, or did it get cut as noise?

Quick fixes either way:
- Typo: trivial.
- (2) and (3) are substantive — your call whether to revise the criterion + restore some cost wording, or keep the simpler bullet as-is and accept the criterion shift / dropped cost.

And with our "trapezoidal" Merge it's less likely we'll be doing many "Squash
to one commits". My thoughts.

Past cycles drifting from squash-candidate to merge is real evidence the simpler proxy is more honest than the conceptual "one logical change" framing.

Applying the typo fix now.

```
- **Merge non-ff** — `main` gains a merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via the second
  parent. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. Set up: `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev>
  -d <sub-tip>` before pushing.
```

5 lines vs current 6. Changes:

1. **Rename**: `Merge (non-FF)` → `Merge non-ff` (your suggestion, no parens).
2. **Drops `git log --first-parent shows main as one jump per cycle`** — git-user reference. The project is jj-first; `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` is the canonical inspection move.
3. **Drops the `.claude` multi-line trailer cross-link** — the `## ochid trailers` section already covers multi-line trailers (with the `forks-multi-user.md` link); duplicating here is noise.
4. **Adds `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` with the word "trapezoidal"** — your term for the shape, useful visual handle.
5. **Keeps the `jj rebase -r <tip> -d <prev> -d <sub-tip>` setup** — load-bearing operational reference for how to create the merge before pushing.

**One alternative to consider**: the rename could go further. "Merge" alone, or "Trapezoidal merge" (your own coinage, evocative). The "non-ff" qualifier disambiguates from `git merge --ff-only`, but if the protocol only ever does non-FF, the qualifier is implicit. Could be:

- **Merge** — minimalist
- **Merge non-ff** — your proposal
- **Trapezoidal merge** — picks up your vivid word

Any of those. My lean: **Merge non-ff** (your call) for technical precision; **Trapezoidal merge** if you want to lean into the visual.

Apply with **Merge non-ff**, or pick another name?
