content.content:
Move revisions to different parent(s)

This command moves revisions to different parent(s) while preserving the
changes (diff) in the revisions.

There are three different ways of specifying which revisions to rebase:

* `--source/-s` to rebase a revision and its descendants
* `--branch/-b` to rebase a whole branch, relative to the destination
* `--revisions/-r` to rebase the specified revisions without their
  descendants

If no option is specified, it defaults to `-b @`.

There are three different ways of specifying where the revisions should be
rebased to:

* `--onto/-o` to rebase the revisions onto the specified targets
* `--insert-after/-A` to rebase the revisions onto the specified targets and
  to rebase the targets' descendants onto the rebased revisions
* `--insert-before/-B` to rebase the revisions onto the specified targets'
  parents and to rebase the targets and their descendants onto the rebased
  revisions

See the sections below for details about the different ways of specifying
which revisions to rebase where.

If a working-copy revision gets abandoned, it will be given a new, empty
revision. This is true in general; it is not specific to this command.

### Specifying which revisions to rebase

With `--source/-s`, the command rebases the specified revision and its
descendants to the destination. For example, `jj rebase -s M -o O` would
transform your history like this (letters followed by an apostrophe are
post-rebase versions):

```text
O           N'
|           |
| N         M'
| |         |
| M         O
| |    =>   |
| | L       | L
| |/        | |
| K         | K
|/          |/
J           J
```

content.text:
Confirmed via `jj rebase --help`: the canonical flag is `--onto/-o` now. `-d` still works (silent — no deprecation warning), but it's not in the help output. So switching to `--onto` is right.

**Why the command works (mechanism):**

- `-r <tip>` selects the cycle's close-out commit (the `X.Y.Z`) as the revision to rewrite.
- **Multiple `--onto` flags give the rewritten commit multiple parents** — i.e., they construct a merge.
- First `--onto <prev>` becomes the first parent → the main-line ancestry.
- Second `--onto <sub-tip>` becomes the second parent → the cycle's content chain stays reachable.

So `<tip>` morphs from a normal end-of-chain commit into a merge commit pointing at both `<prev>` and `<sub-tip>`. `main` advances to `<tip>`; the chain `<prev>+1 ... <sub-tip>` is preserved via the second-parent traversal. That's the trapezoidal shape.

**Proposed bullet** (~6 lines):

```
- **Merge non-ff** — `main` gains a merge commit
  (`X.Y.Z`); cycle commits stay reachable via the second
  parent. `jj log -r ..@ -n <N>` shows the trapezoidal
  shape. Set up: `jj rebase -r <tip> --onto <prev>
  --onto <sub-tip>` (two `--onto` → `<tip>` becomes a
  merge of `<prev>` + `<sub-tip>`).
```

input.old_string:
### vc-x1 push wrapper

`vc-x1 push <bookmark>` wraps:

1. Preflight (`vc-x1 sync --check` + `cargo fmt` /
   `clippy` / `test`).
2. **Work-review prompt** — shows `jj diff --stat` for
   both repos and asks `[y/N]`.
3. **Commit-description-review prompt** — uses `--title`
   / `--body` or opens `$EDITOR`.
4. Commits both repos with ochid trailers, advances the
   bookmark, `jj git push --bookmark <bookmark>` for the
   app, finalizes `.claude` (its single per-push commit).

`<bookmark>` is the app-side working branch (often
`main`, sometimes a feature branch); `.claude` always
pushes its `main` regardless. See `vc-x1 push --help`
for the full flag list, resumability (`--from`,
`--restart`, `--status`), and recovery. Run `vc-x1 sync
--check` manually before editing to surface remote
changes early.

- Symmetric squash (planned, to be captured in `-2`).
- Per-repo bookmark names (planned).

content.text:
All four edits applied on the current `@`. Diff summary will show on `jj diff`.

**My one clarifying question on `<sub-tip> == <prev>`:**

I want to make sure I understand — in my model:

- `<prev>` = the previous cycle's close-out commit (where `main` was BEFORE this cycle started).
- `<sub-tip>` = the current cycle's content tip (the last Work commit, parent of `<tip>`).
- `<tip>` = this cycle's close-out commit (the bare `X.Y.Z`).

So for *this* cycle (0.59.0):
- `<prev>` = `qzpynrkk a199d062 (0.58.0)` (previous close-out, where main is now).
- `<sub-tip>` = `knvppzwq` (current `-1` work commit, the eventual content tip).
- `<tip>` = the not-yet-created `0.59.0` close-out commit.

In that model `<sub-tip>` and `<prev>` are different commits (separated by 3+ cycle commits). I can't find a typical case where they'd be equal — even a minimal cycle (`-0` then close-out) has `<sub-tip>` = the preparation commit and `<prev>` = previous close-out, which are distinct.

Did you mean something different by "sub-tip == prev in the typical case"? Possibilities I considered but couldn't reconcile:

- (a) A zero-work-commit cycle? Even then `<sub-tip>` = preparation ≠ `<prev>`.
- (b) Some specific shape I haven't seen?
- (c) The variable names overlap meaning in some way I'm missing?

If it turns out you meant something else and `<prev>` defined in the bullet is wrong, easy fix.

Both new items are nice-to-haves, not urgent:
- **Per-repo bookmark names** — pure ergonomic improvement; not blocking anything.
- **Trapezoidal-merge diagram** — docs polish.

Neither warrants P1/P2/P3 placement. Updating `-2`'s plan to route both to `todo-backlog.md`.

content.content:
# Todo Backlog

 Non-prioritized `## Todo` entries — the long tail. When an
 entry becomes a priority, move it (and any refs it cites)
 to `notes/todo.md > ## Todo` and add a tier line under the
 right `### Pn` in `notes/todo.md > ## Priorities`.

 Same formatting rules as `notes/todo.md > ## Todo` — see
 `notes/README.md > ## Todo format`. Run
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo-backlog.md` to
 renumber.

## Todo

1. **vc-x1 push: `--merge` flag (close-out shape).** Teach
   push to set up the non-FF merge ("trapezoid") shape
   itself instead of requiring the user to pre-rebase
   before invoking push. Sibling to `--squash` (#23);
   both are close-out shape choices made at push time.
   Dogfooded manually in `0.56.0`. [[1]]
   - Preparation (`-0`): open chores section; settle flag
     surface (`--merge` mutually exclusive with `--squash`
     / `--keep`?) and preflight checks (cycle tip is
     descendant of `<prev>`, not already a merge, sub-tip
     reachable from current bookmark, etc.).
   - Work-1 (`-1`): parse the flag through `PushArgs` →
     `PushParams`; reject mutually-exclusive combinations.
   - Work-2 (`-2`): implement the rebase setup inside
     push's state machine — locate `<tip>` and
     `<sub-tip>`, run the equivalent of `jj rebase -r
     <tip> -d <prev> -d <sub-tip>`; ensure the `.claude`
     commit emits a multi-line `ochid:` covering every
     sub-step in the merge.
   - Work-3 (`-3`): tests + dogfood on a real cycle.
   - Close-out: finalize chores; `## Done` entry; CLAUDE.md
     `### Pushing` text updated to describe the flag.
2. **Investigate `linkme` for subcommand registration.**
   Distributed-slice registry — each subcommand registers itself
   at link time; `main.rs` discovers them via the slice rather
   than matching a `Commands` enum. Reduces per-subcommand
   touchpoints from 3 (mod decl + enum variant + match arm) to 1
   (registration). Costs: loses compile-time exhaustiveness
   (missing registration = runtime gap); help-output ordering
   depends on link order unless sorted; macro-magic dependency.
   Revisit once the `0.50.0` trait sweep's per-arm cost has been
   felt under real "add a subcommand" load.
   <https://github.com/dtolnay/linkme>
3. **Investigate `inventory` as `linkme` alternative.** Same
   shape as `linkme` — runtime-iterable registry populated by
   `inventory::submit!` per subcommand. Trade-offs mirror

file.content:
## ochid trailers

Every commit body carries `ochid:` trailer(s) linking it to
its counterpart(s) in the other repo. The path is
workspace-root-relative — `/.claude/<chid>` from the app
side, `/<chid>` from the `.claude` side.

Because `.claude` commits once per push:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
  working-copy change ID, stable until `.claude` is
  committed at push time.
- **The `.claude` commit** carries one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
  per code commit in that push: a single trailer when one
  code commit is pushed (the squashed case), a list when
  several are. The multi-line `ochid:` list and its
  fork / multi-user generalization are designed in
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs
(first line app, second `.claude`).

## Iterative work

content.text:
Re-proposing what I sketched earlier for `## ochid trailers` (we got sidetracked to Pushing before applying):

```
## ochid trailers

Each commit body carries `ochid:` trailer(s) —
workspace-root-relative paths linking it to its
counterpart(s) in the other repo. Asymmetric because
`.claude` commits once per push:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
  working-copy change ID, stable until `.claude` is
  committed at push time.
- **The `.claude` commit** carries one
  `ochid: /<code-chid>` per code commit in that push: a
  single trailer for one code commit (the squashed
  case), or a multi-line list for several (see
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md)).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change
IDs (first line app, second `.claude`).
```

~17 lines vs current 22.

Changes:
- **Merge path definition into intro** — current two sentences become one (the path-explanation collapses into a dash-clause).
- **"Asymmetric because"** instead of "Because" — makes the *purpose* of the lead-in explicit (the bullets are different shapes because of the once-per-push cadence).
- **Tighten the `.claude` bullet's trailing multi-ochid sentence** — same content, fewer words.

Note: ochid invariant + bot-side rules + squash-gating are still scoped to `-2` (per the In Progress plan), so this pass keeps the section descriptive of mechanics only.

Apply on the current `@`?

  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change IDs
(first line app, second `.claude`).

workspace-root-relative paths linking it to its

IDs (first line app, second `.claude`).

message.content: I don't understand "Asymmetric because .claude commits once per push", this is a choice based on which Shape we use on the close-out and in the Keep separate it's 1:1.

content.text:
You're right — that framing was wrong. The asymmetry isn't a property of `.claude` always; it depends on the shape. In Keep separate (1 code commit per push) and Squash (1 squashed commit per push), it's 1:1 — no asymmetry. Only Merge non-ff close-out batches multiple code commits per push, producing the multi-line ochid case.

**Revised framing** — drop the "Asymmetric because" lead-in entirely; state the rules directly:

```
## ochid trailers

Each commit body carries `ochid:` trailer(s) —
workspace-root-relative paths linking it to its
counterpart(s) in the other repo:

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
  working-copy change ID, stable until `.claude` is
  committed at push time.
- **The `.claude` commit** (one per push) carries one
  `ochid: /<code-chid>` per code commit in that push.
  Single-line when the push has one code commit;
  multi-line when several (see
  [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md)).

Use `vc-x1 chid -s code,bot -L` to capture the change
IDs (first line app, second `.claude`).
```

Same line count (~17), but:
- **Intro**: just defines what ochid trailers are; no claim about asymmetry.
- **`.claude` bullet**: explicitly says "(one per push)" — that's the only true asymmetry (one `.claude` commit per push regardless of code-commit count); the trailer-count behavior follows from there. Single-line vs multi-line described by the push's code-commit count, not attributed to a specific shape (avoids being wrong about Squash etc.).

Apply this revision?

message.content: Did some tweaks

attachment.snippet:
1	# Cycle protocol
2	
3	## Cycles
4	
5	A cycle has three phases:
6	
7	- **[Preparation](#preparation)** (`X.Y.Z-0`) — the cycle's
8	  first commit. Sets up the cycle:
9	  - Backfill the previous cycle's chores `Commits:` ref.
10	  - Bump `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
11	  - Pick up a `## Todo` item (typically via `## Priorities`)
12	    into `## In Progress` (bold title + succinct problem
13	    statement + plan ladder).
14	  - Open the [chores section](#chores-sections).
15	- **[Work-N](#work-n)** (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) — the
16	  commits that implement the change. As many as the change
17	  needs; each runs through the
18	  [per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow).
19	- **[Close-out](#close-out)** (bare `X.Y.Z`) — the cycle's
20	  last commit. Bookkeeping only:
21	  - Move the picked-up item to a one-line entry in
22	    `## Done`.
23	  - Move the `## In Progress` block into the
24	    [chores section](#chores-sections).
25	  - Optionally update `notes/README.md` if functionality
26	    changed.
27	
28	A cycle's commits are published to the project remote
29	either incrementally or as one batch at close-out; the
30	result must always be published at close-out. See
31	[Pushing](#pushing).
32	
33	**Sub-cycles.** When a Work commit's scope grows enough to
34	warrant its own ladder, it subdivides — `X.Y.Z-3.0`
35	Preparation, `X.Y.Z-3.1` / `X.Y.Z-3.2` Work, `X.Y.Z-3`
36	Close-out. The same three-phase shape applies recursively
37	at every depth. See [Numbering](#numbering) for the
38	suffix rule and [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)
39	for the local-ladder mechanics.
40	
41	## Chores sections
42	
43	A **chores section** is a `##` section in
44	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md` recording one cycle:
45	
46	- One section per cycle is the default.
47	- Per-commit sections (like 0.55.0's four) are a
48	  deliberate alternative.
49	
50	The phrase **"Open" the chores section** means append a
51	`##` header to the current `notes/chores/chores-NN.md`
52	with the cycle's anticipated close-out title (e.g.
53	`## refactor: foo bar (X.Y.Z)`). The body is empty;
54	content arrives at close-out (see [Close-out](#close-out)).
55	
56	Fuller chores conventions (content rules, header sync,
57	design subsection pattern, `Commits:` formatting) live
58	in CLAUDE.md
59	[Pre-commit Requirements](../CLAUDE.md#pre-commit-requirements).
60	
61	## Preparation
62	
63	The cycle's first commit (`X.Y.Z-0`):
64	
65	- **Backfill the previous cycle's chores section
66	  `Commits:` ref** — see
67	  [Chores commit references](../CLAUDE.md#chores-commit-references).
68	- **Bump the version** in `Cargo.toml` to `X.Y.Z-0`.
69	- **Pick up a `## Todo` item** (if the cycle has one).
70	  Move into `## In Progress` as:
71	  - A **bold title line** — exact match of the chores
72	    section header, minus the `## ` prefix.
73	  - A **succinct problem statement**.
74	  - A **plan ladder**.
75	
76	  See [Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form). A Todo that
77	  duplicates current `## In Progress` work is a process
78	  bug.
79	- **Open the [chores section](#chores-sections)** —
80	  append a `##` header with the cycle's anticipated
81	  close-out title.
82	
83	## Work-N
84	
85	The cycle's work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …)
86	implement the change. As many as needed:
87	
88	- Each commit runs through the
89	  **[per-commit flow](#per-commit-flow)**.
90	- **Interim pushes** are optional (backup, progress
91	  visibility).
92	- Close-out is the only mandatory push (see
93	  [Pushing](#pushing)).
94	- **Subdivide into a sub-cycle** if a Work commit's
95	  scope grows enough (see
96	  [Sub-cycle ladders](#sub-cycle-ladders)).
97	
98	## Close-out
99	
100	The cycle's last commit (bare `X.Y.Z`) does bookkeeping
101	only — the commit body describes that bookkeeping, not
102	what happens post-squash:
103	
104	- **Move the picked-up item** from `## In Progress` to a
105	  one-line entry (with a chores `[N]` ref) in `## Done`.
106	- **Move the `## In Progress` block into the
107	  [chores section](#chores-sections)** (the title-only
108	  placeholder opened during Preparation):
109	  - Cut the problem statement + plan ladder out of
110	    `## In Progress` (drop the bold title line — the
111	    chores `##` header already carries that string).
112	  - Paste under the chores `##` header.
113	  - Sync the chores header to the **final** commit title
114	    if the cycle's scope shifted; update every anchor
115	    back-reference.
116	  - Add an `### As-built ladder` listing the cycle's
117	    commits; optional `### Outcome` notes.
118	  - Replace `## In Progress` with
119	    `_No cycle currently in progress._`.
120	- **Update `notes/README.md`** if functionality changed
121	  (new flags, new subcommands, changed behavior).
122	
123	Whether to **squash** the cycle into one commit before the
124	publishing push, or push as-is, is decided at push time —
125	see [Pushing](#pushing).
126	
127	## Numbering
128	
129	The version suffix on each commit encodes its phase —
130	the **final identifier `0` marks a Preparation**:
131	
132	- `X.Y.Z-0` — Preparation
133	- `X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, … — Work commits
134	- `X.Y.Z` — Close-out (bare version, no suffix)
135	
136	Disambiguation:
137	
138	- `-10` — Work commit #10 (final identifier `10`), not a
139	  Preparation.
140	- `-1.0` — Preparation of the `-1` sub-cycle (final
141	  identifier `0`).
142	
143	**Nesting.** Sub-cycles append another level, recursively:
144	
145	- `X.Y.Z-3.0` — Preparation of the `-3` sub-cycle
146	- `X.Y.Z-3.1`, `X.Y.Z-3.2` — its Work
147	- `X.Y.Z-3` — its Close-out
148	- `X.Y.Z-3.1.0` — Preparation of the `-3.1` sub-sub-cycle
149	
150	Sub-cycles needing no Preparation omit `.0` (`-3.1`,
151	`-3.2`, `-3`); one that grows a Preparation later adds
152	`-3.0` without renumbering siblings.
153	
154	Bump `Cargo.toml` at the start of each phase so `vc-x1 -V`
155	reports the active phase at build time.
156	
157	## Per-commit flow
158	
159	Every commit (Preparation, each Work commit, Close-out) goes
160	through:
161	
162	1. **Mark this commit `(current)`** as the first edit in
163	   `notes/todo.md > ## In Progress`.
164	2. **Do the work** (see [Iterative work](#iterative-work)
165	   for the loop-and-squash technique).
166	3. **Flip this commit `(current)` → `(done)`** in `## In
167	   Progress` — before the cargo cycle and the commit.
168	4. **Cargo cycle** (skip-able for purely-docs commits; full
169	   cycle mandatory at close-out):
170	   1. `cargo fmt`
171	   2. `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`
172	   3. `cargo test`
173	   4. `cargo install --path . --locked`
174	   5. (re-test if anything substantive changed)
175	5. **Work review.** Stop *before* writing any description;
176	   tell the user "ready to commit." The user reviews the
177	   changes and we iterate until complete.
178	6. **Write the commit description** — see
179	   [Commit description](#commit-description).
180	7. **Commit Description review.** Show the title + body
181	   and stop. The user reviews the description. Iterate.
182	8. **Commit.** `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R .` for
183	   the app repo, `-R .claude` for the bot repo (`-R` last
184	   keeps the verb visible):
185	
186	   ```
187	   jj commit -m \
188	   "<type>: <short description> (<version>)" \
189	   -m "<intro paragraph>
190	
191	   - file1: gist
192	   - file2: gist
193	
194	   ochid: /.claude/<chid>" \
195	   -R .
196	   ```
197	
198	**Two overrides apply:**
199	
200	- **Deviation or question** — any time the work deviates
201	  from the agreed plan, or a question arises, stop and
202	  surface it; don't push through.
203	- **ESC-ESC** — the user can interrupt at any point to pull
204	  a review or question forward.
205	
206	## Commit description
207	
208	[Conventional Commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/)
209	with a version suffix:
210	
211	```
212	<type>: <short description> (<version>)
213	```
214	
215	### Title
216	
217	- ≤50 chars total (version suffix counts).
218	- Common types: `feat`, `fix`, `refactor`, `test`,
219	  `docs`, `chore`.
220	- Favor terse phrasings.
221	
222	### Body
223	
224	[Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form) (intro + bullets),

... [336 lines truncated] ...

content.text:
Reviewed. A few observations:

1. **"has one or more `ochid: /<code-chid>` per commit" is ambiguous** — "per commit" could mean "per `.claude` commit" (the implicit subject) or "per code commit" (the quantifier). Previous version said "per code commit in that push" which made it a clear quantifier (one ochid line per code commit pushed alongside). Suggest reverting to that phrasing or rewording to disambiguate.

2. **"ladder item" terminology** — that term is used in `## Sub-cycle ladders` for sub-cycle Work commits specifically. Using it here for "cycle commits in general" overloads the term. "Work commit" or "code commit" would be more precise.

3. **"Also see [`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) for issues to be addressed"** — let me verify what that file actually contains; "issues to be addressed" vs the original "designs" suggests different things:

content.content:
# Forking and multi-user coordination

Captures design discussion about two related but separable
concerns:

1. **Forking** a dual-repo workspace — working on an older
   code-side base while keeping the bot side append-only.
2. **Multi-user collaboration** on the same dual-repo —
   multiple humans, multiple bot sessions, code-side merges.

Both surface the same underlying tension: the code repo has
the classic git/jj DAG (branches, merges, rebases), while the
bot repo is fundamentally an append-only journal of
conversations that should never be rewritten.

For the *content* of the bot repo (vendor identity, format
versioning, multi-bot conventions, vendor-subdir directory

toolUseResult.stdout:
# Forking and multi-user coordination

Captures design discussion about two related but separable
concerns:

1. **Forking** a dual-repo workspace — working on an older
   code-side base while keeping the bot side append-only.
2. **Multi-user collaboration** on the same dual-repo —
   multiple humans, multiple bot sessions, code-side merges.

Both surface the same underlying tension: the code repo has
the classic git/jj DAG (branches, merges, rebases), while the
bot repo is fundamentally an append-only journal of
conversations that should never be rewritten.

For the *content* of the bot repo (vendor identity, format
versioning, multi-bot conventions, vendor-subdir directory
layout), see the companion file
[`bot-data-formats.md`](bot-data-formats.md).

content.text:
Per `notes/forks-multi-user.md` line 3: "Captures design discussion about two related but separable concerns" — it's framed as design, not issues. So "issues to be addressed" misrepresents the file.

**Summary of three concerns:**

1. **"per commit" ambiguity** — suggest reverting to "per code commit in that push" (unambiguous quantifier).
2. **"ladder item"** — overloaded with `## Sub-cycle ladders` usage. "Work commit" or "code commit" is more precise here.
3. **"issues to be addressed"** — the file is design discussion, not issues. Suggest "design discussion" framing.

**Proposed revised version:**

```
Each commit body carries `ochid:` trailer(s) —
workspace-root-relative paths linking it to its
counterpart(s) in the other repo. See
[`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) for the
multi-line trailer design (multi-user / forking).

- **Code-side commits** each carry one
  `ochid: /.claude/<.claude-chid>` — the `.claude`
  working-copy change ID.
- **The `.claude` commit** has one `ochid: /<code-chid>`
  per code commit in that push. More than one occurs on
  Merge non-ff close-out (one ochid per Work commit in
  the cycle).
```

Three changes from your version:
- Intro link: "issues to be addressed" → "design (multi-user / forking)".
- `.claude` bullet: "has one or more ... per commit" → "has one ... per code commit in that push" (unambiguous quantifier; still captures the multi-line case via the "More than one occurs..." follow-up).
- "ladder item on the code repo" → "Work commit in the cycle".

Apply, or want different wording?

message.content: Remove "working-copy" a change ID is generally immutable.

content.text: Applying all the changes (my proposed three + your "working-copy" removal).

content.text:
Yes, both terms are used throughout without being defined. `jj-revsets.md` covers chid ("permanent and doesn't change"); ochid is project-specific (this file is the canonical def).

**My recommendation**: define both in the intro of `## ochid trailers` (the natural anchor — "ochid" derives from "chid"). One sentence each, with `jj-revsets.md` cross-link for chid. Other sections that use "chid" stay implicit since the file is small enough that a reader can find the definition.

Draft (~3 added lines to the existing intro):

```
## ochid trailers

A **chid** is jj's change ID — a permanent identifier
that survives rebases and describes (see
[`jj-revsets.md`](jj-revsets.md)). An **ochid** trailer
on a commit body links it to its counterpart in the
other repo via that counterpart's chid, written as a
workspace-root-relative path. See
[`forks-multi-user.md`](forks-multi-user.md) for the
multi-line trailer design (multi-user / forking).

- **Code-side commits** ...
```

**Tradeoff**: 3 lines longer but the terms become self-defined (helpful for README.md or external readers). Alternative: a `## Glossary` section at the top of the file (chid + ochid + other terms) — more discoverable but adds a section to the TOC.

Apply the inline def, or want the glossary approach?

## Iterative work

When work for a single commit benefits from incremental
review, loop:

1. `jj new -R .` — fresh empty `@` on top of the target
   commit.
2. Make the next round of changes.
3. User reviews the round (see
   [Reviewing changes](#reviewing-changes)).
4. `jj squash -R .` to fold into the target; jj
   auto-creates a new empty `@` for the next round.

Same jj mechanics as a
[sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at
single-commit scope — the cycle's version suffix
doesn't change.

content.text:
Squashed. `## Iterative work` is already quite tight (17 lines, current state above). Honest assessment: not much obvious bloat here.

A few micro-observations if you want any of them addressed:

1. **"target commit" not explicitly tied back** — the intro says "for a single commit" and step 1 says "on top of the target commit." A reader landing here cold might wonder which commit is "the target." Could clarify in step 1 as "on top of the commit you're iteratively editing." Cost: 4 more words.

2. **Step 4's clause** — `jj squash -R .` to fold into the target; jj auto-creates a new empty `@` for the next round." — the second clause is informational (the loop continuation behavior). Could tighten to "`jj squash -R .` folds into the target and creates a new empty `@`." Same info, less prose.

3. **Closing paragraph** — "Same jj mechanics as a [sub-cycle ladder](#sub-cycle-ladders), but at single-commit scope — the cycle's version suffix doesn't change." — useful contrast. No real cut available without losing meaning.

4. **The section's title** — "Iterative work" is good. Alternatives: "Iteration loop", "Incremental rounds". Current is fine.

If none of these feel worth touching, the section can stay as-is. Otherwise pick what to apply. Or skip ahead to the last big section (`## Sub-cycle ladders`, ~120 lines — the biggest remaining)?

420	## Sub-cycle ladders
421	
422	The local ladder of sub-cycle Work commits is typically
423	kept on its own jj `@` chain and **collapsed into the
424	sub-cycle's Close-out** before the parent cycle continues.
425	Ladder commits are scratch — they exist for review and
426	bisection during the sub-cycle and disappear into one
427	commit at sub-cycle close-out.
428	
429	### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder
430	
431	For each Work commit in the ladder:
432	
433	1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
434	2. Do the commit's work on this `@`.
435	3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable.** Build and
436	   clippy alone are not enough — past experience
437	   (0.41.1-6.5) had a regression introduced in an early
438	   ladder commit that didn't surface until a later one ran
439	   the full suite, by which point bisection cost was higher
440	   than it needed to be.
441	4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — give it a working
442	   title and body. The title does **not** need a version
443	   suffix during the ladder; the sub-cycle Close-out collects
444	   every commit into one final commit carrying the
445	   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker. Ladder titles are scratch — they
446	   never reach the remote.
447	
448	### Navigating the ladder
449	
450	Common moves while a ladder is active:
451	
452	- `jj log -r 'all()' -R .` — see the whole ladder at a
453	  glance.
454	- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any commit in
455	  the ladder by chid prefix. Useful for bisection: re-run
456	  the failing test at each candidate without manual
457	  checkout juggling.
458	- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
459	- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit's diff in
460	  isolation.
461	
462	Example using a revset with the `::` operator from the last
463	commit pushed to the remote:
464	
465	```
466	$ jj log -r lu::
467	○  pynknoqu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 33612c81
468	│  docs: cycle protocol pointer
469	@  xqpnxtyu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 f40c49a7
470	│  docs: cycle protocol formal protocol
471	○  lzqmlpvy wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 8c722f8c
472	│  docs: jj-revsets.md review fixes
473	○  wmmxkuoz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 11:28:46 65a6705f
474	│  chore: scratch — jj-revsets learning + scaffolding
475	◆  lulqxovr wink@saville.com 2026-04-27 22:27:23 main 55eadc8e
476	│  docs: capture 0.41.1 init+clone redesign plan (0.42.0-4)
477	~
478	```
479	
480	Ladder work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected.
481	Use `jj edit -r <chid>` to switch to whichever ladder
482	commit you want to work on. Modifications to a selected
483	commit rewrite that commit in place; descendants auto-rebase.
484	
485	### Close-out: squash the ladder
486	
487	When all ladder Work commits are done and `cargo test --bins`
488	is green on the latest:
489	
490	```
491	jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
492	```
493	
494	Where `<base>` is the parent of the **first** ladder
495	commit — typically the bookmark or commit the ladder
496	branched from. The `-u` flag (use destination message)
497	tells jj to keep `@`'s description and discard the source
498	descriptions; the intermediate titles served their purpose,
499	and `vc-x1 push` will overwrite `@`'s description anyway.
500	
501	After squash:
502	
503	- All ladder diffs are folded into `@`.
504	- The intermediate commits become empty and are
505	  abandoned automatically.
506	- History is linear: `<base> → @`.
507	
508	Then run `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` exactly as for any other
509	commit. Push's `commit-app` stage finalizes `@` with the
510	sub-cycle Close-out title/body and the proper `(X.Y.Z-N)`
511	version suffix.
512	
513	For N = 1 the close-out squash is a no-op (`base..@-` is
514	empty when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit
515	directly.
516	
517	### Recovery
518	
519	If a ladder commit goes wrong and you want to back out
520	without losing prior commits:
521	
522	- **Discard the current commit's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
523	  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
524	  parent. Earlier commits are untouched.
525	- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
526	  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
527	  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
528	- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
529	  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
530	  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
531	  ladder work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
532	  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
533	  above is.
534	
535	### Example
536	
537	A 3-commit ladder from `main`, then close-out squash:
538	
539	```
540	jj new -R .                   # repeat per commit:
541	... edit files; cargo test --bins ...
542	jj describe -m "..." -R .
543	
544	jj squash --from "main..@-" --into @ -u -R .
545	```
546	
547	Result: linear `main → @` with all ladder diffs folded
548	into `@`; intermediate commits abandoned. Then
549	`vc-x1 push main …` per normal.

file.content:
## Sub-cycle ladders

The local ladder of sub-cycle Work commits is typically
kept on its own jj `@` chain and **collapsed into the
sub-cycle's Close-out** before the parent cycle continues.
Ladder commits are scratch — they exist for review and
bisection during the sub-cycle and disappear into one
commit at sub-cycle close-out.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work on this `@`.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable.** Build and
   clippy alone are not enough — past experience
   (0.41.1-6.5) had a regression introduced in an early
   ladder commit that didn't surface until a later one ran
   the full suite, by which point bisection cost was higher
   than it needed to be.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — give it a working
   title and body. The title does **not** need a version
   suffix during the ladder; the sub-cycle Close-out collects
   every commit into one final commit carrying the
   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker. Ladder titles are scratch — they
   never reach the remote.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves while a ladder is active:

- `jj log -r 'all()' -R .` — see the whole ladder at a
  glance.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any commit in
  the ladder by chid prefix. Useful for bisection: re-run
  the failing test at each candidate without manual
  checkout juggling.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit's diff in
  isolation.

Example using a revset with the `::` operator from the last
commit pushed to the remote:

```
$ jj log -r lu::
○  pynknoqu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 33612c81
│  docs: cycle protocol pointer
@  xqpnxtyu wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 f40c49a7
│  docs: cycle protocol formal protocol
○  lzqmlpvy wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 16:16:23 8c722f8c
│  docs: jj-revsets.md review fixes
○  wmmxkuoz wink@saville.com 2026-05-01 11:28:46 65a6705f
│  chore: scratch — jj-revsets learning + scaffolding
◆  lulqxovr wink@saville.com 2026-04-27 22:27:23 main 55eadc8e
│  docs: capture 0.41.1 init+clone redesign plan (0.42.0-4)
~
```

Ladder work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected.
Use `jj edit -r <chid>` to switch to whichever ladder
commit you want to work on. Modifications to a selected
commit rewrite that commit in place; descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and `cargo test --bins`
is green on the latest:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Where `<base>` is the parent of the **first** ladder
commit — typically the bookmark or commit the ladder
branched from. The `-u` flag (use destination message)
tells jj to keep `@`'s description and discard the source
descriptions; the intermediate titles served their purpose,
and `vc-x1 push` will overwrite `@`'s description anyway.

After squash:

- All ladder diffs are folded into `@`.
- The intermediate commits become empty and are
  abandoned automatically.
- History is linear: `<base> → @`.

Then run `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` exactly as for any other
commit. Push's `commit-app` stage finalizes `@` with the
sub-cycle Close-out title/body and the proper `(X.Y.Z-N)`
version suffix.

For N = 1 the close-out squash is a no-op (`base..@-` is
empty when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit
directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong and you want to back out
without losing prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent. Earlier commits are untouched.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
  ladder work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
  above is.

### Example

A 3-commit ladder from `main`, then close-out squash:

```
jj new -R .                   # repeat per commit:
... edit files; cargo test --bins ...
jj describe -m "..." -R .

jj squash --from "main..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Result: linear `main → @` with all ladder diffs folded
into `@`; intermediate commits abandoned. Then
`vc-x1 push main …` per normal.

content.text:
`## Sub-cycle ladders` is ~130 lines — biggest remaining. Subsections: intro / per-Work-commit contract / navigating / close-out squash / recovery / example.

**Where I see bloat:**

1. **Intro (6 lines)** — two sentences saying overlapping things; can merge.
2. **`### Per-Work-commit contract > step 3`** — the "non-negotiable" rationale has a 4-line historical anchor (`0.41.1-6.5 ...`). Same kind of thing we trimmed elsewhere.
3. **`### Per-Work-commit contract > step 4`** — verbose explanation of working title vs final title.
4. **`### Navigating the ladder`** — has a **14-line concrete code-block example** showing actual jj log output with chids. Useful as visual reference but heavy; could become a single `jj log -r '<base>::'` line.
5. **`### Navigating the ladder` closing paragraph** — "Ladder work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected. Use `jj edit -r <chid>` to switch..." — repeats the bullet about `jj edit -r <prefix>`.
6. **`### Close-out: squash the ladder`** — `-u` flag explanation (3 lines) could be tighter; redundant "After squash:" bullet list (the points are in surrounding prose).
7. **`### Example` (14 lines)** — concrete 3-commit walkthrough. Overlaps with the contract + close-out sections combined. **This is the biggest single drop candidate** — ~14 lines for what's already documented step-by-step above.

**My recommendation: aggressive tightening, ~45-line savings (130 → 85).**

Specifically:
- (1)-(6): in-place tightening, ~25 line savings.
- (7): drop `### Example` entirely (-14 lines).

**Tradeoffs to flag (these were dropped earlier and you restored):**
- The 14-line code-block example in `### Navigating the ladder` (item 4) — visual reference for what a ladder looks like in `jj log`.
- The `### Example` section (item 7) — concrete walkthrough, was previously restored.

If you want to keep both examples for first-time-reader value, savings drops to ~25 lines (intro + step 3 + step 4 + navigating closing + close-out tightening).

Apply aggressive (-45) or conservative (-25)?

input.new_string:
## Sub-cycle ladders

When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
for review and bisection only.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable** — build
   and clippy alone miss regressions until a later
   commit runs the full suite, raising bisection cost.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
   only (no version suffix); the sub-cycle Close-out
   collects everything into one final commit with the
   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves:

- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
  from its base.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
  isolation.

Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.

For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.

```

Ladder work happens on whatever `@` is currently selected.
Use `jj edit -r <chid>` to switch to whichever ladder
commit you want to work on. Modifications to a selected
commit rewrite that commit in place; descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and `cargo test --bins`
is green on the latest:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Where `<base>` is the parent of the **first** ladder
commit — typically the bookmark or commit the ladder
branched from. The `-u` flag (use destination message)
tells jj to keep `@`'s description and discard the source
descriptions; the intermediate titles served their purpose,
and `vc-x1 push` will overwrite `@`'s description anyway.

After squash:

- All ladder diffs are folded into `@`.
- The intermediate commits become empty and are
  abandoned automatically.
- History is linear: `<base> → @`.

Then run `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` exactly as for any other
commit. Push's `commit-app` stage finalizes `@` with the
sub-cycle Close-out title/body and the proper `(X.Y.Z-N)`
version suffix.

For N = 1 the close-out squash is a no-op (`base..@-` is
empty when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit
directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong and you want to back out
without losing prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit's work.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it and gives you a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent. Earlier commits are untouched.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows the
  op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts the repo
  to that point. This is a full undo — it removes *all*
  ladder work after the chosen op. Use only when you want
  to start over. **Not** a close-out recipe; the squash
  above is.

### Example

A 3-commit ladder from `main`, then close-out squash:

```
jj new -R .                   # repeat per commit:
... edit files; cargo test --bins ...
jj describe -m "..." -R .

jj squash --from "main..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

Result: linear `main → @` with all ladder diffs folded
into `@`; intermediate commits abandoned. Then
`vc-x1 push main …` per normal.
toolUseResult.newString:
## Sub-cycle ladders

When a Work commit subdivides into a sub-cycle (see
[Numbering](#numbering) for suffix nesting), its Work
commits typically live as a local jj `@` chain and
**collapse into the sub-cycle's Close-out** before the
parent cycle continues. Ladder commits are scratch —
for review and bisection only.

### Per-Work-commit contract within a ladder

For each Work commit in the ladder:

1. `jj new -R .` — create a fresh empty `@`.
2. Do the commit's work.
3. Run `cargo test --bins`. **Non-negotiable** — build
   and clippy alone miss regressions until a later
   commit runs the full suite, raising bisection cost.
4. `jj describe -m "..." -m "..." -R .` — working title
   only (no version suffix); the sub-cycle Close-out
   collects everything into one final commit with the
   `(X.Y.Z-N)` marker.

### Navigating the ladder

Common moves:

- `jj log -r '<base>::' -R .` — see the whole ladder
  from its base.
- `jj edit -r <prefix> -R .` — jump `@` to any ladder
  commit by chid prefix; useful for bisection.
- `jj edit @-- -R .` — quick-jump back two commits.
- `jj diff -r <chid> -R .` — review one commit in
  isolation.

Modifications to any ladder commit rewrite it in place;
descendants auto-rebase.

### Close-out: squash the ladder

When all ladder Work commits are done and tests pass:

```
jj squash --from "<base>..@-" --into @ -u -R .
```

`<base>` is the parent of the first ladder commit; `-u`
keeps `@`'s description and discards the sources'.
After squash, history is linear: `<base> → @`;
intermediate commits are auto-abandoned.

Then `vc-x1 push <bookmark>` as for any other commit.

For N = 1 the squash is a no-op (`<base>..@-` is empty
when `@-` is `<base>`); push the single commit directly.

### Recovery

If a ladder commit goes wrong, back out without losing
prior commits:

- **Discard the current commit.** `jj abandon @ -R .`
  drops it; you get a fresh empty `@` on the same
  parent.
- **Edit an earlier commit.** `jj edit -r <chid> -R .`,
  make corrections, then `jj edit -r <last-ladder-chid>`
  to return. Descendants auto-rebase.
- **Discard the entire ladder.** `jj op log -R .` shows
  the op history; `jj op restore <op-id> -R .` reverts
  to that point. Full undo — removes *all* ladder work
  after the chosen op. Use only to start over.

# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**docs: cycle protocol updates (0.59.0)**

Cycle protocol moves out of CLAUDE.md into its own file
(`notes/cycle-protocol.md`) as the single source of truth,
replacing the aged `notes/substep-protocol.md`. A new
`## Ideas` section in `notes/todo.md` collects items that
aren't solid enough for `## Todo` (or surface during
close-out / end-of-day), with Preparation triaging them on
the next cycle. The three 0.58.0 follow-ups (merge default,
close-out follow-up capture, meta-note) land in the new
file, with capture targeting `## Ideas`. The cycle also
codifies the **ochid invariant** — every public ochid must
resolve in the public graph — and the bot-side rules that
make it feasible (never squash; descriptions editable so
bot→code trailers can be updated without changing bot
chids). The invariant gates squash as a close-out shape:
merge is the default and currently the only invariant-safe
shape, since squash requires symmetric-squash tooling
(`vc-x1 push --squash`) that doesn't yet exist.

- 0.59.0-0 Preparation (done)
  - backfill `0.58.0` `Commits:` ref in chores-12
  - bump Cargo.toml to `0.59.0-0`
  - open chores-12 next section (anticipated close-out title)
  - delete stray `close-out.txt` (swept into 0.58.0-0)
  - capture 0.58.0 follow-up Todo (`vc-x1 push` N:1 support)
    in `notes/todo.md > ## Todo` per Option C (retroactive)
  - refresh `## Priorities`:
    - drop satisfied P1 (chores-12 active)
    - promote `vc-x1 push` N:1 to new P1
  - populate `## In Progress` (this block)
- 0.59.0-0.1 capture design notes for `-1` as `###` subsections
  in chores-12 (end-of-day handoff before `/exit`) (done;
  superseded by the ochid invariant updates applied in `-2`)
- 0.59.0-1 Relocate (done)
  - create `notes/cycle-protocol.md` by verbatim extract of
    CLAUDE.md `## Cycle Protocol` (demote `###` → `##`)
  - fold `notes/substep-protocol.md` recipes (sub-cycle
    close-out squash, recovery moves, worked example,
    substep-test.sh reference) into the new file
  - delete `notes/substep-protocol.md`
  - replace CLAUDE.md `## Cycle Protocol` with pointer +
    tiny essentials (phases, numbering one-liner, link)
  - update active cross-ref in
    `notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md`; leave chores
    historical refs as-is
  - bump Cargo.toml to `0.59.0-1`
- 0.59.0-2 Refine
  - add `## Ideas` to `notes/todo.md` between In Progress
    and Priorities
  - update `cycle-protocol.md` for the three 0.58.0
    follow-ups, Ideas-aware:
    - Preparation triages `## Ideas` (promote / fold /
      drop) before declaring the plan
    - Close-out captures unprocessed follow-ups into
      `## Ideas`
    - Merge is the default shape for multi-commit cycles
    - Meta-note that close-out often surfaces follow-ups
  - codify the **ochid invariant** in cycle-protocol.md:
    every public ochid must resolve in the public graph
  - codify bot-repo rules in cycle-protocol.md: bot repos
    are never squashed; their commit descriptions are
    editable (chid stable through `jj describe`) so
    bot→code ochid trailers can be updated when code-side
    chids change
  - codify squash gating in cycle-protocol.md: until
    symmetric-squash tooling exists, merge is the only
    permitted close-out shape for multi-commit cycles
  - sketch cross-repo migration in cycle-protocol.md (or
    a sibling notes file): ochids change at every merge
    until the change reaches the canonical repo's `main`,
    where they become permanent — the merge tool's job
    each hop
  - update chores-12 design subsections to match: revise
    `### Squash carve-out`; revise `### Close-out
    follow-up capture` (target = `## Ideas`); add
    `### Ochid invariant`; add `### Cross-repo migration
    sketch`
  - capture symmetric-squash tooling Todo (likely P1, as
    it gates the squash shape) — `vc-x1 push --squash`
    that performs symmetric squash on both repos +

content.text:
**0.59.0 ladder status:**

**Done:**
- `0.59.0-0` Preparation (committed + pushed to `feat/0.59.0`).
- `0.59.0-0.1` design notes (committed + pushed; now stale, superseded by `-2`).
- `0.59.0-1` **Relocate** — all working-copy content complete in `@-` (`knvppzwq`). Cargo cycle was run earlier. Just needs commit description + the commit itself (per-commit flow steps 6-8).

**Remaining:**

- **`0.59.0-1` finish** (commit description + commit): write the description for the accumulated `-1` work (now significantly broader than original "verbatim relocate" — added all the in-place tightening, ochid trailers chid/ochid definition, restorations, etc.). Worth noting the scope grew well beyond the original plan; that drift will show up at close-out when In Progress moves into the chores section.

- **`0.59.0-2` Refine** (substantial commit, per the In Progress plan):
  - Add `## Ideas` to `notes/todo.md`.
  - Update `cycle-protocol.md` with the three 0.58.0 follow-ups (Ideas-aware).
  - Codify the **ochid invariant** in cycle-protocol.md.
  - Codify **bot-repo rules** (never squash; descriptions editable).
  - Codify **squash gating** (merge is the only invariant-safe shape until tooling lands).
  - Sketch **cross-repo migration** (ochids change at every merge until canonical main).
  - Update chores-12 design subsections to match (revise `### Squash carve-out`; revise `### Close-out follow-up capture` → Ideas target; add `### Ochid invariant`; add `### Cross-repo migration sketch`).
  - Capture `## Ideas` entry for relocating CLAUDE.md `## Pre-commit Requirements` subsections into cycle-protocol.md.
  - Capture symmetric-squash tooling `## Todo` entry (likely P1).

- **`0.59.0` close-out** via **merge non-ff** (chosen as the invariant-safe shape since symmetric-squash tooling doesn't exist).

So practically: ready to commit `-1`, then do `-2`, then close-out.

content.content:
Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

  -R, --repo <PATH>
          Workspace root or single jj repo path [default: .]

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

  -s, --scope <code|bot|code,bot>
          Side(s) to query; composes with --repo as workspace root

  -n, --commits <COMMITS>
          Number of commits to show

  -l, --label <TEXT>
          Custom label decoration between repos
          
          [default: ===]

  -L, --no-label
          Suppress label between repos

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

content.text:
Drafting `-1` commit description per the protocol's `## Commit description` format.

**Title** (49 chars):

```
docs: extract cycle protocol (0.59.0-1)
```

**Body** (file-by-file):

```
Extract `## Cycle Protocol` from CLAUDE.md into
`notes/cycle-protocol.md` — restructured with phase-first
overview, `###` subsections, and chid/ochid definitions.
Folds in the retired `notes/substep-protocol.md` recipes
as `## Sub-cycle ladders`. CLAUDE.md keeps a 10-line
pointer. Two new backlog entries captured (per-repo
bookmark names for `vc-x1 push`; trapezoidal-merge
diagram for README.md).

- CLAUDE.md
  -- `## Cycle Protocol` (~390 lines) → 10-line pointer
  -- 3 broken anchor refs updated to cross-file refs
- notes/cycle-protocol.md: new (504 lines). Phase-first
  structure: `## Cycles` overview → `## Preparation` /
  `## Work-N` / `## Close-out` → shared mechanics →
  `## ochid trailers` (chid/ochid defined) →
  `## Iterative work` + `## Sub-cycle ladders`.
- notes/substep-protocol.md: deleted (215 lines; recipes
  folded into `cycle-protocol.md > ## Sub-cycle ladders`).
- notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md: cross-ref updated.
- notes/todo.md: `## In Progress` reflects broadened
  0.59.0 plan (relocate + refine + close-out via merge).
- notes/todo-backlog.md: 2 new entries at top; 42 existing
  renumbered via `vc-x1 fix-todo`.
- Cargo.toml: `0.59.0-0.1` → `0.59.0-1`.
- Cargo.lock: auto-sync.

ochid: /.claude/qkznvqkzusqlkvxuorykvtytrrrpkrqs
```

**Commit operation**: since the content is in `@-` (not `@` — we iterated via `jj new` + `jj squash` rounds and `@` is now an empty commit), the right command is `jj describe -r @-` rather than `jj commit`:

```
jj describe -r @- -m "<title>" -m "<body>" -R .
```

This sets `@-`'s description without creating a new commit (the empty `@` above already exists from prior squashes). No push — `-1` stays local; we'll push later (interim or at close-out).

Approve the title + body + the describe approach? Or want changes?

content.text:
Agreed it's mechanical and bounded — good candidate to do now. My lean: **split into two top-level Work commits** rather than bundling into one bigger `-2` or sub-cycling:

- **`-2` ChoresRelocation** (this becomes the relocation work — the Cargo.toml bump we already did is fine; it stays as `0.59.0-2`).
- **`-3` Refine** (what was originally planned for `-2`: Ideas-aware updates, ochid invariant codification, bot-repo rules, squash gating, cross-repo sketch, chores-12 design updates, symmetric-squash Todo).
- **`0.59.0` close-out**.

**Why split rather than bundle**:
- Focused commit scope — relocation is one logical change (atomic doc move); Refine is several intertwined codifications. Bundling them into one `-2` makes the commit body sprawl.
- Reviewable separately — relocation is easy to verify (grep that subsections moved + cross-refs work). Refine needs more thinking.
- Plan ladder cleaner than sub-cycling — `-2.0`/`-2.1`/`-2.2`/`-2` overstructures for two related Work commits.

**Why split rather than defer (keep as Idea)**:
- The work IS solid enough to be a Todo, as your sub-bullets demonstrated.
- Doing it now while the cycle protocol structure is fresh in mind means cycle-protocol.md ends up cleaner for the subsequent Refine work in `-3`.

**Mechanical impact** if you agree:
- Update In Progress: rename current `-2 Refine (current)` → `-2 ChoresRelocation (current)` with the sub-bullets from the Idea. Add `-3 Refine` with the prior `-2` plan items.
- Remove the Idea from `## Ideas` (it's now being done, not pending).
- Do the relocation work.

Go with the split? Or prefer bundle / sub-cycle?

message.content: Hmm, on the other hand, I now vote to close this out 0.59.0 and squash it, thoughts

content.text:
Honest reconsideration — there's a real case for it:

**For squashing now:**
- `-1` already captures the cycle's bulk value (relocate + extensive tightening — most of what 0.59.0 was for).
- The `-2` codifications (ochid invariant, squash gating, bot-repo rules) are rules-for-the-future; they bind work that doesn't exist yet (symmetric-squash tooling). Deferring them to a future cycle has low cost since nothing currently depends on them.
- The chores relocation Idea is bounded but isn't blocking anything either.

**The catch (we discussed this earlier):**

`.claude/main`'s `af60f979` (the `0.59.0-0.1` push) has TWO ochid trailers pointing at app-side `vxrtvvrs` (0.59.0-0) and `yqzowtqv` (0.59.0-0.1). Squashing 0.59.0 makes those app chids unreachable from `main`. The cleanup options are still:

- **K. Keep `feat/0.59.0` on origin after the squash** — the pre-squash app chids stay reachable; trailers continue to resolve. Cost: a permanent feature branch on origin "abandoned relative to main." **My recommended choice** — cheapest and matches the comment already in `af60f979`'s body ("pushed app side to a feature branch to preserve squash candidacy for close-out").
- **D. Delete `feat/0.59.0`** — clean remote; trailers go dangling (resolve only locally via jj op log until GC).
- **F. Force-push `.claude/main` to rewrite `af60f979`** — replace the two trailers with one pointing at the squashed chid. Clean trailer state, but force-push and loses the per-commit granularity that the existing trailers preserve.

**Net thought**: yes, squash + Option K is reasonable. We end up with:
- `main` gets one clean `0.59.0` commit (the squash).
- `feat/0.59.0` lingers as the "long-way" record (preserves ochid resolution).
- Future cycles handle the codifications.
- The Ideas section + the chores-relocation Idea entry get carried into the squashed commit (so the Idea stays queued for a future cycle).

If you want to go with squash, **which cleanup — K, D, or F?** And do you want to drop the `-2 Refine` plan ladder entry from In Progress now (since it's becoming the close-out instead of a separate Work commit)?

lastPrompt: Hmm, on the other hand, I now vote to close this out 0.59.0 and squash it, thoughts

message.content: I think we should force-push the bot repo description and update the chid, this is something we need to handle, would you agree?

content.text:
Agreed. Option F is the right thing — it respects the ochid invariant (even though we haven't codified it in cycle-protocol.md yet, doing F now sets the precedent for "this is how squash works properly"). The manual version IS what `vc-x1 push --squash` would automate later.

**Concrete steps to execute F:**

1. **Re-bump Cargo.toml** `0.59.0-2` → `0.59.0` (close-out version).
2. **Rewrite the In Progress block** to reflect the actual cycle (drop the `-2 Refine` plan items since `-2` isn't happening; just `-0`, `-0.1`, `-1`, `0.59.0` close-out).
3. **Capture the deferred work** (`-2`'s planned items) as Ideas / Todos so they don't get lost:
   - Ochid invariant codification + bot-repo rules + squash gating → `## Ideas` (or `## Todo` if you want them prioritized).
   - Symmetric-squash tooling → `## Todo` / `## Priorities` P1.
   - Ideas-aware Preparation/Close-out bullets → smaller `## Ideas` entry.
   - chores-12 design subsection updates → ?(part of next cycle's work either way).
4. **Apply close-out bookkeeping**: move picked-up item to `## Done`, move `## In Progress` block into chores-12 section, update `notes/README.md` if applicable.
5. **Commit close-out** as bare `0.59.0`.
6. **Squash cycle** (`-0`, `-0.1`, `-1`, `0.59.0`) into one commit on `main`. Capture the squashed commit's chid.
7. **Bot-side rewrite**: `jj describe -r af60f979` (or its chid `otywtnxx`) — replace the two existing `ochid:` trailers with one `ochid: /<squashed-chid>`. Verify `af60f979`'s chid (`otywtnxx`) stays stable through `jj describe`.
8. **Force-push `.claude/main`** (rewrites the published `af60f979` commit ID; the chid is preserved).
9. **Push app `main`** with the squashed commit.
10. **Then** finalize `.claude` for THIS push (the current session work since `af60f979` becomes a new bot commit, paired with the squashed app commit via `ochid: /<squashed-chid>`).

**Two things to flag:**

- **Force-pushing `.claude/main`** rewrites a public history — exactly the operation we want to teach the tool to do, but doing it by hand is a remote rewrite that warrants an explicit "are you sure" beat.
- **Setting precedent without codification**: we're doing F before the cycle-protocol.md says "do F when squashing." Future readers reconstructing the rule from this cycle's chores-12 narrative will see the precedent. That's fine but worth noting in the close-out chores section.

Confirm we should proceed with the 10-step plan above? Or want to discuss any step first?

message.content: One thing, we're going to end up with two bot commits pointing to one code commit, I don't have a problem with that but want to make sure you agree.

content.text:
Agreed — 2:1 bot→code is fine. Reasoning:

- **Semantically accurate**: both bot commits' session data DID contribute to the squashed code commit. `af60f979`'s session led to `-0`/`-0.1`; the new bot commit's session led to `-1` + close-out work. All collapsed into one code commit. So both bot commits honestly describe their session as contributing to that code commit.
- **Already in the protocol's design space**: the existing N:1 bot→code case (one bot commit, multiple ochid trailers for multiple code commits in one push) is the inverse asymmetry. Post-F, we have many bot commits → one code commit. Both are natural consequences of an append-only bot side paired with a code side that can squash.
- **Code→bot stays 1:1**: the squashed code commit's `ochid:` points at the new close-out bot commit (its actual push pair). The fact that `af60f979` ALSO claims association from the bot side doesn't break the code-side rule (each code commit carries one `ochid:` linking to ONE bot commit). The bot side's append-only journal carries the richer history.

**One suggestion worth flagging**: when rewriting `af60f979`'s description, consider including a brief note in the body acknowledging the rewrite — e.g., "Trailer rewritten 2026-05-25 when 0.59.0 squashed; originally paired with 0.59.0-0 and 0.59.0-0.1 (now collapsed)." That preserves the historical truth for future readers without losing the new (valid) ochid pointer.

Without that note, a reader sees `af60f979` (titled "chore: WIP session capture (0.59.0-0.1)") pointing at a `0.59.0` squashed commit, which feels like a versioning mismatch. The note explains.

Add the rewrite note, or keep `af60f979`'s rewrite minimal (just the trailer change)?

file.content:
# Chores-12

Continuation of `chores-11.md` (which is closed at `0.57.0` —
the `--merge` todo entry cycle). This file covers the `0.58.0`
cycle onward. Reference numbering is file-local — see
[`README.md`](../README.md#reference-numbering); chores-12
starts at `[1]`.

## refactor: notes/todo restructure (0.58.0)

Commits: [[1]]

`notes/todo.md` is too large for routine reads and its
`## Todo` intro duplicates `notes/README.md`.

### As-built ladder

- 0.58.0-0 Preparation
  - backfill `0.57.0` `Commits:` ref in chores-11
  - bump Cargo.toml to `0.58.0-0`
  - open chores-12 with bare section header
  - add minimal `## File reads` to CLAUDE.md
  - populate `## In Progress`
- 0.58.0-1 add `## Priorities` + trim `## Todo` intro
- 0.58.0-2 move `## Bugs` → `notes/bugs.md`; pointer in todo.md
- 0.58.0-3 extract `notes/todo-backlog.md` (prioritized entries
  stay in todo.md; references re-packed per file)
- 0.58.0-4 expand CLAUDE.md `## File reads` to bugs.md / chores
- 0.58.0-5 codify in CLAUDE.md: chores title-only during cycle,
  In Progress moves into chores at close-out, problem+plan
  shape for In Progress / chores intros / Todo entries
- 0.58.0 close-out: move In Progress block into chores-12;
  `## Done` entry; update `notes/README.md`

### Outcome

Cycle grew from 3 planned commits (intro trim + Priorities +
bugs split) to 6 as the work surfaced two related concerns:
the bot's routine read cost (handled by extracting
`todo-backlog.md` and the CLAUDE.md `## File reads` rule),
and the chores/In-Progress duplication during cycles (handled
by codifying the new "chores title-only during cycle, move at
close-out" mechanic and the problem+plan shape).

`notes/todo.md` is now sized for cheap routine reads — the
head limit=60 covers intro + `## In Progress` + `## Priorities`,
the live surfaces. The split infrastructure (`notes/bugs.md`,
`notes/todo-backlog.md`, re-packed per-file refs) makes
future similar splits cheap.

## docs: cycle protocol updates (0.59.0)

### Squash carve-out (for CLAUDE.md `### Pushing`)

Default = merge for any multi-commit cycle. Squash = explicit
opt-in for cycles whose sub-steps are intermediate validation
points rather than distinct landings. Every cycle is ≥3
commits (Preparation + ≥1 Work + Close-out), so the
"merge of one parent" edge case is moot.

### Close-out follow-up capture (for CLAUDE.md `### Close-out`)

At close-out, write any follow-up Todos surfaced during the
cycle to `notes/todo.md > ## Todo` (if prioritized) or
`notes/todo-backlog.md` (if long-tail). List them in the
close-out commit body so the additions are reviewable.

### Close-out surfaces follow-ups (meta-note, for `## Cycle Protocol`)

Close-out often surfaces follow-up items that became visible
only by doing the cycle's work. Capture them at close-out
(per the rule above) so they survive `/exit` between cycles
— sessions don't carry conversational context across `/exit`.

# References

[1]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/a199d062ff6e "a199d062ff6e88b5e2d87d57551d1c60e75b073b"

file.content:
## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

**docs: cycle protocol updates (0.59.0)**

Cycle protocol moves out of CLAUDE.md into its own file
(`notes/cycle-protocol.md`) as the single source of truth,
replacing the aged `notes/substep-protocol.md`. A new
`## Ideas` section in `notes/todo.md` collects items that
aren't solid enough for `## Todo` (or surface during
close-out / end-of-day), with Preparation triaging them on
the next cycle. The three 0.58.0 follow-ups (merge default,
close-out follow-up capture, meta-note) land in the new
file, with capture targeting `## Ideas`. The cycle also
codifies the **ochid invariant** — every public ochid must
resolve in the public graph — and the bot-side rules that
make it feasible (never squash; descriptions editable so
bot→code trailers can be updated without changing bot
chids). The invariant gates squash as a close-out shape:
merge is the default and currently the only invariant-safe
shape, since squash requires symmetric-squash tooling
(`vc-x1 push --squash`) that doesn't yet exist.

- 0.59.0-0 Preparation (done)
  - backfill `0.58.0` `Commits:` ref in chores-12
  - bump Cargo.toml to `0.59.0-0`
  - open chores-12 next section (anticipated close-out title)
  - delete stray `close-out.txt` (swept into 0.58.0-0)
  - capture 0.58.0 follow-up Todo (`vc-x1 push` N:1 support)
    in `notes/todo.md > ## Todo` per Option C (retroactive)
  - refresh `## Priorities`:
    - drop satisfied P1 (chores-12 active)
    - promote `vc-x1 push` N:1 to new P1
  - populate `## In Progress` (this block)
- 0.59.0-0.1 capture design notes for `-1` as `###` subsections
  in chores-12 (end-of-day handoff before `/exit`) (done;
  superseded by the ochid invariant updates applied in `-2`)
- 0.59.0-1 Relocate (done)
  - create `notes/cycle-protocol.md` by verbatim extract of
    CLAUDE.md `## Cycle Protocol` (demote `###` → `##`)
  - fold `notes/substep-protocol.md` recipes (sub-cycle
    close-out squash, recovery moves, worked example,
    substep-test.sh reference) into the new file
  - delete `notes/substep-protocol.md`
  - replace CLAUDE.md `## Cycle Protocol` with pointer +
    tiny essentials (phases, numbering one-liner, link)
  - update active cross-ref in
    `notes/init-clone-refactor-conflict.md`; leave chores
    historical refs as-is
  - bump Cargo.toml to `0.59.0-1`
- 0.59.0-2 Refine (current)
  - add `## Ideas` to `notes/todo.md` between In Progress
    and Priorities
  - update `cycle-protocol.md` for the three 0.58.0
    follow-ups, Ideas-aware:
    - Preparation triages `## Ideas` (promote / fold /
      drop) before declaring the plan
    - Close-out captures unprocessed follow-ups into
      `## Ideas`
    - Merge is the default shape for multi-commit cycles
    - Meta-note that close-out often surfaces follow-ups
  - codify the **ochid invariant** in cycle-protocol.md:
    every public ochid must resolve in the public graph
  - codify bot-repo rules in cycle-protocol.md: bot repos
    are never squashed; their commit descriptions are
    editable (chid stable through `jj describe`) so
    bot→code ochid trailers can be updated when code-side
    chids change
  - codify squash gating in cycle-protocol.md: until
    symmetric-squash tooling exists, merge is the only
    permitted close-out shape for multi-commit cycles
  - sketch cross-repo migration in cycle-protocol.md (or
    a sibling notes file): ochids change at every merge
    until the change reaches the canonical repo's `main`,
    where they become permanent — the merge tool's job
    each hop
  - update chores-12 design subsections to match: revise
    `### Squash carve-out`; revise `### Close-out
    follow-up capture` (target = `## Ideas`); add
    `### Ochid invariant`; add `### Cross-repo migration
    sketch`
  - capture symmetric-squash tooling Todo (likely P1, as
    it gates the squash shape) — `vc-x1 push --squash`
    that performs symmetric squash on both repos +
    rewrites the two surviving commits' ochid trailers
    to reference each other + force-pushes both sides
  - capture `## Ideas` entry: relocate the three
    chores-related subsections from CLAUDE.md
    `## Pre-commit Requirements` (`### Headings and
    entries that record a commit`, `### Chores section
    content`, `### Chores commit references`) into
    cycle-protocol.md, leaving CLAUDE.md with only the
    general writing conventions (`### Notes references`,
    `### Markdown anchor links`). Makes cycle-protocol.md
    fully self-contained on the cycle/chores workflow for
    README.md and other consumers.
- 0.59.0 close-out via **merge** (the only invariant-safe
  shape until symmetric-squash tooling lands)

## Ideas

 Items not yet solid enough for `## Todo` (or surfaced
 during close-out / end-of-day before they're fully
 formed). Triaged at the next Preparation: promote to
 `## Todo` / `notes/todo-backlog.md`, fold into a
 picked-up cycle, or drop.

1. **Relocate CLAUDE.md chores conventions into
   `notes/cycle-protocol.md`.** Makes cycle-protocol.md
   fully self-contained on the cycle/chores workflow for
   README.md and other consumers.
   - Identify cross-refs in CLAUDE.md and elsewhere
     pointing at the three subsections being moved.
   - Move `### Headings and entries that record a
     commit` from CLAUDE.md `## Pre-commit Requirements`
     into cycle-protocol.md.
   - Move `### Chores section content` from CLAUDE.md
     into cycle-protocol.md.
   - Move `### Chores commit references` from CLAUDE.md
     into cycle-protocol.md.
   - Restructure CLAUDE.md `## Pre-commit Requirements`
     after the moves — it then holds only general
     writing conventions (`### Notes references`,
     `### Markdown anchor links`); consider renaming.
   - Update all cross-refs to point at the new
     locations.

## Priorities

- P1 is highest priority, same priority are grouped equally
- A line with `...` is a prefix-match against a `## Todo` title
  - resolves locally (in this file); should be unique
- Entries without a prefix-match are free-form
  - chores tasks, ad-hoc reminders.

### P1

- `**vc-x1 push: support...**`

### P2

- `**single-field...**`

### P3

- `**por -> dual...**`
- `**por/dual parity...**`

## Todo

 Prioritized entries (referenced by `## Priorities`) lower priority
 todo's are in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). In all cases
 we use the [Prose Form in CLAUDE.md](/CLAUDE.md#prose-form). When
